pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric geophysical survey of the Lower Tanana River, Big Delta and Circle in eastern Alaska.
abstract: This high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey was flown May 14 to August 4, 2022 in the Yukon-Tanana Uplands in eastern Alaska. The survey was jointly funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resource Initiative and the State of Alaska Division of Geologic and Geophysical Surveys (DGGS) and designed to meet the complimentary needs related to geologic mapping and mineral resource research. The effort focused on an extensive belt of Mesozoic and early Cenozoic alkaline igneous rocks in eastern Alaska with known carbonatite occurrences and the area covers where the Arctic Alaska Terrane and North American assemblages are juxtaposed. The survey included 97,386 line-km of magnetic and radiometric data collected from a fixed-wing aircraft along flight lines spaced at 400-m. Mean ground clearances were: Tanana, 188 m; Chena, 352 m; Healy, 295 m; Eisen, 430 m; North Pole, 180 m. The Eisen Infill block was flown with a line spacing of 200 m and a mean ground clearance of 422 m. The mineral systems of interest in the survey area are: Peralkaline igneous rocks, carbonatite, placer and paleoplacer, and magmatic hydrothermal [porphyry Cu-Mo-Au, intrusion related Au, epithermal Au, Sn-W skarn, Zu-Pb-Zn skarn], marine hydrothermal [VMS-Beshi, VMS-Kuroko], metamorphic and deformation-related [orogenic Au] systems, orogenic- and intrusion-related gold, tin-tungsten skarns/greisens/placers, Nb-REE carbonatites, and antimony-arsenic-gold quartz-vein systems. The potential critical mineral commodities investigated include: rare earth elements, Th, Re, As, Sn, Bi, In, PGE, Y, Nb, U, Zr, Hf, monazite, zircon, uranothorite, fluorapatite, and W with additional prospectively for non-critical Au, Cu, Mo, Sn, Pb, and Zn.

pdescrip: Ozarks, Ouachita, and Southern Lowlands Project and Earth MRI Project
abstract: The high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey was acquired in the fall of 2018 to the spring of 2019 and is located in northwestern Arkansas. The survey was jointly funded by the USGS National Cooperative Geologic Mapping Program and the Earth Mapping Resource Initiative and designed to meet the complimentary needs related to geologic mapping and mineral resource research.  The survey covers an irregular area 225 by-110 km over the transition between the Ouachita and Ozarks Plateau physiographic provinces.  Precambrian crystalline basement comprised of the ca 1.3 Ga and ca 1.4 Ga Granite Rhyolite terranes underlies the area and is covered by a Paleozoic sedimentary section ranging in age from Cambrian through Permian.  Upper Ordovician (Rawtheyan) sedimentary rocks are exposed in the eastern part of the survey area and host marine phosphate and Mn deposits. The survey includes 67,614-line km of magnetic and radiometric data that were collected from a fixed-wing aircraft flown at median flight height of 167 meters (m) above topography along N-S flight lines spaced at 200-m. Tie lines were flown in an E-W direction every 3000 m. The mineral deposit types of interest in the survey area are: 1) sedimentary marine phosphate deposits and 2) sedimentary marine Mn deposits. The potential critical mineral commodities investigated include: rare earth elements, PO, F, Sr, Sc, Mn, Co and Ni.

pdescrip: Iron Oxide-Copper-Cobalt-Gold-Rare Earth Element Deposits of Southeast Missouri-From the Ore Deposit Scale to a Global Deposit Model
abstract: The high-resolution magnetic gradiometry and radiometric survey was acquired in the fall of 2018 to the spring of 2019 and covers parts of southeast Missouri and western Illinois. The survey covers a 146 by-154 km area centered on the St. Francois Mountains terrane, which is part of the exposed southeast Missouri iron metallogenic province.  Mesoproterozoic volcanic rocks within the province are part of the Eastern Granite Rhyolite terrane and host several major iron-oxide apatite (IOA) deposits and one iron oxide-copper-gold (IOCG) deposit. Directly overlying the Precambrian basement are Paleozoic sedimentary cover rocks that host world-class Mississippi Valley Type (MVT) districts that include the Viburnum Trend and Old Lead Belt Pb-Zn-Ag.  Although not recovered, these ores are also enriched in Ge, Ga, and In. The survey includes 68,375-line km of magnetic gradiometry and radiometric data that were collected from a fixed-wing aircraft flown at a nominal flight height 117 meters (m) above topography along E-W flight lines spaced at a 300-m line spacing. Tie lines were flown in an N-S direction every 3000 m. The mineral deposit types of interest in the survey area are: 1) IOA-REE and IOCG deposits and 2) sedimentary-hosted Mississippi Valley Type Co, Pb, Zn, Cd, Ge, Ag, and barite. The potential critical mineral commodities investigated include: rare earth elements, Ge, Ga, In, Co, and barite with additional prospectivity for non-critical iron, lead, zinc, and copper.

pdescrip: Earth Mapping Resource Initiative Project: Ti, REE and Zr placer deposits in SC
abstract: This high-resolution Earth MRI magnetic and radiometric survey was flown March-July 2020 and covered parts of the South Carolina Coastal Plain and Piedmont adjacent to a 2019 magnetic and radiometric survey over Charleston, SC and the surrounding area. The survey targeted Atlantic Coastal Plain sediments that host deposits of critical mineral resources titanium, zirconium, and rare earth elements in heavy mineral sands. This 127 x 127 km survey was flown with a fixed-wing aircraft flown at a nominal flight height of 120 meters (m) above topography along NE-SW flight lines with 300-m spacing. Tie (control) lines are flown in an NW-SE direction every 3,000 m. The combined Fall Zone 2019 Charleston survey cover a "source to sink" transect across the Atlantic Coastal Plain starting from sediment sources in the hard rock of the metamorphic Piedmont and ending at the coastline of the Atlantic Ocean. The radiometric data from these surveys, which use gamma spectrometry to provide estimates of K, Th, and U within 1 meter of the surface, highlight areas rich in heavy mineral sand deposits such as flood plains of key rivers and ancient coastlines; these data will provide insight into processes leading to deposit formation and consideration of areas that might be prospective for critical minerals. The magnetic data will image basement rock buried beneath the Atlantic Coastal Plain sediments and assist with mapping ancient igneous activity, faults and deformation, and exotic terranes. The Earth MRI mineral system of interest is placer/heavy mineral sands deposits. The potential critical mineral commodities investigated are: rare earth elements (REE), titanium, and zirconium.

pdescrip: Multidisciplinary effort for Earthquake Hazards, Geologic Mapping and Mineral Resources applications
abstract: This high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey was flown May-July 2019 as a multidisciplinary collaborative effort between the USGS Earthquake Hazards, National Cooperative Geologic Mapping, and Mineral Resources Programs. This 134 km x 90 km survey was flown with a fixed-wing aircraft at a nominal flight height of 120 meters (m) above topography along NE-SW flight lines with 400-m spacing. Tie (control) lines are flown in an NW-SE direction every 4,000 m. The survey had multiple targets. The region marks the site of the 1886 ~M7 earthquake that did heavy damage to the city of Charleston, SC, but for which the causative fault remains unknown. These surveys will assist mapping of buried faults, some which may be seismogenic, and thus provide key information to estimates of damage risk in this intraplate seismic zone. The region is also home to the South Georgia rift basin, which is one of the largest basins to have formed during opening of the Atlantic Ocean but is now buried beneath hundreds of meters of Atlantic Coastal Plain sediments. The new magnetic data will help map faults, folds, and other features within and around this buried basin.  The radiometric data, which provide relative estimates of K, Th, and U from gamma spectrometry within the upper 1 meter of Earth's surface, will help with mapping of map heavy mineral sand 9placer) deposits, providing insights into how these deposits form in response to coastal and fluvial processes. Heavy mineral sand (placer) deposits are observed in the eastern U.S. from New Jersey to northern Florida and contain titanium, zirconium, and rare earth elements, all which are considered critical mineral resources.

pdescrip: Earth Mapping Resource Initiative Project
abstract: The high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey was acquired in the fall of 2019 and covers parts of southeast Illinois, southern Indiana, and western Kentucky. The survey covers a 91 by-128 km area  centered on the Hicks Dome mineralized intrusive center located in Hardin County Illinois.  Hicks Dome is a ~270 Ma peralkaline igneous breccia complex that contains rare earth elements, fluorspar, and thorium mineralization and lies within the Illinois-Kentucky fluorspar district. The survey includes 61,974 line km of magnetic and radiometric data that was collected from a fixed-wing aircraft flown at a nominal flight height 116 meters (m) above topography along N-S flight lines spaced at a 200-m line spacing. Tie lines were flown in an E-W direction every 3000m. The mineral deposit types of interest in the survey area are: 1) peralkaline intrusion-related breccia complex with HREE-enriched fluorspar mineralization, and 2) sedimentary-hosted Mississippi Valley Type fluorite, Pb, Zn, Cd, Ge, Ag, and barite. The potential critical mineral commodities investigated are: REE, fluorspar, beryllium, niobium, titanium, and barite with additional prospectivity for non-critical thorium, lead, zinc, silver, cadmium, and yttrium.

pdescrip: Rare earth element deposits near Mountain Pass, CA
abstract: The high-resolution airborne magnetic and radiometric survey was acquired starting December 2019 in the southeast Mojave Desert of California and Nevada. The survey was designed to meet the complimentary needs related to geologic mapping and mineral resource research. The effort is focused on the only economically proven rare earth element (REE) deposit in the U.S. at Mountain Pass, California, a ~1.4 Ga carbonatite deposit associated with alkaline granitoid rocks and hosted by an areally extensive 1.7 Ga gneiss. The 40x80 km survey includes magnetic and radiometric data collected from a fixed-wing aircraft along flight lines spaced at 200 m. The mineral systems of interest in the survey area are magmatic carbonatite and associated alkaline intrusive rocks, and gneiss-hosted REE. The potential critical mineral commodities investigated include rare earth elements; Ag, Au, Cu, and Zn in vein deposits; and Nb in carbonatite deposits.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric survey of the Neoarchean Mentor Complex and surrounding region
abstract: The high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey was flown from May of 2021 to July of 2021 in northwestern Minnesota. The survey was funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resource Initiative and designed to meet complementary needs related to geologic mapping and mineral resource research. The survey design was coordinated with the Minnesota Geologic Survey to optimize coverage of geologic features of greatest interest. The effort was focused on region of the Mentor Complex, a Neoarchean anorthosite-mafic intrusive complex situated near the boundaries of multiple subprovinces, and multiple greenstone belts, within the Archean Superior Province. The survey acquired a total of 40,139-line km of magnetic and radiometric data from a fixed-wing aircraft along flight lines spaced at 250 m and a nominal terrain clearance of 120 m. The mineral systems of interest in the survey area include Mafic magmatic, Porphyry Sn, and Metamorphic. Potential critical mineral commodities include Cr, PGE, Co, graphite, REE, Li, Ta, and Sn. There is additional potential for Mn, Ni, Cu, Fe, Mg, and Cs.

pdescrip: A Rank 1 airborne magnetic and radiometric survey is planned for 2020 and will fill the gap between airborne surveys flown by the USGS from 2014-2020. The survey covers an area permissive for at least four mineral systems that may host critical metal deposits.
abstract: As part of the Phase 2 Earth Mapping Resource Initiative (Earth MRI), a high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey was conducted from March to July 2021 over parts of Missouri, Illinois, and Kentucky. The Gap survey is intended to fill the gap between two Rank 1 magnetic and radiometric surveys flown from 2014 to 2020 by the USGS.  The Gap survey was jointly funded by the Missouri Geological Survey, the USGS National Cooperative Geologic Mapping Program and the Earth MRI and designed to meet the complimentary needs related to geologic mapping, mineral resource, and natural hazard research.  The survey covered a rectangular area 88 by 160 km. Precambrian crystalline basement comprised of the ca 1.3 Ga and ca 1.4 Ga Granite Rhyolite terranes underlies the area and is covered by a Paleozoic sedimentary section.  Permian-age igneous rocks intrude into the sedimentary section. A total of 64,233-line kilometers (km) of magnetic and radiometric data were collected from a fixed-wing aircraft flown at median flight height of 100 (m) above topography along E-W flight lines spaced at 250 m apart. At least four mineral systems are either present or prospective within the Gap geophysical survey and include (1) IOA/IOCG +/- REE +/- Co associated with the Eastern Granite-Rhyolite terrane; (2) magmatic REE with potential for F, REE, Ba, Ge, Y, Nb, Be, and Ti associated with Permian peralkaline complexes and related lampropheric dikes; (3) marine chemocline HREE associated with phosphate enrichments within Upper Devonian, Upper Ordovician and Pennsylvanian sedimentary rocks; and (4)  basin brine path MVT deposits with potential for Co and base metals of Pb, Zn, Cu, Ag, and Ni.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric survey of the Virginia and North Carolina Fall Zone
abstract: This high-resolution Earth MRI magnetic and radiometric survey was flown between January 22 and May 13, 2021. A total of 64,266 line-kilometers were acquired. This effort targeted critical mineral resources in Virginia and North Carolina within both the Atlantic Coastal Plain and the Piedmont Provinces. The 72 km x 185 km survey was flown with a fixed-wing aircraft flown at a nominal flight height of 80 m above ground along E-W flight lines with 275-m spacing. Tie (control) lines were flown in an N-S direction every 2500 m. The survey targeted deposits of heavy mineral sands and their geologic context within the Atlantic Coastal Plain near its boundary with the Piedmont. Minerals containing titanium, zirconium, and rare earth elements (REE) were deposited along the ancient rocky coast that formed when Atlantic Ocean opened. Some were later reworked by fluvial, coastal, tidal, and eolian processes, forming additional deposits elsewhere in the coastal plain. Deposits within the survey area have been mined since the 1990s. The survey also targets the Petersburg terrane, within the Piedmont Province, which may contain REE deposits hosted within regolith (soil). Lastly, the survey includes the Richmond basin, which is part of a series of rift basins that formed during the tectonic events that led to the opening of the Atlantic Ocean. This and the other rift basins are part of the Central Atlantic Magmatic Province (known as CAMP), which in areas is known to contain mafic igneous rocks hosting platinum group elements and cobalt.  The primary potential critical mineral commodities investigated are rare earth elements, titanium, and zirconium, secondary potential minerals include the platinum group elements and cobalt.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric geophysical survey in the eastern Yukon-Tanana Uplands (YTU), Alaska, Eagle survey.
abstract: The high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey was acquired from May 24 to June 27, 2021 in the Yukon-Tanana Uplands (YTU) in eastern Alaska. A total of 26,926 line kilometers were collected covering 9,731 square kilometers. The survey was jointly funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resource Initiative and the State of Alaska Division of Geological & Geophysical Surveys (DGGS) and designed to meet the complimentary needs related to geologic mapping and mineral-resource research. The survey was coordinated with DGGS to optimize coverage while avoiding unnecessary overlap with existing surveys. The area contains parautochthonous North America (Lake George assemblage), overlain by allochthonous Ladue/Fortymile assemblage and Klondike Schist, which has been intruded by mid-Cretaceous through Paleocene igneous rocks. The area's mineral potential includes orogenic- and intrusion-related gold, sediment-hosted and volcanogenic massive sulfides, porphyry molybdenum, tin-tungsten skarns, and antimony-arsenic-gold quartz-vein systems.
pkeyword: tin; tungsten; aeromagnetic surveying; aeroradiometric surveying; study areas; geophysics; metamorphic rocks

pdescrip: Aeromagnetic survey focused on an area of Tertiary alkaline intrusive rocks that are known to have high REE concentrations and which are exemplified by the Round Top REE deposit, Hudspeth County, Texas.
abstract: This high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey in the northern Trans-Pecos region of Texas and parts of southern New Mexico was acquired in early 2021. The survey was funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resource Initiative and will be coordinated with both the Texas Bureau of Economic Geology and the New Mexico Bureau of Geology and Mineral Resources. The effort is focused on an area of Tertiary alkaline intrusive rocks that are known to have high REE concentrations and which are exemplified by the Round Top REE deposit, Hudspeth County, Texas. The survey includes 34,483-line km of magnetic and radiometric data collected from both a helicopter and fixed-wing aircraft covering 2,419 square miles.  Flight lines were spaced at 200 m and draped at 125 m altitude. The mineral systems of interest in the survey area are: REE in highly fractionated rhyolites, REE-Th-U veins, REE-F veins, Cu-REE-F veins, REE-F breccia pipes, Fe skarns, Porphyry Mo, and a potential for Carbonatites. The potential critical mineral commodities investigated include: REE, W, U, Nb, Be, F, Rb, Hf, Ta, Sn, Li, and Zr, with additional prospectively for non-critical Mo, Cu, Fe, and Th. Much of the survey area is composed of Cretaceous sedimentary rock or Quaternary basin fill and the survey is designed to reveal concealed Tertiary intrusive rocks that are associated with mineral deposits.
pkeyword: beryllium; copper; fluorine; hafnium; iron; lithium; molybdenum; niobium; rare earth elements; rubidium; tantalum; thorium; tin; tungsten; uranium; zirconium; aeromagnetic surveying; aeroradiometric surveying; study areas; geophysics

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric survey of the Wet Mountains, Colorado
abstract: A high-resolution, airborne magnetic and radiometric survey was conducted over the region surrounding the Wet Mountains of southern Colorado, including parts of Custer and Fremont Counties.  The data were collected as part of the Earth Mapping Resource Initiative (Earth MRI) program of the U.S. Geological Survey (USGS) in collaboration with the Colorado Geological Survey to better understand the geology and critical mineral resources in the area.  Several alkaline intrusive complexes are of particular interest because they are known to be enriched in thorium, rare earth elements (REE), and other critical minerals. The three alkaline suites, at McClure Mountain, Gem Park, and Democrat Creek, include mafic and ultramafic compositions, carbonatites, syenites, and lamprophyres.  Extensive areas of veins, dikes, and fracture zones associated with the complexes have known occurrences of rare earth elements, niobium, thorium, minor titanium, and potential for tantalum, uranium, and vanadium.  Additional mineral systems in the area include Climax-type, alunite lithocap, and epithermal gold and silver mineralization, with potential for additional critical minerals such as aluminum, tin, and tungsten, as well as other commodities, such as molybdenum, gold, silver, copper, lead, and zinc.  The survey was flown in summer of 2021 using a helicopter equipped with a magnetometer mounted in a stinger extending from the nose of the aircraft and a gamma-ray spectrometer stowed onboard.  The helicopter pilots followed pre-planned flight paths in a grid-like pattern, with east-west lines spaced 150 meters apart and north-south lines spaced 1,000 meters apart.  A terrain clearance of 80 meters above ground could be realized in areas of low relief to maximize detection of gamma-rays, but higher clearances, as much as 200–500 meters, were required over rugged terrain and populated areas for safety reasons.  A total of 17,032 kilometers were flown along the lines, covering a 2,200 square-kilometer irregular area.  Data acquisition was completed in July 2021; data processing was completed in February 2022; and the data were released to the public in February 2023.

pdescrip: The Geoscience Data Acquisition for Western Nevada (GeoDAWN) project is a joint effort between the USGS, Department of Energy Geothermal Technologies Office, the Bureau of Land Management, the USDA Natural Resources Conservation Service, and the Nevada Bureau of Mines and Geology  to collect airborne magnetic and radiometric data and lidar data over a large part of western Nevada centered on the Walker Lane geologic province.
abstract: The Geoscience Data Acquisition for Western Nevada (GeoDAWN) project is a joint effort between the USGS, Department of Energy Geothermal Technologies Office, the Bureau of Land Management, the USDA Natural Resources Conservation Service, and the Nevada Bureau of Mines and Geology. The goal of the effort is to collect airborne magnetic and radiometric data and lidar data over a large part of western Nevada centered on the Walker Lane geologic province. The Nevada Bureau of Mines and Geology will conduct geologic mapping in the Clayton Valley and Rhyolite Ridge, areas known to contain lithium resources. The area of focus is highly prospective for geothermal resources--Nevada being second only to California in geothermal energy production and capacity. In addition, the new data collection effort will cover the Humboldt mafic igneous complex, which has potential for hosting iron, cobalt, rare earth elements, platinum group metals, chromium, nickel, and copper.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric survey of the Medicine Bow Mountains, Wyoming
abstract: The high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey is planned for acquisition in the summer of 2023 in the Medicine Bow Mountains of southern Wyoming. The survey is funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resource Initiative, with contributions from the Wyoming State Geological Survey (WSGS), and is designed to optimize coverage of geologic features of greatest interest. The effort is focused on the Lake Owen Complex, a Paleoproterozoic layered mafic intrusion with known PGE mineralization, and the surrounding area that includes the Cheyenne Belt, which marks the southern margin of the Wyoming Province. The planned survey includes magnetic and radiometric data collected from a helicopter along flight lines spaced no wider than 200 m and a nominal terrain clearance of 60-120 m. The mineral systems of interest in the survey area include Mafic magmatic, Magmatic REE, Placer, and Porphyry Cu-Mo-Au, and Volcanogenic seafloor. Potential critical mineral commodities include As, Ba, Be, Bi, Co, Cr, fluorspar, Ga, Ge, Hf, In, Mg, Mn, PGE, REE, Sc Ta, Te, Ti, V, Zn, and Zr. There is additional potential for Ag, Au, Cd, Cu, Fe, La, Mo, Pb, Th, U, and Y.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric survey of the Maine Munsungun Region
abstract: This high-resolution Earth MRI magnetic and radiometric survey in northern Maine was conducted June-July 2021. The study area is marked by volcanic belts of submarine arc and rift-arc related mafic and felsic volcanic rocks of Mid-Upper Ordovician age within the Munsungun anticlinorium. These volcanic rocks host the Bald Mountain and other known volcanic massive sulfide (VMS) deposits. The anticlinorium is part of a larger volcanic belt with additional VMS deposits elsewhere in Maine and in Canada. The region is a priority study area for the Maine Geological Survey, with ongoing bedrock mapping efforts conducted in collaboration with faculty and students from the University of Maine. Mapping in the region can be challenging, however, because much of the area is covered by glacial sediments. The aeromagnetic data facilitates imaging of subsurface volcanic rocks. The targeted geophysical survey area was roughly 50 km x 100 km and conducted using a fixed-wing aircraft flown at a nominal flight height of 100 m above ground along NW-SE flight lines with 250-m spacing. Tie (control) lines were flown in an northeast-southwest direction every 2500 m. A total of 43,271-line kilometers (km) of data were collected. The Bald Mountain and other deposits are known for copper, zinc, gold, and silver, but they also contain Earth MRI Phase II critical mineral commodities cobalt and tin. Secondary potential critical minerals include bismuth, tellurium, indium, germanium, gallium, antimony, and arsenic.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric survey of the Idaho Cobalt Belt
abstract: A high-resolution Earth MRI airborne magnetic and radiometric survey was flown in east-central Idaho from September 12, 2021 to October 31, 2021. The study area lies ~30 km west of Salmon, Idaho and is defined by the Fe-Co-Cu-Au-Bi-rare earth element (REE) deposits known as the Idaho Cobalt Belt (ICB). The ICB, a sulfide facies variant of a magmatic-hydrothermal iron oxide copper-gold (IOCG) deposit contains the largest known cobalt resources in the United States. The ICB is a ~2-10 km-wide, 60 km-long NW-striking belt defined by relatively high concentrations of Co, as well as high concentrations of other critical elements such as REE, Be, U, and Sn. The ICB deposits are generally weakly magnetic and predominantly stratabound, hosted by Mesoproterozoic banded siltites and argillites. Locally, massive, vein-like banded magnetites contain cobaltiferous pyrite, and discordant, tourmalinized breccias also have locally high Co. Mineralization may be associated with nearby Mezoproteroizic peraluminous granites and augen gneisses and/or diabase sills. The ICB deposits have been subject to extensive faulting, folding, shearing, and pervasive recrystallization during Cretaceous thrusting and metamorphism. Metamorphic grades range from lower to middle-upper greenschist facies. The ICB is a priority study area for the Idaho Geologic Survey and previous 1:24,000 scale geologic mapping has focused on the southern ICB. The high-resolution aeromagnetic survey will span existing and planned detailed mapping, and will help define stratigraphic relationships, structural features, and intrusions related to Co deposition and subsequent deformation. The radiometric may help identify K-rich biotite zones which are associated with known Co deposits in the study area. The survey covers an approximately 65 kilometer by 90 kilometer area centered on the igneous complex underlying the Salmon Challis National Forest. Data were collected along east west flight lines spaced 200 meters apart with north south tie lines flown every 2,000 meters. A mean terrain clearance of 200 meters was maintained except where safety dictated a higher elevation. A total of 16,950 line kilometers of data were collected.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric survey of the Boulder Batholith, Montana
abstract: This high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey is planned for acquisition in FY 2021 in Montana. The Late Cretaceous Boulder Batholith and co-magmatic Elkhorn Mountains Volcanics (EMV) are located along the Continental Divide in southwestern MT and together are a rare and well- preserved example of continental arc magmatism. A plethora of precious and base metal mineral deposits, and related critical minerals, are documented within the Boulder Batholith-EMV magma system including two world-class Cu-Mo porphyry systems at Butte, Montana. A variety of precious and base metal veins, Mo-Cu porphyry, and skarn deposits are documented within the volcanic and intrusive rocks of the Boulder Batholith including the largest gold mine (Golden Sunlight mine) and copper mine (Butte mining district) in the state. As well, the region has potential for the critical minerals As, Sb, Te, Bi, Sn, Ga, W and potentially Re, U, REE, Zr, Hf, Nb, Ta, Be, Li, Co, and Ga. Rio Tinto provided some of the funding for this project.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric geophysical survey in part of the western Yukon-Tanana Uplands (YTU), Alaska, White Mountains survey.
abstract: The high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey was acquired from June 30 to August 22, 2021  in part of the western Yukon-Tanana Uplands (YTU) in eastern Alaska. A total of 36,933 line kilometers were collected covering 13,423 square kilometers. The survey was jointly funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resource Initiative and the State of Alaska Division of Geological & Geophysical Surveys (DGGS) and designed to meet the complimentary needs related to geologic mapping and mineral-resource research. The survey was coordinated with DGGS to optimize coverage while avoiding unnecessary overlap with existing surveys. The area covers where the Arctic Alaska Terrane and North American assemblages are juxtaposed. The area's mineral potential includes orogenic- and intrusion-related gold, sediment-hosted and volcanogenic massive sulfides, porphyry molybdenum, tin-tungsten skarns, and antimony-arsenic-gold quartz-vein systems.
pkeyword: tin; tungsten; aeromagnetic surveying; aeroradiometric surveying; study areas; geophysics; metamorphic rocks

pdescrip: 2022 airborne magnetic and radiometric survey: Appalachian Transect, Pennsylvania
abstract: This high-resolution Earth MRI magnetic and radiometric survey covering parts of West Virginia, Pennsylvania, and Maryland is planned for 2022/2023. This survey targets clays containing aluminum, gallium, lithium, titanium, and rare earth elements, metalliferous black shales, mafic magmatic and volcanogenic seafloor deposits, and mafic magmatic and cobalt-bearing skarn deposits of the Newark-Gettysburg Basin. The survey traverses four of the primary physiographic provinces of the eastern U.S. From east to west, these are the Piedmont, Blue Ridge, Valley and Ridge, and Appalachian Plateau Provinces. The Valley and Ridge and Appalachian Plateau comprise sedimentary rock. Radiometric data, which are sensitive to low amounts of K, U, and Th, can highlight outcropping sedimentary units that have elevated concentrations of various critical minerals. Magnetic data in these areas reflect geologic variations in the underlying crystalline basement. The Piedmont and Blue Ridge comprise mostly crystalline rock; within these provinces the magnetic data will be especially helpful for highlighting shallow or exposed volcanic rocks and magmatic bodies along with the faults and folds that deform them. The radiometric data will help define lithologic boundaries near the surface. The information from these datasets will be helpful not only for understanding the geologic framework of mineral deposits, but also for evaluating earthquake hazard risk, which is elevated in parts of the survey area. The survey, which at its widest dimensions is about 350 km E-W and 200-km N-S, is being flown with a combination of fixed wing and helicopter aircrafts at a nominal flight height of 120 meters (m) above topography. The E-W flight lines will have 250-m line spacing and N-S tie-lines will have 2500-m line spacing. The Earth MRI mineral systems of interest include chemical weathering, mafic magmatic, marine chemocline, and volcanogenic seafloor deposits. The critical mineral commodities investigated are: aluminum, antimony, arsenic, barite, beryllium, chromium, cobalt, gallium, graphite, lithium, magnesium, manganese, nickel, platinum group elements, rare earth elements, tellurium, tungsten, uranium, vanadium, and zinc.

pdescrip: 2022 airborne magnetic and radiometric survey: Alabama Florida Source to Sink
abstract: This high-resolution Earth MRI magnetic and radiometric survey is planned for 2022/2023. Covering parts of Alabama and Florida, it targets the Alabama graphite belt, Ti-Zr-REE placer deposits, Cr- and Mg-bearing ultramafic rocks, the Alabama pegmatite zone (Ta and Sn), the Cragford and Goldville-Hog Mountain districts, eastern Blue Ridge volcanogenic massive sulfide deposits, potential REE regolith deposits, and the western part of the South Georgia Rift Basin. The survey extends from crystalline rock within the Piedmont Province in Alabama to the Gulf Coast in Florida. Within the Piedmont Province, the magnetic and radiometric data will facilitate the imaging of ancient volcanic rock, magma bodies, faults, folds, and lithologic boundaries, providing geologic framework information for mineral deposits. This survey will also complement a planned airborne electromagnetic survey over the Alabama graphite belt. The traverse over coastal plain sediments to the coast provides "source to sink" coverage of Ti-Zr-REE-bearing minerals that were eroded from Piedmont crystalline rock and can ultimately form economic placer deposits. The radiometric data highlight areas with shallow concentrations of these minerals by detecting Th in monazite, a mineral often found in Ti-Zr-REE placer deposits. The magnetic data over the coastal plain areas will image variations in crystalline rock buried beneath the sediments, assisting with the mapping of ancient magma bodies, volcanic rocks, faults, tectonic deformation, and accreted exotic terranes. The roughly 450 x 200 km region is being flown with a fixed-wing aircraft flown at a nominal flight height of 120 meters (m) above topography along N-S flight lines with 250-m spacing. Tie (control) lines are flown in an E-W direction every 2500 m. The Earth MRI mineral systems of interest include metamorphic, mafic magmatic, placer, porphyry, and volcanogenic seafloor deposits. The potential critical mineral commodities investigated are: aluminum, antimony, arsenic, beryllium, bismuth, cesium, chromium, cobalt, gallium, graphite, hafnium, lithium, magnesium, manganese, nickel, niobium, platinum group elements, rare earth elements, rubidium, scandium, tantalum, tellurium, titanium, tin, yttrium, zinc, and zirconium.

pdescrip: 2022 airborne magnetic and radiometric survey: Kuskokwim Focus Area, Alaska
abstract: This high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey is planned for acquisition in the summer of 2023 and is adjacent to the FY2021 Earth MRI high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey of the Yukon Tanana Uplands (YTU). The survey is funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resources Initiative and is designed to meet complementary needs related to mineral resource assessments and mapping studies to be conducted by the USGS and DGGS. Critical and non-critical mineral deposits are dispersed throughout the KR region's various terranes and flysch units, and are spatially and genetically associated with different ages and compositions of intrusive suites and fault systems. In addition to being part of the world-class Tintina Gold Belt, with numerous significant gold deposits, the KR region contains known tin, rare-earth-element, tungsten, and antimony deposits, and has high potential for additional undiscovered critical mineral deposits.

pdescrip: 2022 airborne magnetic and radiometric survey: Central Great Basin, Utah
abstract: A high-resolution airborne magnetic and radiometric survey is planned for acquisition in 2022 in western Utah. The survey is funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resource Initiative and is designed to meet the complimentary needs related to geologic mapping and mineral resource research. The effort includes an economically proven tract of beryllium deposits along the east-west Deep Creek-Tintic Belt and numerous other critical minerals and mineral systems. The airborne survey consists of magnetic and radiometric data collected using fixed-wing and/or helicopter aircraft along flight lines spaced about 200-m part. The mineral systems of interest in the survey area include Basin brine path, Carlin-type, Climax-type, Lacustrine evaporite, Marine chemocline, Meteoric convection, and Porphyry Cu-Mo-Au. The potential critical mineral commodities include antimony, arsenic, beryllium, bismuth, fluorspar, indium, lithium, magnesium, manganese, PGE, potash, rhenium, scandium, tellurium, tungsten, uranium, vanadium, and zinc.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric survey of the Sierra Madre-Elkhead Mountains-Medicine Bow Mountains region, Wyoming
abstract: This high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey is planned for acquisition in the summer of 2023 in the greater Sierra Madre-Elkhead Mountains-Medicine Bow Mountains region along the Wyoming-Colorado border. The survey is funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resource Initiative and is designed to meet complementary needs related to geologic mapping and mineral resource research. The survey design is coordinated with the Wyoming State Geologic Survey, the Colorado Geological Survey, and staff from the National Cooperative Geologic Mapping Program to optimize coverage of geologic features of greatest interest. The survey is also designed to adjoin and augment the previously planned Medicine Bow Mountains airborne magnetic and radiometric survey that will be flown during the summer of 2023. The effort is focused on the Cheyenne Belt corridor along the southern margin of the Archean Wyoming Province, a region that contains several known and suspected mineral systems of high interest for their critical mineral potential. There has been abundant past and current exploration and mining, although exploration efforts are hampered by a lack of high-quality geophysical data. Several fundamental questions on the region's structure and Paleoproterozoic tectonomagmatic evolution are also unresolved. The airborne survey data are further expected to aid mapping of suspected Quaternary faults and elements of the geology important to groundwater resources in the Saratoga Valley. The planned survey includes magnetic and radiometric data collected from a helicopter along flight lines spaced 200 m and a nominal terrain clearance of 100 m. Parts of the survey area may be suitable for a fixed-wing aircraft. The mineral systems of interest in the survey area include Climax-type, Mafic magmatic, Magmatic REE, Placer, Porphyry Cu-Mo-Au, and Volcanogenic Seafloor. Potential critical mineral commodities include As, Be, Bi, Co, Cr, fluorspar, Hf, Ga, Ge, In, Mg, Mn, Nb, Ni, PGE, REE, Sb, Sc, Sn, Ta, Te, V, and Zr. There is additional potential for Ag, Au, Cd, Cu, Fe, Mo, Pb, Se, vermiculite, and U.

pdescrip: 2022 airborne magnetic and radiometric survey: Western Spirit Lake tectonic zone and Trans-Hudson orogen, Iowa
abstract: This high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey is planned for acquisition during 2022 and/or 2023 in the region of the entirely concealed western Spirit Lake tectonic zone in parts of Iowa, Minnesota, South Dakota, and Nebraska. Parts of the Trans-Hudson orogen may also be covered. The survey is funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resource Initiative and is designed to meet complementary needs related to geologic mapping and mineral resource research. The survey design is coordinated with the Iowa Geological Survey, Minnesota Geological Survey, South Dakota Geological Survey, and Nebraska Geological Survey to optimize coverage of geologic features of greatest interest. The western Spirit Lake tectonic zone forms the southern boundary of the Archean Superior Province between the Mesoproterozoic Midcontinent Rift and the Paleoproterozoic Trans-Hudson orogen, and is the location of the suture resulting from the Paleoproterozoic Yavapai orogeny. This is thought to be a highly favorable regional environment for mineralization, and several known intrusions have notable critical mineral potential, however the region is significantly underexplored and existing geophysical data are of poor quality. The planned survey includes magnetic and radiometric data collected from a fixed-wing aircraft along flight lines spaced 400 m and a nominal terrain clearance of 100 m. Mineral systems of interest in the survey area include Mafic Magmatic, Magmatic REE, IOA-IOCG, Porphyry Sn, and Chemical Weathering. Potential critical mineral commodities include PGEs, Co, REEs, Ni, Ti, Nb, Sc, Li, Sn, Ti, and Cr. There is additional potential for Cu and Au.

pdescrip: 2022 airborne magnetic and radiometric survey: Northeast Washington, Washington
abstract: This high-resolution airborne magnetic and radiometric survey is planned for acquisition during 2022 and/or 2023 in northeastern Washington state. The survey is funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resource Initiative and is designed to meet complementary needs related to geologic mapping and mineral resource research. The survey design is coordinated with the Washington Department of Natural Resources geologic and geophysical mapping team to optimize coverage of geologic features of great interest. Terrane accretion along the western margin of Paleozoic North America, followed by subsequent deformation, metamorphism, and intrusion, has created series of diverse mineralogic districts in northeastern Washington State. Northeastern Washington hosts nearly all the potential mineral system in the state. Existing exploration data indicates that the structure and minerology of these systems are complex. The distribution and grade of ore is uncertain, and mechanistic controls on ore minerals deposition and emplacement are poorly understood. The planned survey includes magnetic and radiometric data from a helicopter survey along flight lines spaced 200 meters and a nominal terrane clearance of 100 meters. Mineral systems of interest include Basin brine path, Chemical weathering, Climax-type, Mafic magmatic, Magmatic REE, Metamorphic, Meteoric recharge, Placer, Porphyry Cu-Mo-Au, Porphyry Sn (granite-related), Reduced intrusion-related, Volcanogenic seafloor.

pdescrip: 2022 airborne electromagnetic survey: Graphite Vanadium Belt, Alabama
abstract: This high-resolution airborne electromagnetic survey (AEM) is planned for acquisition in 2022 over the Piedmont province of the southern Appalachian Mountains in Alabama. The survey is funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resources Initiative and is designed to meet complementary needs related to mineral resource assessments and regional geologic framework and mapping studies to be conducted by the USGS and the Geological Survey of Alabama. The survey will focus on the Alabama graphite-vanadium belt, including areas of historic graphite mining, and the subsurface extent of the belt beneath the Inner Piedmont and Coastal Plain provinces. Secondary goals include mapping: (1) structural fabric, including fault systems that dissect and imbricate structural panels throughout the region, (2) porphyry systems hosting critical minerals within the Cragford, Goldville-Hog Mountain, and Alabama pegmatite districts and (3) black shales and placer deposits within the adjoining Valley and Ridge and Coastal Plain provinces, respectively. The planned survey includes time-domain electromagnetic and magnetic data to be collected from a helicopter-towed system. Flight lines will be oriented perpendicular to the range front, spaced at 500 m over prospective lithologies within the graphite-vanadium belt and expanding to 1000 m outside of the belt. Flight lines will be flown with a system terrain clearance of 30-50 m. Expected depth of penetration is 300-500 m and with resolution as shallow as 5-10 m depth.

pdescrip: 2022 airborne electromagnetic survey: Basin and Range, Nevada
abstract: This regional airborne electromagnetic survey (AEM) is planned for acquisition over 3 years beginning in 2022 and covering a large portion of the Basin & Range (B&R) province in Nevada and smaller extents in California and Oregon. The survey is funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resources Initiative and is designed to meet complementary needs related to mineral resource assessments and regional geologic framework and mapping studies to be conducted by the USGS and state geologic surveys. The B&R province hosts a diversity of base, precious, and critical mineral resources. This survey is focused on lithium brines within arid, closed basins throughout the region as well as lithium clays within post-Miocene calderas in the northern survey area. Secondary goals include mapping porphyry, Climax-type, and Carlin-type mineral systems hosting a range of critical minerals as well as imaging the numerous fault systems that dissect the region and exert a primary control on certain types of mineralization. The planned survey includes time-domain electromagnetic and magnetic data to be collected from a helicopter-towed system. Flight lines will be oriented west to east, nominally perpendicular to range fronts, at a spacing of 5 km. Flight lines will be flown with a system terrain clearance of 30-50 m. Expected depth of penetration is between 300 m and 500 m and with resolution as shallow as 5-10 m depth. The data to be collected may further be relevant to water and geothermal resource investigations as well as earthquake and volcanic hazard studies.

pdescrip: 2022 airborne electromagnetic survey: Seward Peninsula, Alaska
abstract: This high-resolution airborne electromagnetic survey (AEM) is planned for acquisition in 2022 in the Kigluaik, Bendeleben, and Darby mountains on the Seward Peninsula, Alaska. The survey is funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resources Initiative and is designed to meet complementary needs related to mineral resource assessments and regional geologic framework and mapping studies to be conducted by the USGS and DGGS. The survey will focus on the Kigluaik, Bendeleben, and Darby Cretaceous gneiss domes composed of Neoproterozoic--Mesozoic(?) metasedimentary and plutonic rocks. The Kigluaik Mountains gneiss dome hosts the world-class Graphite Creek deposit. However, the detailed structure of the dome and regional extent of graphitic rocks in the adjacent Bendeleben and Darby gneiss domes are uncertain. The planned survey incudes time-domain electromagnetic and magnetic data to be collected from a helicopter-towed system along flight lines oriented perpendicular to the range front, spaced at 1000 m, and with a system terrain clearance of 30-50 m. Expected depth of penetration is 300-500 m and with resolution as shallow as 5-10 m depth. The survey will target graphite (±V) mineral systems, while also delineating neighboring stratigraphy, faults, and regional structural fabrics that may influence other system types. The potential critical mineral commodities investigated are tin, lithium, fluorspar, tantalum, beryllium, tungsten, platinum group elements, graphite, uranium, and vanadium.

pdescrip: 2022 airborne electromagnetic survey: Arkoma Basin, Arkansas
abstract: A high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey is planned that will cover the eastern side of the Arkoma Basin in 2022.  The survey is funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resource Initiative (EMRI) and will serve the complementary needs for other earth science efforts to conduct geologic mapping, define potential seismic hazards, and develop a better understanding regarding carbon storage capacity and geothermal resources.  The new survey will connect several previously flown airborne magnetic and radiometric surveys providing a continuous high-resolution magnetic and radiometric coverage across central Arkansas and southeast Missouri. The focus of the new airborne survey includes 15 EMRI focus areas that have been formed from eight mineral systems including the enigmatic Magnet Cove carbonatite and other associated features within the Arkansas alkaline/carbonatite province. The survey is designed to collect data along N-S flight lines spaced 300 m apart at a nominal elevation of 80-100 m above ground using a fixed-wing aircraft. Potential critical mineral commodities and by-products include Sb, Li, REE, Co, Ba, F, Be, U, Ti, Sr, Ge, Ga, In, Mn, V, phosphate, Zn, Cd, Al, Se, and Pb.

pdescrip: 2022 airborne magnetic and radiometric survey: Porphyry Copper Belt, New Mexico
abstract: The New Mexico porphyry copper belt aeromagnetic/aeroradiometric survey will be flown in FY22 in the southwestern part of the state. This high-resolution survey will cover approximately 25,000 square km with 200 m line spacing draped at 125 m altitude. The survey area includes multiple mineral systems, many known mineral occurrences, and many productive mineral deposits. Mineral systems present in the survey area include porphyry Cu-Mo-Au, magmatic REE, marine evaporite, lacustrine evaporite, hybrid magmatic REE / basin brine path, climax type, chemical weathering, and borderland carbonate replacement. Potential commodities include REE, aluminum, arsenic, bismuth, gallium, germanium, indium, potash, rhenium, scandium, tellurium, tin, tungsten, uranium, zinc, copper, gold, lead, antimony, molybdenite, silver, lithium. In addition, 50 to 60 percent of the survey area is primarily unexplored in that it is bedrock (potential host rock) concealed by Cenozoic basin fill sediments of the Basin and Range Geologic Province. This new high-resolution data may help define potential concealed mineral resources occurrences and will compliment geologic mapping to be performed by the New Mexico Bureau of Geology and Mineral Resources.

pdescrip: Salton Sea regional geophysical survey
abstract: This high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey is being conducted over the Imperial Valley, Salton Sea and surrounding areas in southern California starting from mid-January 2022 into Spring 2023.  This effort, referred to as GeoFlight: Salton Trough initiative, is supported by the U.S. Geological Survey's Earth Mapping Resources Initiative and the Department of Energy's Geothermal Technologies Office. Its primary focus is to aid studies into the region's Li-brine and hidden geothermal resources.  The magnetic and radiometric data will help map surface geology and soils and characterize subsurface geology and structure.  Additionally, the USGS is conducting a high-altitude airborne lidar survey over the area (in cooperation with the Bureau of Land Management and Natural Resources Conservation Service), and coordinating detailed geologic mapping efforts through the California Geological Survey. Together, these data will result in more accurate and detailed 3D geologic maps that will aid resource exploration, characterization, and development, while also contributing to a wide range of research in geology, hydrology, the environment, and natural hazards such as earthquakes and volcanic eruptions.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric geophysical survey of 7 counties in Montana
abstract: This high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey is planned for acquisition in FY 2023 in Montana, including parts of Beaverhead, Deer Lodge, Granite, Jefferson, Madison, Powell, and Silver Bow Counties. The survey is funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resource Initiative (EMRI) and is designed to meet complementary needs related to geologic mapping, earthquake hazards, and mineral resource research. The survey footprint is coordinated with the Montana Bureau of Mines and Geology and extends coverage over the Boulder Batholith and famous Butte mining district, in addition to the historic Philipsburg and Hecla mining districts. The survey will use a helicopter to collect magnetic and radiometric data along east-west flight lines spaced 200 m apart and flown 100-300 m above ground.  The airborne geophysical survey will provide valuable information about the geology below the ground surface. This new information will be combined with geologic mapping and sampling by the Montana Bureau of Mines and Geology to develop an improved understanding of the fundamental geologic framework underpinning the mining districts, which will help industry determine the potential for undiscovered critical minerals including As, Sb, Te, Bi, Sn, Ga, W and potentially Re, U, REE, Zr, Hf, Nb, Ta, Be, Li, Co, and Ga, and associated co- and by-product commodities (base, precious, industrial minerals).

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric geophysical survey adjacent to the FY21 Yukon Tanana Uplands (YTU)
abstract: This high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey is an extension planned for acquisition in the summer of 2023 and is adjacent to the FY2021 Earth MRI high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey of the Yukon Tanana Uplands (YTU). The survey is funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resources Initiative and is designed to meet complementary needs related to mineral resource assessments and mapping studies to be conducted by the USGS and DGGS. Critical and non-critical mineral deposits are dispersed throughout the KR region's various terranes and flysch units, and are spatially and genetically associated with different ages and compositions of intrusive suites and fault systems. In addition to being part of the world-class Tintina Gold Belt, with numerous significant gold deposits, the KR region contains known tin, rare-earth-element, tungsten, and antimony deposits, and has high potential for additional undiscovered critical mineral deposits.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric geophysical survey of western Kentucky and Northern Tennessee
abstract: This high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey is planned for acquisition during 2023-24 in a region of the southern midcontinent that covers western Kentucky and northern Tennessee, and smaller parts of the adjacent states of Arkansas, Missouri, Illinois, and Indiana. The survey is funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resource Initiative (EMRI) and is designed to meet complementary needs related to geologic mapping, earthquake hazards, and mineral resource research. The survey footprint is coordinated with the state geological surveys to optimize coverage of geologic features of greatest interest. The survey is named after the Kentucky-Tennessee body, which is a large magnetic anomaly first identified in satellite data in the 1980’s.  The source for the anomaly is entirely concealed but several mineral resource districts with critical mineral potential are located along its edge. In addition, the survey will image major tectonic features and their controls on surface faults including the Reelfoot Rift, Rome Trough and Rough Creek graben.  The survey is expected to advance geologic hazards research by providing continuous magnetic coverage to image basement faults related to the active New Madrid and Wabash Valley seismic zones.  The radiometric data will cover numerous regions with highly elevated domestic radon concentrations.  The survey will replace poor quality magnetic and radiometric data and augment existing high-resolution geophysical surveys over an underexplored region of the midcontinent that is thought to contain critical mineral potential. The planned survey will collect data from a fixed-wing aircraft(s) along flight lines spaced 400 m and a nominal terrain clearance of 100 m. Eight EMRI focus areas will be mapped that represent several critical mineral systems. Potential critical mineral commodities include Ba, Co, F, Ga, Ge, REE, Sr, Zn, phosphate. There is additional potential for Pb, Cd, Cu, and Ag.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric geophysical survey of the Arizona Porphyry Copper Belt
abstract: The southwestern North America porphyry copper province comprises one of the world’s largest endowments of copper, molybdenum, and other elements due to the Late Cretaceous to early Paleogene (“Laramide”) magmatism and related hydrothermal systems in the region. USGS Earth MRI will conduct the Arizona porphyry copper belt aeromagnetic/aeroradiometric survey over part of this region to help understand mineral endowment in the Southwest United States. The Arizona porphyry copper belt aeromagnetic/aeroradiometric survey will take place in the southeastern corner of Arizona beginning in late 2023 or early 2024.  The high-resolution survey will have a flight-line spacing of 200-meters and be flown as a draped surface with a 120-meter terrain clearance over level terrain and up to a 200-meter terrain clearance over mountainous regions. Covering over 28,000 square kilometers the survey will be comprised of almost 155,000 flight-line kilometers of acquired data. The survey area includes multiple mineral systems, many known mineral occurrences, and many mineral deposits that either are producing or have produced ore. Mineral systems present in the survey area include Porphyry Cu-Mo-Au, volcanogenic seafloor, Porphyry Sn, climax type, mafic magmatic, IOA-IOCG, chemical weathering, lacustrine evaporite, hybrid magmatic REE basin brine path, magmatic REE, and marine evaporites. Potential commodities include aluminum (alunite), arsenic, bismuth, cobalt, copper, gallium, germanium, gold, indium, lead, lithium, manganese, molybdenite, PGE, potash, REE, rhenium, scandium, silver, sulfuric acid, tellurium, tin, tungsten, uranium, and zinc. Occurring in the Basin and Range Geologic Province, the survey area is mostly unexplored for mineral occurrences and deposits because bedrock (potential deposit host rock) is concealed by Cenozoic basin fill sediments over 50 to 60 percent of the survey area. This new high-resolution survey data will help define potential concealed mineral resources occurrences and will compliment ongoing mapping and geochemical studies being done by the Arizona Geological Survey and others.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric geophysical survey of northern Idaho and southwestern Montana
abstract: As part of the USGS Earth Mapping Resource Initiative (EMRI), the U.S. Geological Survey, in cooperation with the Idaho Geological Survey and the Montana Bureau of Mines and Geology, is conducting a high-resolution aeromagnetic and aeroradiometric survey to be flown in FY2023 in northern Idaho and southwestern Montana. The survey will support geologic mapping and mineral resource research. The survey will be flown according to specifications for high-resolution data collection: nominal 100 m flight height above ground and 200 m spacing between adjacent flight lines. With an aerial extent of 3,000 km2, the survey will cover large areas of the Idaho Cobalt Belt and the western edge of the Montana-Idaho porphyry belt, which have the potential to host critical REE, cobalt, titanium, and niobium minerals. The survey will help map rocks and minerals at the surface across vast regions that may have limited outcrop due to extensive vegetation. It will also help map subsurface geology, where some rocks can be located 100’s to 1000’s of m below the surface based on their magnetic signature.  Such mapping can improve the understanding of the geologic framework within which the potential mineral resource may occur. The survey data will be made publicly available to support resource management decision-making at the Federal, State, and local levels of government.

pdescrip: Airborne electromagnetic survey continues FY22 Basin and Range provice survey
abstract: This regional airborne electromagnetic survey is a continuation of data acquisition that began in 2022 covering portions of the Basin and Range province in Nevada and smaller extents in Oregon and California. The survey is funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resources Initiative and is designed to meet complementary needs related to mineral resource assessments as well as geologic framework and geologic mapping studies being conducted by the USGS and the Nevada Bureau of Mines and Geology.  The Basin and Range province hosts a diversity of base, precious, and critical mineral resources. The survey area includes substantial basin brine and lacustrine evaporite mineral systems prospective for lithium resources, as well as Cu-Mo-Au porphyry, Climax type, IOA-IOGC, and metamorphic mineral systems. The survey will also provide subsurface context on the numerous fault systems that dissect the region and exert a primary control on fluid and heat flow and certain types of mineralization. The airborne survey includes time-domain electromagnetic and magnetic data to be collected from a helicopter-towed system. Flight lines will be oriented perpendicular to the major structural trends with a nominal spacing of 5 km. Flight lines will be flown with a target system terrain clearance of 30-60 m. Expected depth of penetration is between 300 and 500 m and with resolution as shallow as 10 m depth. These data may be further relevant to water and geothermal resource investigations as well as earthquake and volcanic hazards.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric geophysical survey in southwestern Utah
abstract: As part of the USGS Earth Mapping Resource Initiative (EMRI), the U.S. Geological Survey, in cooperation with the Utah Geological Survey and other state and tribal entities, are conducting a high-resolution aeromagnetic and aeroradiometric survey to be flown in FY2032 in southwestern Utah. This survey will acquire magnetic and radiometric data to support geologic investigations that include geologic mapping, mineral, energy, and groundwater resource research. The survey will be flown according to specifications for high-resolution data collection: nominal 100 m flight height above ground and 200 m spacing between adjacent flight lines. With an aerial extent of 10,000 km2, the survey will cover important mineral-rich regions including Goldstrike, Iron Axis, and Tutsagubet mining districts. These districts contain the critical minerals antimony, barite, gallium, germanium, and uranium, as well as other important minerals such as copper, gold, iron, phosphorus, selenium, and silver. Data from the survey will help identify rocks and minerals at the surface across vast regions where vegetation obscures the identity of the rocks. It will also help map the geology in the subsurface, where some rocks can be located 100’s to 1000’s of m below the surface based on their magnetic signature.  Such identification improves geologic mapping research, which in turn improves the understanding of the regional rock structure and history that is central to understanding mineral and energy resource potential, and groundwater capacity and flow. When published, the survey will be publicly available for improved decision-making at federal, state, and local levels of government, and in the private sector for resource management and development.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric geophysical survey of the central and southwestern portions of the Colorado Mineral Belt
abstract: A high-resolution, airborne magnetic and radiometric survey over the middle and southwestern portions of the Colorado Mineral Belt, southwestern Colorado, is planned for acquisition as part of the USGS Earth Mapping Resource Initiative (Earth MRI).  The Colorado Mineral Belt is a broad, northeast-southwest trending alignment of historic mining districts that have produced multiple types of commodities, including critical minerals that are vital to the Nation’s security and economy.  The airborne survey will cover a wide swath from the Gunnison mining district on the northeast to the La Plata mining district on the southwest, including parts of Chaffee, Gunnison, Saguache, Hinsdale, Ouray, San Miguel, San Juan, Dolores, Montezuma, and La Plata Counties.  The survey will use a helicopter to collect magnetic and radiometric data along east-west flight lines spaced 200 m apart and flown 100-300 m above ground.  Areas with restricted airspace, such as Wilderness Areas, will be avoided.  The airborne geophysical survey will provide valuable information about the geology below the ground surface.  This new information, along with geologic mapping by the Colorado Geological Survey and USGS, will lead to a better understanding of the fundamental geologic framework underpinning the Colorado Mineral Belt.  The new understanding should shed light on how multiple mineralizing events concentrate in one region over millions of years and help industry determine the potential for undiscovered critical minerals.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric geophysical survey of parts of North and South Carolina
abstract: This high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey covering parts of North and South Carolina is planned for 2024 through funding from the USGS Earth Mapping Resource Initiative. This region, which encompasses Piedmont crystalline rock and Atlantic Coastal Plain sediments, is the site of over 20 focus areas that are prospective for critical mineral resources. These include the Li-rich pegmatites of the Kings Mountain district, Ti-Zr-REE placer deposits within Atlantic Coastal Plain sediments, porphyry deposits of the Haile-Ridgeway mining district, North Carolina fireclays, several Triassic rift basins, volcanogenic seafloor deposits of the Kings Creek mining district, eastern Blue Ridge volcanogenic massive sulfide deposits, various ultramafic rocks with chromite and/or magnesium, and potential for REE regolith deposits. The coverage area also includes part of the Cape Fear Arch, an unusual, poorly understood part of the Atlantic passive margin that is currently experiencing tectonic uplift. Data will be collected along northwest-southeast flight lines spaced 250 m at a nominal height of 90 m above ground. Its longest dimensions are roughly 350 km northwest-southeast and 245 km northeast-southwest. The survey will slightly overlap two magnetic/radiometric surveys flown in South Carolina, one over Charleston (2019) and one along the Fall Zone (2020) to image Ti-Zr-REE placer deposits and assist with the mapping of subsurface faults for earthquake hazard studies. Mineral systems to be covered include chemical weathering, IOA-IOCG, mafic magmatic, meteoritic recharge, placer, porphyry, volcanogenic seafloor deposits. The critical mineral commodities to be investigated include antimony, arsenic, barite, beryllium, bismuth, cesium, chromite, gallium, germanium, indium, lithium, manganese, niobium, platinum group elements (PGE), rare earth elements, scandium, tantalum, tellurium, tin, titanium, and zinc; other commodities include clay, copper, gold, iron, kaolin, lead, molybdenum, selenium, serpentine, silver, talc, thorium, uranium, and yttrium.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric geophysical survey of Puerto Rico
abstract: A high resolution magnetic and radiometric survey of Puerto Rico and the surrounding shelf areas will be conducted starting in early 2023. This survey is collaboratively funded by the Earth Mapping Resources Initiative, USGS Earthquake Hazards Program, and USGS Coastal and Marine Hazards and Resources Program. The multidisciplinary effort will support geologic mapping and tectonic analyses to address studies of earthquake hazards, mineral resources, landslide risk, and other applications. The survey will be flown by a fixed-wing airplane along north-south flight lines that are spaced 250 meters except over a few deeper offshore areas (> 800 m water depth), where they will be spaced 500 meters. The nominal height above ground and water will be 90 meters, with greater heights above populated areas and rugged mountainous terrain. The survey dimensions are approximately 75 km north-south and 215 km east-west. The region is part of a complex deformation zone associated with oblique subduction of the North American plate beneath the Caribbean plates. Situated between the Puerto Rico trench to the north and the Muertos Trench to the south, tectonic complexities and heavy vegetative cover make it difficult to detect and map faults. Large earthquakes include Mw6.4 (2020), Mw~7.1 (1918), and Mw~7.2 (1867) events, some which were associated with tsunamis. Several mineralizing systems are present in Puerto Rico including porphyry, volcanogenic seafloor, chemical weathering, and placer deposits. Associated critical mineral commodities include aluminum, cobalt, gallium, manganese, nickel, platinum group elements (PGE), potash, scandium, and titanium.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric geophysical survey of northwestern New York
abstract: A high resolution Earth MRI magnetic and radiometric survey in northwestern New York is planned for the summer of 2023 or 2024. This effort, which is funded by the Earth Mapping Resources Initiative, will support geologic mapping and research over a portion of Proterozoic metamorphic and igneous rock of the Adirondack lowlands. This region contains known deposits of graphite, zinc, titanium, and other minerals. The magnetic and radiometric survey, at its longest dimensions, is 130 km N-S x 90 km E-W. It will be flown with north-south flight lines spaced 250 meters at a nominal flying height of 120 meters above ground. The Earth MRI mineral systems of interest include basin brine (sedimentary exhalative and Mississippi Valley type), iron-oxide apatite, mafic magmatic, metamorphic, and placer deposits. The primary critical mineral commodities to be investigated are graphite, rare earth elements, titanium, and zinc; there may also be potential for cobalt, fluorite, gallium, germanium, indium, tin, vanadium, and zirconium.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric geophysical survey of South Pass Granite Mountains, Wyoming
abstract: A high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey is planned for acquisition in summer to fall 2023 in the greater South Pass-Granite Mountains region in central Wyoming. Funded by the state of Wyoming and coordinated and contracted by the USGS, the survey is designed to optimize coverage of geologic features of greatest interest and meet complementary needs related to geologic mapping, mineral resource research, and mapping of Quaternary faults. The effort is focused on the areas encompassing and surrounding the Oregon Trail Structural Belt, which may represent the largely obscured boundary between the Beartooth-Bighorn magmatic zone and the southern accreted terranes. This region contains several known and suspected mineral systems of high interest for their critical mineral potential, and has been the subject of abundant past and current exploration and mining. The area covers the South Pass-Atlantic City region, Rattlesnake Hills, Granite Mountains, and the Seminoe-Ferris mountains. Additionally, the airborne survey data are expected to aid in mapping and investigations along the North and South Granite Mountains faults, the Continental Fault, and other suspected Quaternary faults. The planned survey includes magnetic and radiometric data collected from a helicopter along flight lines spaced 200 meters and a nominal terrain clearance of 100 meters. The mineral systems of interest in the survey area include alkalic porphyry, mafic magmatic, magmatic REE, metamorphic graphite, meteoric convection, orogenic gold, porphyry Cu-Mo-Au, and volcanogenic seafloor. Potential critical mineral commodities: aluminum, antimony, arsenic, barium, beryllium, bismuth, cobalt, chromium, fluorspar, gallium, germanium, graphite, hafnium, indium, manganese, niobium, nickel, platinum group elements, rare earth elements, scandium, tantalum, tin, tungsten, vanadium, zinc, and zirconium. There is additional potential for cadmium, copper, gold, iron, lead, mercury, molybdenum, phosphorus, selenium, silver, thorium, and yttrium.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric geophysical survey of Minnesota's Cuyuana Iron Range
abstract: The high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey is planned for acquisition in the non-winter months of 2024 in the region of Minnesota’s Cuyuna Iron Range. The survey is funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resource Initiative and is designed to optimize coverage of geologic features of greatest interest. The Cuyuna Iron Range is known for past production of iron, as well as unusually high concentrations of manganese. The surrounding region has further potential for several additional types of critical mineral deposits, including magmatic sulfides. The planned survey includes magnetic and radiometric data collected from an airplane along flight lines spaced 150 m and a nominal terrain clearance of 100 m. The mineral systems of interest in the survey area include Marine Chemocline, Volcanogenic seafloor, Orogenic, Mafic magmatic, Chemical weathering, and Placer-paleoplacer. Potential critical mineral commodities include Mn, Co, PGE, and Ni. There is significant additional potential for Cu, Fe, and Au in the survey region.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric geophysical survey of northern and central Florida
abstract: A high resolution Earth MRI magnetic and radiometric survey in northern and central Florida is planned for the summer of 2023 or 2024. This effort will support geologic mapping and research for industrial and critical minerals over areas hosting phosphates, heavy mineral sands, and mining waste. Improved knowledge of these resources and the surrounding geology will assist an understanding of these mineral resources and provide information needed to help build hurricane resiliency. The survey is in two parts, comprising a 150 km E-W by 140 km N-S area in northern Florida and a 55 km E-W by 75 km N-S area in central Florida. Data will be collected along east-west flight lines spaced 250 meters at a nominal flying height of 90 m above ground. Earth MRI mineral systems of interest are placer (physical weathering) and phosphate (marine chemocline) systems. Critical commodities to be investigated are titanium, zirconium, and rare earth elements. The survey also covers industrial mineral resources such as phosphates and sand/gravel; titanium and zirconium also have industrial uses as pigments and in ceramics, respectively.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric survey, western Arkansas
abstract: The high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey was acquired in the spring of 2022 and is located in west-central Arkansas. The survey was jointly funded by the National Cooperative Geologic Mapping Program and the Earth Mapping Resources Initiative and designed to meet the complementary needs related to geologic mapping and mineral resource research. The survey covers a rectangular area of 92-by-92 km spanning from the Arkansas River valley to the northern Ouachita Mountains physiographic provinces. The survey area covers part of the Arkoma basin that formed by late Paleozoic faulting and folding and that is important for energy, mineral, and water resources as well as understanding potential for geologic hazards such as landslides, floods, or seismicity. The survey includes 31,006 line-km of magnetic and radiometric data that were collected from a fixed-wing aircraft flown at a median flight height of 170 meters (m) above topography along N-S flight lines spaced at 300 m. Tie lines were flown in an E-W direction every 3000 m. Natural resources in the area include gas deposits formed in faulted Paleozoic sedimentary rock of the Arkoma basin and potential mineral resources in Pennsylvanian black shales that have potential to contain intervals enriched in rare earth elements (REE) and other critical minerals, including molybdenum, nickel, platinum group elements, uranium and vanadium.

pdescrip: Airborne electromagnetic survey in Penokean orogen in the northern Midcontinent, Michgian
abstract: This regional airborne electromagnetic survey is planned for acquisition over multiple years and will parts of the 1.85 Ga Penokean orogen in the northern Midcontinent and the 1.1 Ga Midcontinent Rift System. Initial surveying will be carried out primarily in upper Michigan and northern Wisconsin. The survey is funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resources Initiative and is designed to meet complementary needs related to mineral resource assessments and regional geologic framework and mapping studies to be conducted by the USGS and state geologic surveys. The region is host to numerous base-metal and critical-mineral deposits and historically was one of the most strategically important areas of mineral production for the nation. There is potential for metamorphic graphite in Paleoproterozoic metasedimentary rocks throughout the survey area, with some past production from graphitic slates in Michigan. Rocks of the Midcontinent Rift System host known Ni-Cu-Co-PGE conduit-type and contact-type magmatic sulfide deposits in mafic/ultramafic rocks that intrude the Paleoproterozoic country rock. Recent discoveries of high-grade conduit-type Ni-Cu-PGE-Co deposits in Michigan and Minnesota include the only producing nickel mine in the nation. The geologic setting also indicates significant potential for Iron Oxide Apatite and Iron Oxide-Copper-Gold mineral systems. The survey block covers the entirety of mapped Paleoproterozoic metasedimentary rocks in the region and their geophysically-inferred extension beneath Phanerozoic sedimentary cover in the east. The survey block is drawn to capture the fault-bounded southern extent of Penokean metasedimentary rocks and extends to the north into overlaying MRS clastic sedimentary rocks. The planned survey will include time-domain electromagnetic and magnetic data collected from a helicopter-towed system. Flight lines will be oriented perpendicular to major structural trends with a nominal line spacing of 2 km. Flight lines will be flown with a system terrain clearance of 30-60 m. Expected depth of penetration is between 300 m and 500 m and with resolution as shallow as 5-10 m depth. The data to be collected are further relevant to water resource investigations and surficial mapping studies.

pdescrip: Magnetic and radiometric survey in Upper Peninsula of Michigan and neighboring parts of Wisconsin
abstract: This high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey is planned for acquisition during 2025 in the Upper Peninsula of Michigan and neighboring parts of Wisconsin. The survey will focus on mapping Archean, Paleoproterozoic, and Mesoproterozoic rocks and major tectonic boundaries formed during the amalgamation of the Superior Province, breakup of supercraton Superia, the Penokean orogeny and subsequent Paleoproterozoic orogenies, and the Midcontinent Rift. The survey is funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resource Initiative and is designed to meet complementary needs related to geologic mapping and mineral resource research. The survey design is coordinated with the Michigan Geological Survey to optimize coverage of geologic features of greatest interest. The survey region is known to host economic and subeconomic deposits of several critical minerals, but the rocks of interest are concealed in many areas, there is much still to learn about the geologic framework relevant to the occurrence of those minerals, and the existing geophysical data are of poor quality. Data from the survey will significantly improve our understanding of the region’s tectonic history.
