The planned survey includes magnetic and radiometric data collected from a fixed-wing aircraft along flight lines spaced 150 m in the western Upper Peninsula and Wisconsin, and 400 m in the eastern Upper Peninsula. The nominal terrain clearance is 100 m. Mineral systems of interest in the survey area include Mafic Magmatic, Magmatic REE, IOA-IOCG, Porphyry Sn, Metamorphic Graphite, and Marine Chemocline. Potential critical mineral commodities include Ni, PGEs, Co, graphite, REEs, Li, Mn, Cs, Ta, Sn, Ti, Nb, Cr, and V. There is additional potential for Fe, Cu, and Au.

pdescrip: Magnetic and radiometric survey in Montana, including parts of Beaverhead and Deer Lodge Counties, and in Idaho, including parts of Lemhi, Custer, Clark, Butte, and Blaine Counties
abstract:
This high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey is planned for acquisition in FY 2024 in Montana, including parts of Beaverhead and Deer Lodge Counties, and in Idaho, including parts of Lemhi, Custer, Clark, Butte, and Blaine Counties. The survey is funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resource Initiative (EMRI) and is designed to meet complementary needs related to geologic mapping, earthquake hazards, and mineral resource research. The survey footprint is coordinated with the Montana Bureau of Mines and Geology and Idaho Geological Survey and extends coverage over the northeast-striking Great Falls tectonic zone and Montana-Idaho porphyry belt. The survey will use a helicopter to collect magnetic and radiometric data along east-west flight lines spaced 200 m apart and flown 100-300 m above ground.  
The airborne geophysical survey will provide valuable information about the geology below the ground surface. This new information will be combined with geologic mapping and sampling by the Montana Bureau of Mines and Geology and Idaho Geological Survey to develop an improved understanding of the fundamental geologic framework underpinning the mineral systems in the region, which will help industry determine the potential for undiscovered critical minerals including As, Co, Cu, F, Ga, Ge, In, Mo, Mn, Nb, Re, REEs, Sb, Te, Ti, W, and Zn, as well as base, precious, and industrial minerals.

pdescrip: Southern New Mexico Alkaline Igneous Belt aeromagnetic and aeroradiometric survey
abstract:
The North American Cordilleran alkaline-igneous belt (NACAIB) extends southward from southeastern Alaska along the eastern edge of the Rocky Mountains and Basin and Range Provinces into northeastern Mexico. The unique igneous rocks that make up this belt have high concentrations of rare earth elements (REE), fluorine, zirconium, tellurium, gallium, other critical minerals, gold, and uranium. 
The southern New Mexico Alkaline Igneous Belt aeromagnetic/aeroradiometric survey will cover over 15,150 square kilometers (over 5,800 square miles) within the NACAIB in southern New Mexico. This airborne survey will include over 45,200 flightline kilometers (over 28,000 flightline miles) of passively acquired Earth’s magnetic field and radiometric data with a flightline spacing of 200 meters draped at approximately 120 meters above the ground. The survey will border the existing Trans-Pecos aeromagnetic/aeroradiometric survey to the south and includes several important known critical and other mineral occurrences including the Gallinas Mountains REE (carbonatite), Capitan Mountains REE, White Oaks (gold), Jicarilla (gold), and more. In addition, the survey area lies within the Basin and Range geologic province providing concealed terrane that is virtually unexplored. The current geophysical data in the region is inadequate and these new data will add tremendously to our geologic knowledge, help understand existing mineral occurrences and deposits, and possibly help define unknown mineral occurrences.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric geophysical survey of the Kuskokwim Mineral Belt, 2024 extension.
abstract: This high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey is an extension planned for acquisition in the summer of 2024 and is adjacent to the FY2022 and FY2023 Earth MRI high-resolution magnetic and radiometric surveys of the Kuskokwim Mineral Belt. The survey is funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resources Initiative and is designed to meet complementary needs related to mineral resource assessments and mapping studies to be conducted by the USGS and DGGS. Critical and non-critical mineral deposits are dispersed throughout the KR region's various terranes and flysch units, and are spatially and genetically associated with different ages and compositions of intrusive suites and fault systems. In addition to being part of the world-class Tintina Gold Belt, with numerous significant gold deposits, the KR region contains known tin, rare-earth-element, tungsten, and antimony deposits, and has high potential for additional undiscovered critical mineral deposits.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric survey in east central Nevada
abstract: As part of the USGS Earth Mapping Resource Initiative (EMRI), the U.S. Geological Survey, in cooperation with the Nevada Bureau of Mines and Geology is conducting a high-resolution aeromagnetic and aeroradiometric survey to be flown in FY2024 in east central Nevada. The survey will support geologic mapping and mineral resource research. The survey will be flown according to specifications for high-resolution data collection: nominal 100 m flight height above ground and 200 m spacing between adjacent flight lines. With an aerial extent of 22,600 km2, the survey will cover areas in east central Nevada. Survey targets include Carlin, porphyry copper, reduced intrusion-related, and lacustrine evaporite mineral systems, which contain critical minerals including tungsten, tin, arsenic, lithium, antimony, bismuth, tellurium, manganese, magnesium, beryllium, fluorine, and platinum group metals. The survey will help map rocks and minerals at the surface across vast regions that may have limited outcrop due to vegetation and unconsolidated rock cover. It will also help map subsurface geology, where some rocks can be located 100’s to 1000’s of m below the surface based on their magnetic signature. Such mapping can improve the understanding of the geologic framework within which the potential mineral resource may occur. The survey data will be made publicly available to support resource management decision-making at the Federal, State, and local levels of government.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric survey in southeastern South Dakota, southwestern Minnesota, and extreme northwestern Iowa
abstract: This high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey is planned for acquisition during 2025 in southeastern South Dakota, southwestern Minnesota, and extreme northwestern Iowa. The survey will focus on mapping buried crystalline rocks of the Archean Superior Province. The region is known to have rocks of interest for their critical mineral potential. However, the regional geologic setting is poorly understood, due to the rocks of interest being entirely concealed under younger rocks, and existing geophysical data are of uneven quality. Data from the survey will significantly improve our understanding of the region’s tectonic and magmatic history. The survey is funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resource Initiative and is designed to meet complementary needs related to geologic mapping and mineral resource research. The survey design is coordinated with the South Dakota Geological Survey, Minnesota Geological Survey, and the Iowa Geological Survey to optimize coverage of geologic features of greatest interest. The planned survey includes magnetic and radiometric data collected from a fixed-wing aircraft along flight lines spaced 200 m. The nominal terrain clearance is 100 m. Mineral systems of interest in the survey area include Mafic Magmatic, Volcanogenic seafloor, Magmatic REE, Porphyry Sn, and Orogenic. Potential critical mineral commodities include Ni, Cr, Co, PGE, Te, Ti, V, REE, Zn, Bi, In, Sn, Ge, Ga, Sb, As, Mn, W, Sb, Graphite, Zr, Hf, Nb, Ta, Be, Li, and Cs. There is additional potential for Au, Cu, and Fe.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric survey in the Laramine Mountains of southeast Wyoming
abstract: A high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey is planned for acquisition beginning in 2025 in the Laramie Mountains of southeast Wyoming. The survey is designed to optimize coverage of geologic features of greatest interest, meet complementary needs related to geologic mapping and mineral resource research, and to overlap with the recently completed and ongoing surveys in the region of the Medicine Bow Mountains. The effort is focused on the trace, extent, and geometry of the Cheyenne belt, the suture zone between the Archean Wyoming batholith to the north and the Proterozoic Colorado Province to the south, and related structural features. In the Laramie Mountains, the Cheyenne belt is largely obscured in the survey area by the 1.43 Ga Laramie Anorthosite Complex, a known host for rare earth element and titaniferous magnetite mineralization. Continuous survey data across southeastern Wyoming will contribute to a more complete understanding of this complex continental suture zone and associated mineral systems. Significant mineralization is present along the trace of the Cheyenne belt, largely known through historical mining or private-sector exploration, but little public work is available on the extent and relationships of these systems. The area covers the Mesoproterozoic Sherman and related batholiths, Laramie Anorthosite Complex, Paleoproterozoic Kennedy dike swarm, several Archean greenstone terrane fragments, and the Archean Wyoming batholith and associated intrusions. The planned survey includes magnetic and radiometric data collected from a helicopter along flight lines spaced 200 meters and a nominal terrain clearance of 100 meters. The mineral systems of interest in the survey area include mafic magmatic; magmatic REE; metamorphic graphite; porphyry Cu-Mo-Au, orogenic, or reduced-intrusion related; and volcanogenic seafloor. Potential critical mineral commodities: Al, Sb, As, barite, Be, Bi, Co, Cr, Ga, Ge, graphite, Hf, In, Mn, Nb, Ni, PGEs, REEs, Sc, Ta, Te, Sn, Ti, W, V, Zn, and Zr. There is additional potential for Cd, Cu, Au, Fe, Pb, Hg, Mo, P, Se, Ag, and Th.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric survey in southeastern Nebrask and northeastern Kansas
abstract: This high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey is planned for acquisition during 2025 in southeastern Nebraska and northeastern Kansas. The survey will focus on mapping buried crystalline rocks related to the Mesoproterozoic Midcontinent Rift and the Paleozoic Elk Creek carbonatite. The Elk Creek carbonatite is known to host deposits of critical minerals that have been explored for decades. However, the regional setting for the carbonatite is poorly understood, due to the rocks of interest being entirely concealed under younger rocks, and the existing geophysical data in the region are poor quality. Data from the survey will significantly improve our understanding of the region’s tectonic and magmatic history. The survey is funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resource Initiative and is designed to meet complementary needs related to geologic mapping and mineral resource research. The survey design is coordinated with the Nebraska Conservation and Survey Division and the Kansas Geological Survey to optimize coverage of geologic features of greatest interest. The planned survey includes magnetic and radiometric data collected from a fixed-wing aircraft along flight lines spaced 400 m. The nominal terrain clearance is 100 m. Mineral systems of interest in the survey area include Magmatic REE and Mafic Magmatic. Potential critical mineral commodities include Nb, REEs, Sc, Ti, V, PGE, and Ni. There is additional potential for Cu.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric survey over the Ozark Plateau physiographic region, spanning parts of Missouri, Kansas, Oklahoma, and Arkansas
abstract: A high-resolution airborne magnetic and radiometric survey is planned for the fall of 2024 to spring of 2025 over the Ozark Plateau physiographic region, spanning parts of Missouri, Kansas, Oklahoma, and Arkansas. The Earth Mapping Resources Initiative (EMRI) will fund this survey to meet geologic mapping and mineral resource research needs. By imaging surface and concealed geology, lithology, and structure, data from this survey will facilitate geologic mapping related to mineral resource potential within the Paleozoic sedimentary section, as well as provide insight into the underlying structures of the Precambrian crystalline basement. This region is renowned for its world-class Mississippi Valley-type (MVT) deposits and has an extensive mining history associated with a variety of mineral commodities. The Ozark Plateau survey will connect adjacent high-resolution airborne magnetic and radiometric surveys from Southeast Missouri and Western Illinois (2018-2019), Central Arkansas-Southern Missouri (Arkoma basin, 2022-2023), and Northwest Arkansas (2019-2020) to provide continuous high-resolution coverage across the southern Midcontinent. To optimize investigation of significant geologic features, the survey will collect data along N-S flight lines spaced 300 m apart at a nominal elevation of 100 m above ground using multiple fixed-wing aircraft, with tie lines flown in an E-W direction at 3000-m intervals. This survey incorporates parts of at least eight EMRI focus areas: the Tri-State MVT District; the Central Missouri Barite-Lead District; the South-Central Iron Oxide-Apatite (IOA) – Iron Oxide-Copper-Gold (IOCG) Province; the Southeast Missouri Metallogenic Province; the Fayetteville Shale/Upper Mississippian (Serpukhovian) Phosphate; the Upper Devonian Phosphate; the Missouri Cheltenham Fireclay District; and the Pennsylvanian Phosphate, Black Shale, Underclay. Three mineral systems are present in the survey area, including basin-brine path, IOA-IOCG, and marine chemocline. Potential critical mineral commodities investigated include Al, Ba, Co, Cr, F, Ga, Ge, In, Li, Mn, Ni, Sn, V, Zn, platinum group elements (PGE), and rare earth elements (REE). Other mineral commodities include Ag, Au, Cd, Cu, Fe, Mo, Pb, Re, Se, Si, Sr, and U.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric survey over the northeastern portion of the Colorado Mineral Belt, north-central Colorado
abstract:
A high-resolution, airborne magnetic and radiometric survey over the northeastern portion of the Colorado Mineral Belt, north-central Colorado, is planned for acquisition as part of the USGS Earth Mapping Resource Initiative (Earth MRI). Airborne geophysical surveys covering the southwestern and central portions of the Colorado Mineral Belt are already in progress. The Colorado Mineral Belt is a broad, northeast-southwest trending alignment of historic mining districts that are best known for production of silver, gold, base metals, and molybdenum.  Nine mineral systems present in the northeastern portion of the belt attest to a more diverse potential:  Climax-type, alkalic porphyry, porphyry Cu-Mo-Au, magmatic REE, volcanogenic seafloor, basin brine path, chemical weather, Coeur d’Alene-type uranium, and metamorphic.  Known occurrences of critical minerals include beryllium, bismuth, fluorspar, graphic, manganese, REE, rhenium, tellurium, tin, tungsten, uranium, and zinc. The northeastern survey will cover a wide swath between Leadville and Boulder and extend north along the Colorado Front Range, including parts of Boulder, Clear Creek, Eagle, Gilpin, Jefferson, Lake, Larimer, and Summit Counties. The survey will use a helicopter to collect magnetic and radiometric data along east-west flight lines spaced 200 m apart.  Flights will be low (100 m above ground) except over populated areas, where clearances will be 300 m, as regulated by the FAA.  Areas with restricted airspace, such as Wilderness Areas, will be avoided. 
The airborne geophysical survey will provide valuable information about the geology below and at the ground surface.  This new information, along with geologic mapping by the Colorado Geological Survey and USGS, will lead to a better understanding of the fundamental geologic framework underpinning the Colorado Mineral Belt.  The new understanding should shed light on how multiple mineralizing events concentrate in one region over millions of years, help industry determine the potential for undiscovered critical minerals, and inform many other applications, such as aquifer mapping and land use planning.

pdescrip: Airborne electromagnetic survey covering a northeast-trending structural corridor stretching from the Cheyenne Belt in Wyoming through to the Black Hills in South Dakota
abstract: This regional airborne electromagnetic survey is planned for acquisition over multiple years and is focused on a northeast-trending structural corridor stretching from the Cheyenne Belt in Wyoming through to the Black Hills in South Dakota. The survey is funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resources Initiative and is designed to meet complementary needs related to mineral resource assessments and regional geologic framework and mapping studies to be conducted by the USGS and state geologic surveys. The 1.78–1.74 Ga Cheyenne Belt is a tectonic suture juxtaposing Archean rocks of the Wyoming Province to the north against a range of Paleoproterozoic arcs, supracrustal rocks, and Mesoproterozoic intrusions to the south. The Black Hills region has been subjected to multiple tectonic events from 1.89–1.71 Ga, as revealed through magmatic, metamorphic, and structural overprints. Due to the amalgamated geologic structure along these sutures, the region is prospective for at least 9 major mineral systems and a number of deposits with known critical mineral resources fall within the survey area.  Much of the area is prospective for mineralization associated with mafic magmatic, volcanogenic seafloor, and porphyry mineral systems. The planned survey will include time-domain electromagnetic and magnetic data collected from a helicopter-towed system. Flight lines will be oriented perpendicular to major structural trends with a line spacing of 1-2 km. Flight lines will be flown with a system terrain clearance of 30-60 m. Expected depth of penetration is between 300 m and 500 m and with resolution as shallow as 5-10 m depth. The data to be collected, by mapping faults and basin geometry, are further relevant to hydrologic modeling, oil and gas exploration, uranium exploration, and carbon capture and sequestration.

pdescrip: Airborne electromagnetic survey near Dubuque, Iowa and spanning portions of Illinois, Iowa and Wisconsin
abstract: This focused airborne electromagnetic (AEM) survey is planned for acquisition in 2024 over an area near Dubuque, Iowa and spanning portions of Illinois, Iowa and Wisconsin. The survey is funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resources Initiative and is designed to meet complementary needs related to mineral resource assessments and regional geologic framework and mapping studies to be conducted by the USGS and state geologic surveys. The survey area includes a Rare-Earth Element (REE)-bearing phosphate horizon near the base of the upper Ordovician Maquoketa shale. The specific location is of particular interest due to the nexus of local enrichment in REE concentrations, increased thickness of the phosphate horizon, and thin cover sequence. The survey area also overlaps with parts of the Upper Mississippi Valley mineral district known to host zinc-lead Mississippi-Valley-Type mineralization within limestone of the Ordovician Galena Group. The primary goals of the AEM survey are to delineate the thickness of unconsolidated Quaternary sediments, the spatial distribution of the Maquoketa shale, the thickness of the Maquoketa shale where preserved, and the topography on the top of the Galena Group limestone. The planned survey will include frequency-domain electromagnetic and magnetic data collected from a helicopter-towed system. Flight lines will be flown with a nominal line spacing of 500 m and a system terrain clearance of 30-60 m. Flight lines orientation will be variable to avoid nearby urban infrastructure. Expected depth of penetration is between 60 and 100 m and with resolution as shallow as 1-2 m depth. The data to be collected are further relevant to water resource investigations and surficial mapping studies.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric survey in North American cordilleran alkaline-igneous belt in west Texas
abstract:
The North American cordilleran alkaline-igneous belt (NACAIB) extends southward from southeastern Alaska along the eastern edge of the Rocky Mountains and Basin and Range Provinces into northeastern Mexico. The unique igneous rocks that make up this belt have high concentrations of rare earth elements (REE), fluorine, zirconium, tellurium, gallium, other critical minerals, gold, and uranium. 
The Texas Alkaline Igneous Belt aeromagnetic/aeroradiometric survey will cover over 28,700 square kilometers (over 11,000 square miles) within the NACAIB in west Texas. This airborne survey will include over 158,000 flightline kilometers (over 98,000 flightline miles) of passively acquired Earth’s magnetic field and radiometric data with a flightline spacing of 200 meters draped at approximately 120 meters above the ground. The survey will border the existing Trans-Pecos aeromagnetic/aeroradiometric survey to the north and includes several important known mineral occurrences including Cave Peak (Mo), the Quitman Mountains caldera, and other Laramide calderas. The Round Top REE occurrence lies just to the north of the new survey in the southern portion of the Trans-Pecos survey. In addition, the survey area lies within the Basin and Range geologic province providing concealed terrane that is virtually unexplored. The current geophysical data in the region is inadequate and these new data will add tremendously to our geologic knowledge, help understand existing mineral occurrences and deposits, and possibly help define unknown mineral occurrences.

pdescrip: Airborne electromagnetic survey in portions of the Basin and Range province in Nevada and smaller extents in Oregon and California
abstract: This regional airborne electromagnetic survey is a continuation of data acquisition that began in 2022 and 2023 covering portions of the Basin and Range province in Nevada and smaller extents in Oregon and California. The survey is funded by the USGS Earth Mapping Resources Initiative and is designed to meet complementary needs related to mineral resource assessments as well as geologic framework and geologic mapping studies being conducted by the USGS and the Nevada Bureau of Mines and Geology.  The Basin and Range province hosts a diversity of base, precious, and critical mineral resources. The survey is designed to encompass regional-scale lacustrine evaporite mineral system focus areas, as well as basin brine, porphyry Cu-Mo-Au, Climax type, Carlin type, chemical weathering, IOA-IOGC, magmatic, and metamorphic mineral systems. Potential critical mineral commodities include lithium, antimony, barite, beryllium, fluorite, graphite, tin, tungsten, rare earth elements, uranium, and vanadium.  The survey will also provide subsurface context on the numerous fault systems that dissect the region and exert a primary control on fluid and heat flow and certain types of mineralization. The airborne survey includes time-domain electromagnetic and magnetic data to be collected from a helicopter-towed system. Flight lines will be oriented perpendicular to the major structural trends with a nominal spacing of 5 km. Flight lines will be flown with a target system terrain clearance of 30-60 m. Expected depth of penetration is between 300 and 500 m and with resolution as shallow as 10 m depth. These data may be further relevant to water and geothermal resource investigations as well as earthquake and volcanic hazards.

pdescrip: Airborne electromagnetic and magnetic survey covering parts of the western Mojave Desert of California
abstract: An airborne electromagnetic and magnetic survey, funded through the U.S. Geological Survey’s Earth Mapping Resource Initiative, is planned in 2024 covering parts of the western Mojave Desert of California.  The hydrogeologically closed basins of the western Mojave Desert host lacustrine and marine evaporite mineral systems prospective for critical mineral commodities including lithium, potash, tungsten, and magnesium. The survey area includes the Hector Mine region, the type locality of hectorite, one of the primary clay minerals prospective for lithium resources. These geophysical data will improve our understanding of the geologic setting surrounding these proven clay deposits, which are structurally deformed and obscured by volcanic deposits and basin fill sediments. These new data will also help define the geometry of brine-hosting basins, the extent of volcanic rocks buried by basin fill sediments, and the geometry of faults and other concealed structures. The airborne survey includes time-domain electromagnetic and magnetic data to be collected from a helicopter-towed system. Flight lines will be oriented perpendicular to the major structural trends with a nominal spacing of 1 km. Flight lines will be flown with a target system terrain clearance of 30-60 m. Expected depth of penetration is between 300 and 500 m and with resolution as shallow as 10 m depth. This airborne geophysical survey will support co-located geologic mapping and geochemical studies being conducted by the California Geological Survey and will facilitate a better understanding of the subsurface geologic framework of this structurally complex region. These data may be further relevant to water, hazard, and geothermal resource investigations.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric survey covering parts of North Carolina, Virginia, and West Virginia
abstract: This high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey covering parts of North Carolina, Virginia, and West Virginia is planned for 2025 through funding from the USGS Earth Mapping Resource Initiative. This survey will provide continuous coverage over a large area from Virginia to South Carolina, also serving as a “bridge” to a survey north to Pennsylvania. The survey area is the site of over 15 focus areas that are prospective for critical mineral resources within Piedmont and Blue Ridge crystalline rock, Valley and Ridge sedimentary rock, and Atlantic Coastal Plain sediments. Focus areas include Ti-Zr-REE placer deposits within Atlantic Coastal Plain sediments, porphyry deposits of the Hamme Tungsten District, porphyry tin within several pegmatite zones (e.g. Little Friar Mountain), volcanogenic seafloor deposits of both the Piedmont Chopawamsic Formation and the eastern Blue Ridge, North Carolina fireclays, barite from basin brines, MVT deposits in the Timberville mining district, several Triassic rift basins, several areas with ultramafic rocks containing chromite and/or magnesium, supergene manganese and cobalt within the Valley and Ridge,  and potential for REEs in both phosphates and regolith. The coverage area also includes young (Eocene) volcanic rocks within the Valley and Ridge. With no plate tectonic activity at the time of emplacement, the processes that created these rocks are poorly understood and widely debated.  Data will be collected along northwest-southeast flight lines spaced 250 m at a nominal height of 90 m above ground. Its longest dimensions are roughly 350 km northwest-southeast and 245 km northeast-southwest. The survey will slightly overlap two magnetic/radiometric surveys flown in South Carolina, one over Charleston (2019) and one along the Fall Zone (2020) to image Ti-Zr-REE placer deposits and assist with the mapping of subsurface faults for earthquake hazard studies. Mineral systems to be covered include chemical weathering, IOA-IOCG, mafic magmatic, meteoritic recharge, placer, porphyry, volcanogenic seafloor deposits. The critical mineral commodities to be investigated include antimony, arsenic, barite, beryllium, bismuth, cesium, chromium, cobalt, gallium, germanium, indium, lithium, manganese, nickel, niobium, platinum group elements (PGE), rare earth elements, scandium, tantalum, tellurium, tin, titanium, tungsten, zinc, and zirconium. Other commodities include clay, copper, gold, iron, kaolin, lead, molybdenum, selenium, serpentine, silver, talc, uranium, and yttrium.

pdescrip: Airborne magnetic and radiometric survey covering parts of Connecticut, Massachusetts, New Hampshire, Rhode Island, and Vermont
abstract: This high-resolution magnetic and radiometric survey covering parts of Connecticut, Massachusetts, New Hampshire, Rhode Island, and Vermont is planned for 2024-2025 through funding from the USGS Earth Mapping Resource Initiative. This five-state survey, covering the Connecticut River Valley, Hartford Basin, and surrounding rocks, covers multiple mineral systems. The focus areas include mafic magmatic and basin brine deposits of the Central Atlantic Magmatic Province, cobalt, nickel, and graphite within the Brimfield Schist, several areas with volcanogenic seafloor rocks, porphyry tin and lithium in pegmatites (mined in the 19th century), and chromite in ultramafic rocks. The survey will also have several hazard-related applications. The new data will provide important information regarding the potential extent of pyrrhotite-bearing sulfides; concrete batched from aggregate containing pyrrhotite has led to severe damage to thousands of homes in the region. The Connecticut River Valley is also an intraplate seismic zone, with a history of multiple earthquake swarms of magnitude 3.5 or lower. The key critical mineral commodities include barite, beryllium, cobalt, fluorspar, graphite, lithium, manganese, and zinc; other commodities of interest include antimony, arsenic, bismuth, cesium, chromium, gallium, germanium, indium, magnesium, nickel, niobium, platinum group elements (PGE), rare earth elements, scandium, tantalum, tellurium, tin, titanium, tungsten, and zirconium. Other commodities include copper, feldspar, gold, iron, lead, mica, selenium, serpentine, silver, and talc.

pdescrip: Geologic Mapping of alkaline intrusions in the Eastern Tanacross region for characterization of REE-U-Th mineralization
abstract: The Alaska Division of Geological & Geophysical Surveys (DGGS) is mapping the Eastern Tanacross region of the Yukon-Tanana Upland Earth MRI focus area to investigate carbonatite and peralkaline igneous intrusions for potential REE, U, Th, Re, Cu, Mo, Au, and Sn mineral commodities. Polydeformed Paleozoic metamorphic rocks in this region have been episodically affected by rift- and arc- related magmatism from the Devonian through the Quaternary, resulting in the formation of both metavolcanic and metaplutonic rocks. A regional belt of alkaline igneous rocks in the area host REE-U-Th mineralization and are the primary target for mapping within the region. Mapping will be conducted throughout ten 1:63,360 scale quadrangles at a scale of 1:100,000 and will be supported by in-field XRF analysis, whole rock geochemistry, petrography, and radiometric dating.

pdescrip: Geologic Mapping of Regolith-Hosted REE Deposits in the Alabama Piedmont
abstract: The Geological Survey of Alabama (GSA) is mapping a portion of the Regolith-Hosted REE deposits of the SE Earth MRI focus area in east-central Alabama to investigate potential REE mineral commodities. The Piedmont province of the southern Appalachian orogen comprises polydeformed metasedimentary and metavolcanics rocks that were formed and emplaced throughout the Paleozoic. Peralkaline, metaluminous, and peraluminous granitoids intrude the Paleozoic metamorphics and exhibit both light- and heavy-REE enrichment. Intense weathering and slow erosion of these granitoids has resulted in the development of granite-derived packages of regolith and have a high potential for hosting REEs. Mapping will take place in four 7.5-minute quadrangles and will be focused on identifying areas where thick layers of in situ REE-bearing regolith have accumulated. Mapping will be supported by bulk REE geochemical concentration analysis of samples from those areas.

pdescrip: HREE- and manganese-enriched sedimentary marine phosphate deposits of Arkansas
abstract: The Arkansas Geological Survey (AGS) mapped a portion of the phosphate deposits of Arkansas Earth MRI focus area to investigate potential REE, Ni, Co, P, and Mn mineral commodities. The focus area was within in the Ozark Plateaus of north-central Arkansas, which comprised Upper Ordovician to Mississippian marine sedimentary formations. Mapping was focused on the Cason Shale and its bounding units to determine the thickness, composition, and grade and mineralized REE- and manganese-enriched phosphate deposits. The AGS produced new 2-D geologic framework maps spanning nine 7.5-minute quadrangles to show the distribution of sedimentary units that are potentially enriched in these resources. Mapping was supported by geochemical analysis of samples collected from the field area.

pdescrip: Geologic Mapping in the Mountain Pass, California Focus Area
abstract: The California Geological Survey (CGS) is mapping a portion of the Mountain Pass Earth MRI focus area to investigate carbonatite and alkaline igneous intrusions for potential REE mineral commodities. The project area is within the New York Mountains, a range comprising Precambrian and Cambrian metasediments that are intruded by Mesozoic igneous rocks and sporadically overlain by Tertiary volcanics. Of the Mesozoic intrusions, carbonatites host Rare Earth Elements (REE) that have been historically mined at the nearby Mountain Pass mining operation. Recent mapping has identified carbonatites in the New York Mountains that are lithologically similar to those at the Mountain Pass REE mining operation. Mapping will focus on identifying REE-enriched carbonatite terranes within four 7.5-minute quadrangles and will be conducted at a scale of 1:24,000.

pdescrip: Geologic Mapping of Mesoproterozoic stratigraphy along the Idaho Cobalt Belt
abstract: The Idaho Geological Survey (IGS) is mapping a portion of the Idaho Cobalt Belt Earth MRI focus area located in east-central Idaho to investigate magmatic hydrothermal deposits for potential Fe, Co, Cu, Au, Bi, Y, and REE mineral commodities. The project area is within the northern end of the Basin and Range, where Proterozoic metasedimentary rocks have been periodically intruded by Proterozoic to Tertiary-aged granites. Proterozoic metasedimentary rocks within the Idaho Cobalt Belt (ICB) have been found to contain strata-bound Fe-Co-Cu-Au-Bi-Y-REE deposits with a history of hydrothermal mineralization and late epigenetic vein emplacement. Additionally, Paleozoic intrusions with anomalous concentrations of REEs have been discovered in this region. The IGS will conduct detailed geologic mapping of the Mesoproterozoic stratigraphy within three 7.5' quadrangles at 1:24,000 scale to better characterize strata-bound cobalt-copper mineralization and identify potentially REE-rich intrusive rocks within the ICB. The IGS will also compile data from preexisting 1:16,000 scale mapping efforts in the Blackbird mine area of the ICB. Whole rock geochemical analysis will be performed on samples collected from the study area to support mapping.

pdescrip: Geologic Mapping in the Hicks Dome, Illinois Focus Area
abstract: The Illinois State Geological Survey (ISGS) mapped a portion of the Hicks Dome Earth MRI focus area to investigate a mineralized peralkaline breccia complex for potential REE, fluorite, barite, Th, Y, Nb, Be, and Ti mineral commodities. The Hicks Dome is within the Illinois-Kentucky Flourspar District (IKFD), an area that has historically been mined for critical fluorite and barite. Mineralized zones of fluorite and barite within this region are spatially associated with ultramafic alkaline igneous rocks that intrude Paleozoic sedimentary strata and are likely due to epithermal fluid mixing associated with igneous activity. The rocks at Hicks Dome were of interest due to having a high concentration of REE minerals in addition to fluorite and barite. In order to characterize the REE potential within this area, the ISGS conducted three mapping projects. The Hicks Dome area was mapped at 1:12,000 scale, the Illinois portion of the IKFD was mapped at 1:16,000 scale, and a compilation map of 38 7.5-minute quadrangles spanning southern Illinois was developed to accompany the Earth MRI geophysical survey being conducted in this area. The compilation map combined data from new and preexisting 1:24,000 scale bedrock mapping. Samples were collected throughout the IKFD and analyzed for REEs.

pdescrip: Kentucky Geologic Data for the Hicks Dome Regional EarthMRI Priority Project
abstract: The Kentucky Geological Survey (KGS) conducted a data compilation and sample analysis project on data from the Hicks Dome Earth MRI focus area to investigate potential REE, fluorite, barite, Th, Y, Nb, Be, and Ti mineral commodities. This focus area straddles the Kentucky-Illinois border, and this project was conducted with the Illinois State Geological Survey. The Hicks Dome is within the Illinois-Kentucky Flourspar District (IKFD), an area that has historically been mined for critical fluorite and barite. Mineralization zones of fluorite and barite within this region are spatially associated with ultramafic alkaline igneous rocks that intrude Paleozoic sedimentary strata and are likely due to epithermal fluid mixing associated with igneous activity. The rocks at Hicks Dome were of interest due to having a high concentration of REE minerals in addition to fluorite and barite. The KGS was involved in compiling preexisting 1:24,000 scale geologic map data that is from the Kentucky portion of the IKFD. Additionally, the KGS has at least 260 rock cores from the Kentucky portion of the IKFD, many of which are inconsistently documented or not documented at all. These cores were thoroughly and consistently catalogued to better our understanding of mineral distribution in the IKFD.

pdescrip: NeoProterozoic Regolith and Rare Earth Elements of the Maryland Blue Ridge
abstract: The Maryland Geological Survey (MGS), in parternship with the USGS, is mapping a portion of the Regolith-Hosted REE deposits of the SE Earth MRI focus area located in central Maryland. Mapping will take place in the Blue Ridge Province of western Frederick County where Neoproterozic basement, consisting of Grenville continental gneisses and basic and felsic igneous extrusive rocks, is covered by a thick layer of regolith. Rhyolitic veins and granitic intrusives have potential for elevated REE abundances and are widely distributed in the area. Surficial mapping of regolith will be conducted in conjunction with collection of 100 regolith cores for REE geochemical analysis to characterize the distribution of granite-derived regolith hosted REE deposits. Mapping will take place over six 7.5-minute quadrangles at 1:24,000 scale.

pdescrip: Bedrock Geologic Mapping for potential Iron Oxide-Apatite and Iron Oxide Copper-Gold deposits in the Upper Peninsula
abstract: The Michigan Geological Survey (MGS) is mapping Iron Oxide-Apatite (IOA) and Iron Oxide-Copper-Gold (IOCG) deposits in the Dickinson County, Michigan Earth MRI focus area to investigate for potential REE, P, Ag, U, Co, Fe, Cu, and Au critical mineral commodities. Paleoproterozoic metasediments containing IOA and IOCG deposits are constrained to narrow E-W trending troughs bounded by Archean metamorphic rocks and Paleoproterozoic igneous terranes. Bedrock mapping will occur throughout seven 7.5-minute quads at 1:24,000 scale to characterize the distribution of IOA and IOCG deposits within these troughs. Geochemical analyses of samples collected from the mapping area will be used to assess the critical mineral potential of Dickinson County.

pdescrip: Geologic Mapping in the Placer Ti-Zr-REE Deposits, North Carolina Focus Area
abstract: The North Carolina Geological Survey (NCGS) is mapping a portion of the Placer Ti-Zr-REE Deposits Earth MRI Focus Area within the Coastal Plain of North Carolina to investigate for REE, Ti, and Zr critical mineral commodities. Mapping is focused on three marine terraces of the Plio-Pleistocene Atlantic Coastal Plain - the Sunderland, Coharie, and Brandywine terrace. Terrace settings are commonly associated with coastal depositional systems and facies containing REEs. Correlatives of these terraces to the south have been found to contain a 20 m thick (4% ore grade) layer of heavy mineral sands. This project will combine 1:24,000 scale surficial mapping within two 7.5' minute quadrangles with widespread coring and geochemical analysis to understand the vertical and lateral extent of Ti-Zr-REE-bearing placer deposits in the Coastal Plain.

pdescrip: Geologic Mapping in the Mountain Pass REE Focus Area
abstract: The Nevada Bureau of Mines and Geology (NBMG) is mapping within the Mountain Pass Earth MRI Focus area to investigate carbonatite and alkaline igneous intrusions for REE mineral commodities. This portion of the Mountain Pass focus is within the Mojave Desert of Southern Nevada. Bedrock is predominantly early Proterozoic gneisses and migmatites that were intruded by several suites of Proterozoic granitoids. Carbonatite intrusions associated with the youngest of the Proterozoic granitoids serve as the main host of REE mineralization. Digitization of previous 1:50,000 scale mapping efforts in the area will be conducted along with new 1:24,000 scale mapping in two 7.5-minute quadrangles with potential REE-bearing carbonatite/alkaline intrusions. Key lithologies from the mapping area will be sampled for geochemical analysis.

pdescrip: Rare Earth Elements (REE) deposits in the Gallinas Mountains, Lincoln and Torrance Counties, central New Mexico
abstract: The New Mexico Bureau of Geology and Mineral Resources (NMBGMR) mapped within the Gallinas Mountains Earth MRI Focus Area to investigate alkaline igneous rocks, mineralized breccia pipes, and epithermal REE-Th-U veins for potential REE, U, Th, Nb, Ga, Zr, Hf, Y, Te, and Au mineral commodities. The Gallinas Mountains mining district in central New Mexico is located where a series of alkaline igneous bodies intrude Permian sedimentary rocks. Th-REE-flourite mineralization associated with trachyte/syenite igneous bodies occurs in epithermal veins and along breccia pipes. Iron skarn deposits occur at the top and edges of the trachyte/syenite. Detailed 1:24,000 scale geologic mapping throughout two 7.5-minute quadrangles were paired with detailed geochemical analyses of samples from this region to better understand the mineralogy and chemical evolution of the mineral deposits.

pdescrip: Geologic Mapping and in the Placer Deposits, South Carolina Focus Area
abstract: The South Carolina Geological Survey (SCGS) is mapping within the Placer Deposits Earth MRI Focus Area to investigate Upper Coastal Plain placer deposits for REE, Zr, and monazite critical mineral commodities. Geophysical surveys show a large aero-radiometric anomaly directly northwest of the Pliocene Orangeburg paleoshoreline, suggesting the presence of REE- and heavy mineral-bearing placer deposits. New 1:24K surficial mapping will span four 7.5-minute quadrangles that fall within the large aero-radiometric anomaly. New surficial maps will be compiled with preexisting surficial maps of six neighboring quadrangles. Eighty sediment cores will be drilled and logged within the new mapping areas, and geochemical analyses will be conducted on samples from each core.

pdescrip: Geologic Mapping in the Placer Ti-Zr-REE deposits, Virginia Focus Area
abstract: The Virginia Division of Geology and Mineral Resources (DGMR) mapped a portion of the Placer Ti-Zr-REE deposits Earth MRI focus area within the Virginia Coastal Plain to investigate the potential for REE, Th, Y, Nb, U, and Zr critical mineral commodities. Ilmenite-rutile-leucoxene-zircon mineral concentrates are currently produced by Iluka Resources LLC from Pliocene-age beach sand placer deposits within the area. Recent airborne geophysical surveys conducted in this area have shown four positive radiometric anomalies within the coastal plain, suggesting the presence of heavy mineral deposits. 1:100,000-scale surficial mapping of these four target areas spanned portions of twenty-two 7.5-minute quadrangles. Twenty-five cores were collected throughout the study area, logged, and added to the Virginia Borehole Database.

pdescrip: The Alaska DGGS will map in the Western Tanacross region to investigate magmatic-hydrothermal systems and metamorphic- and deformation-related systems for As, Bi, Co, In, LREE, Re, Sn, Te, PGE, and W.
abstract: The Alaska Division of Geological and Geophysical Surveys is mapping the Western Tanacross region of the Yukon-Tanana Upland Earth MRI focus area to investigate magmatic-hydrothermal systems and metamorphic- and deformation-related systems for As, Bi, Co, In, LREE, Re, Sn, Te, PGE, and W critical mineral commodities. Poly-deformed Paleozoic metamorphic rocks in this region have been episodically affected by rift- and arc-related magmatism from the Devonian through the Quaternary, resulting in the formation of metavolcanic and metaplutonic rocks. Known mineral systems within the proposed map area include quartz-molybdenite veins, tungsten-Cu-Mo skarns, porphyry molybdenum-tungsten, porphyry copper-molybdenum, simple antimony-gold veins, and potentially epithermal gold-silver. Of particular interest is the Mount Harper area, which has high potential and high certainty for granite-hosted tin mineralization, and associated critical minerals such as fluorspar, In, Mo, Ta, and W. Fourteen 1:63,360 scale quadrangles will be mapped at a scale of 1:100,000, five of which will be mapped in their entirety and nine of which will be mapped partially. Mapping will be supported by in-field XRF analysis, whole-rock geochemistry, ore geochemistry, petrography, and radiometric dating.

pdescrip: The AZGS will map in the Big Sandy Valley to determine the extent of Li-bearing sedimentary deposits and identify source rocks for Li-mineralization.
abstract: The Arizona Geological Survey is mapping in the Big Sandy Valley area of western Arizona to investigate the geologic setting lithium-bearing mineral systems hosted in sedimentary deposits of the Burro Creek area. Sedimentation of these deposits was contemporaneous to the late Miocene-Pliocene volcanism of the adjacent Kaiser Spring volcanic field and rhyolitic lavas of the Kaiser Spring volcanic field may be the source of lithium within the Burro Creek deposit. Mapping will be conducted throughout portions of seven 7.5-minute quadrangles at a scale of 1:24,000 and will be focused on identifying the extent of the lithium-bearing sedimentary deposits and identifying the potential source rocks for mineralization. Mapping will be supported by whole-rock geochemical analyses of samples to characterize the various geologic units.

pdescrip: The CGS will conduct new mapping and map compilation of the Wet Mountains to identify REE, Nb, Ta, Be, Bi, and Sr critical mineral commodities in Cambrian alkalic complexes throughout the area.
abstract: The Colorado Geological Survey is conducting new mapping and map compilation throughout the Wet Mountains of south-central Colorado to investigate the potential rare earth element (REE) and critical mineral commodities of the area. Proterozoic gneisses and granitoids were intruded by Cambrian alkalic complexes. The complexes of interest, the McClure Mountain, Gem Park, and Democrat Creek complexes, consist of carbonatites, lamprophytes, syenites, and mafic and ultramafic rocks. Rare earth elements, Nb, Ta, Be, and Bi have been found throughout the project area. As well, strontium may also be present within the Democrat Creek complex. New 1:50,000-scale mapping will occur throughout portions of five 7.5-minute quadrangles, existing 1:62,500 scale maps of the project area will be rescaled to 1:50,000, and the three complexes of interest will be mapped at 1:24,000 scale. Mapping will be supported by whole-rock geochemical analyses of samples collected from the project area.

pdescrip: The FGS will conduct new surficial mapping of the Trail Ridge placer deposits to understand the distribution of REEs and titanium-bearing oxides in the project area. Mapping will be supported by coring and cross section construction.
abstract: The Florida Geological Survey (FGS) is mapping northeastern Florida to investigate heavy-mineral placer deposits for potential rare earth element (REE) and other critical mineral resources. The surficial geology of the project area is comprised of Miocene to Pliocene sandy, mixed carbonates and sandy shell beds that are overlain by a complex mixture of Pliocene to Pleistocene siliciclastic units containing heavy mineral placer deposits. The Trail Ridge is a north-trending geomorphic feature that extends over 200 km and comprises quartz sand with locally high concentrations of REEs and titanium oxide minerals (ilmenite, leucoxene, and rutile). Surficial mapping at a scale of 1:24,000 will be conducted throughout portions of three 1:100,000-scale quadrangles. The FGS will retrieve up to 250 meters of core throughout the project area to support the accurate construction of detailed cross sections. Intervals of core will be selected for QEMSCAN, XRF, ICP-OEW-MS, and U-Pb geochronology to evaluate bulk geochemistry, major oxides present, trace elements present, and age of heavy minerals.

pdescrip: The MGS will conduct new mapping of the Oxford County Pegmatite Field to map the distribution and characteristics of Li-, Ce-, Nb-, Ta-, and Be-bearing pegmatites.
abstract: The Maine Geological Survey is mapping in Oxford County of western Maine to investigate pegmatites for their potential for hosting critical mineral commodities. Silurian to Devonian-aged metasedimentary bedrock in this region was intruded by Devonian to Carboniferous plutons and granitoids. The Oxford County pegmatite field is located along the northern and eastern edges of the Devonian-Carboniferous Sebago Batholith and contain significant concentrations of Li-, Ce-, Nb-, Ta-, and Be-bearing minerals. 1:24,000-scale geologic mapping will be conducted throughout three 7.5-minute quadrangles to map out the distribution and characteristics of the pegmatites and to better understand the origin of lithium-cesium-tantalum pegmatites. Mapping will be supported by in-field XRF analysis, bulk geochemical analyses of selected samples, and both igneous and detrital zircon geochronology.

pdescrip: The MGS will conduct new mapping of the Fredericktown 7.5' quadrangle to aid development of geologic framework models for cobalt and REE mineralization of the St. Francois Mountains of southeastern Missouri.
abstract: In support of strategic mineral evaluations for cobalt and igneous-hosted rare earth elements (REEs), the Missouri Geological Survey will produce a bedrock geologic map of the Fredericktown 7.5-minute quadrangle to aid development of geologic framework models for cobalt and REE mineralization. The quadrangle is in the eastern St. Francois Mountains and contains a portion of the cobalt-rich Fredericktown-Mine La Motte subdistrict of the Southeast Missouri Lead District. New data and mapping also will be used to modify existing small-scale structure contour mapping of the top of the Mesoproterozoic, which locally hosts Fe, Cu, Ag, Pb, Zn, and REE deposits and prospects. The Southeast Missouri Lead District is a world-class Mississippi Valley-type (MVT) lead district with associated zinc, copper, silver, cobalt, and nickel mineralization in active and past-producing mines, making Missouri one of only nine states with potentially economically viable cobalt deposits. Missouri is also one of only 10 states with potentially viable REE deposits, which are hosted in the basement Mesoproterozoic rocks. New bedrock mapping, in conjunction with recent USGS airborne geophysical surveys, will support identification of cobalt, REE, and other critical mineral resources. The possible genetic links between Cambrian-hosted mineralization and carbonate facies, Mesoproterozoic basement, and structure are poorly understood in the Fredericktown-Mine La Motte subdistrict. The new mapping will aid in characterization of the Cambrian-age facies sedimentary rocks and underlying Mesoproterozoic igneous rocks, as well as aid in the identification of industrial mineral potential, and geologic hazard potential and environmental issues of the region.

pdescrip: The Montana Bureau of Mines and Geology (MBMG) is mapping the Elkhorn 7.5' quadrangle, which lies on the eastern flank of the Late Cretaceous Boulder Batholith and contains the Elkhorn Mountains Volcanics (EMV) magma system and associated mineral deposit
abstract: The Montana Bureau of Mines and Geology is mapping the Elkhorn 7.5-minute quadrangle, which lies on the eastern flank of the Late Cretaceous Boulder Batholith and contains part of the Elkhorn Mountains Volcanic (EMV) magma system. Rocks of this volcanic-plutonic magmatic system host the world‐class Butte porphyry Cu‐Mo deposits, which are overprinted by a vast system of zoned polymetallic Cu‐Zn‐Pb‐Mn‐Ag lode veins. The eastern flank of the Boulder Batholith–EMV magma system is well‐known for historic production of magmatic hydrothermal precious‐ and base‐metal ore deposits at Elkhorn and the Golden Sunlight mine. Elkhorn mines produced ore from deposit types that included Ag, Pb, Zn (Au) carbonate replacements, Fe‐oxide skarns, sulfide‐rich and sulfide poor Au‐Bi skarns, tourmaline‐bearing breccia pipes and an unmined porphyry Mo (Cu) system. Earth‐MRI Phase II critical metals, Platinum Group Elements (PGE's) and tungsten (W), are associated with the types of magmatic hydrothermal base metal replacement/skarns and porphyry Au mineral systems that are known at Elkhorn. The purpose of the mapping is to help characterize the potential for critical metal occurrences in the region.

pdescrip: The New Mexico Bureau of Geology and Mineral Resources will map in the Cornudas Mountains in Trans-Pecos area, southern New Mexico in a coordinated effort between the Texas Bureau of Economic Geology and USGS to characterize the evolution and mineral res
abstract: The New Mexico Bureau of Geology and Mineral Resources will map in the Cornudas Mountains in Trans-Pecos Tertiary alkaline magmatic province of southern New Mexico. The Cornudas Mountains are located in southern Otero County, New Mexico and northern Huspeth County, Texas. The igneous rocks in the Cornudas Mountains were emplaced at or during the beginning of Rio Grande rift extension (~33-36 Ma) and consist of ten larger sills, plugs, and laccoliths and smaller dikes and plugs, which intrude relatively flat-lying limestones and other sedimentary rocks of the Permian Hueco Limestone and Bone Spring Limestone. Compositionally, the igneous rocks in the Cornudas Mountains are classified as alkaline, ferroan, metaluminous to peralkaline and plot as A-type, within-plate granites. The alkaline igneous rocks exhibit typical light-REE-enriched chondrite-normalized patterns. The mapping will help define the geologic framework for areas with potential economic deposits of REE-Zr-Nb (±U, Be) epithermal veins and breccia deposits. In addition, the area has potential of several other types of mineral deposits, including nepheline syenite for ceramics and sand blasting, and Au, Ag, Be, Nb, and U veins. One goal of the effort is to better characterize the igneous system and associated mineralization and alteration regionally in order to assess the economic potential throughout the Cornudas Mountains. This is a coordinated project being conducted in concert with the Texas Bureau of Economic Geology, which is mapping the Cornudas Mountain in Texas, and the USGS Mineral Resources Program, which is acquiring a new airborne magnetic and radiometric survey of the region, all of which are supported by Earth MRI.

pdescrip: The Pennsylvania Geological Survey will conduct 1:24,000-scale bedrock geologic mapping in Mesozoic Basin in Adams County in southcentral Pennsylvania to help understand the cobalt and other mineral resource endowment of the region.
abstract: Mesozoic rift basins in Pennsylvania are distinguished by numerous magnetite skarns where diabase intruded carbonate rocks of varying ages. Many of these deposits were mined as early as the colonial era, and two major mines, Cornwall and the Grace Mine, were in operation until the 1970s. In addition to iron, these two mines were the most important source of cobalt in the United States in the middle 20th century. The mines also produced Au, Ag, and Cu from accessory chalcopyrite. Cobalt was produced from accessory pyrite. In the middle 20th century there was a period of substantial exploration by private, government and academic organizations for additional mineral resources. The Pennsylvania Geological Survey will generate 1:24,000-scale bedrock geologic maps of the Biglerville, Arendtsville, Fairfield and Gettysburg quadrangles and the PA section of the Emmitsburg and Taneytown 7.5-minute quadrangles, which lie in the Mesozoic Basin in Adams County in south-central Pennsylvania.

pdescrip: Researchers from the Texas Bureau of Economic Geology will conduct 1:24,000-scale geologic mapping in two 7.5' quadrangles in the Cornudas Mountains of west Texas in a coordinated effort between the New Mexico Bureau of Geology and Mineral Resources and
abstract: Researchers from the Texas Bureau of Economic Geology will conduct 1:24,000-scale geologic mapping and geochemical sampling in the Sixteen Mountains and Cerro Diablo 7.5-minute quadrangles located in the Cornudas Mountains of west Texas. The area lies within the Trans-Pecos Tertiary alkaline magmatic province that extends northward into New Mexico. The Paleogene alkaline igneous rocks in Trans-Pecos Texas have the potential to host critical mineral resources including rare earth elements (REE), such as those of the Round Top REE deposit. This is a companion project being conducted in concert with the New Mexico Bureau of Geology and Mineral Resources, which is mapping the Cornudas Mountain in New Mexico, and the USGS Mineral Resources Program to conduct an airborne magnetic and radiometric survey of the region, all of which are supported by Earth MRI.

pdescrip: The Utah Geological Survey will map the southern extent of the Gold Hill mining district to better characterize the tungsten, precious- and base-metal resources in the district.
abstract: The Utah Geological Survey will map the Clifton 7.5-minute quadrangle, located in western Tooele County in the southern extent of the Gold Hill mining district. As well, a new enhanced digital version of the Gold Hill 7.5-minute quadrangle geologic map originally published by Robinson (1993) covering the northern extent of the Gold Hill mining district will be generated. The Gold Hill mining district, which has produced notable amounts of precious and base metals, is the largest historical producer of tungsten in Utah, and the most recent tungsten producer in the United States. In addition to tungsten, the Gold Hill district also produced significant amounts of the critical minerals As, Bi, Sb, V, fluorite, and Sn, and has been identified as prospective for Be and Re. Mapping of the Clifton quadrangle and digitization of the Gold Hill quadrangle addresses the Earth MRI goal of collecting geologic mapping and supporting datasets to improve understanding of an area with critical mineral resources and other economically valuable mineral resources. This project will not only provide value by supporting exploration for critical minerals but also enhance baseline geologic and structural understanding and coverage of a sparsely mapped area of Utah's part of the Basin and Range Province.

pdescrip: The Wyoming State Geological Survey is mapping two 7.5' quadrangles in the Central Laramie Range of southeast Wyoming to better understand the regional geology and mineral resource potential.
abstract: The Wyoming State Geological Survey mapped the King Mountain and Ragged Top Mountain 7.5-minute quadrangles in Albany County and Laramie counties and conducted extensive reconnaissance geochemistry and geochronology in the central Laramie Mountains in southeast Wyoming. The project area encompasses the Laramie Anorthosite Complex, has potential for REEs and other critical mineral resources (Ti, V, W, and Cr). The area also contains multiple greenstone belts, including the Elmers Rock Greenstone Belt (ERGB), which consists of intensely deformed metasedimentary and metavolcanic rocks that have been intruded by the Kennedy dike swarm. Komatiitic metabasalts, peridotite dikes, and altered ultramafic rocks are potential sources of Ni, Co, Cr, PGEs, and V. Graphitic schists are also common throughout the greenstone belts, including the Rabbit Creek graphitic schist deposit in Platte County. The study area also contains the Iron Mountain kimberlite district, which intruded into the Mule Creek lobe of the Sherman batholith. Polymetallic deposits bearing a variety of base and precious metals (Au, Ag, Cu, Mo) and critical minerals (Ba, Bi, Ni, Pd, Sb, Sn, W, Zn) are found in the southern part of the study area, but their extent and affinity are not well defined.

pdescrip: Taylor Mountain geologic mapping project
abstract: The Taylor Mountain area lies within the Yukon-Tanana Uplands of east-central Alaska. The Alaska Division of Geological & Geophysical Surveys (DGGS) is mapping parts of the Tanacross and Eagle quadrangles region of the Yukon-Tanana Upland (YTU) Earth MRI focus area. This mapping will better characterize known alkaline intrusions and associated REE-U-Th mineralization, evaluate the area for undiscovered alkaline or peralkaline intrusions among the area's numerous undifferentiated Mesozoic granitic bodies, and delineate the framework geology for areas known to host gold and other mineral resources. The YTU is prospective for rare-earth elements (REE), as well as many other critical and non-critical mineral resources in multiple mineral-system types. Known magmatic-hydrothermal systems contain significant critical-mineral potential for As, Bi, Co, In, LREE, Re, Sn, Te, PGE, and W. Metamorphic- and deformation-related systems have potential for As, Bi, Se, Sb, Te, and W, and graphite occurs in certain metamorphic units. Other critical minerals may be present but are unknown due to the lack of trace-element geologic mapping and geochemical data.

pdescrip: Geologic mapping of the Alabama graphite-vanadium belt, Chilton and Coosa Counties, Alabama
abstract: The Alabama graphite-vanadium belt encompasses an area of approximately 438 square miles (1,135 square kilometers) in the northern Piedmont province of east-central Alabama. This portion of the northern Alabama Piedmont is located within the eastern Blue Ridge terrane of the southernmost Appalachian orogen and is underlain by the upper amphibolite-facies Neoproterozoic-lower Paleozoic(?) graphite- and vanadium-bearing schists and phyllites of the Poe Bridge Mountain, Higgins Ferry, Hatchett Creek, and Wedowee Groups. Known commodities and deposit types are flake graphite and vanadium in carbonaceous phyllite and schists. The area also contains potential for Al, As, Be, Cu, Au, Li, Mn, REEs, Sn, Nb-Ta, Ti, U, and Zn.

pdescrip: Geologic mapping of the Hedges, Picacho Peak, and Ogilby 7.5'' quadrangles, Cargo Muchacho and part of the Chocolate Mountains area, Salton Sea Trough
abstract: The Salton Sea Trough in southern California is a large pull-apart basin located between active strands of the San Andreas Fault zone. The northwest-trending Chocolate Mountains bound the northeastern and eastern edge of the Salton Trough. The southern portion of the Chocolate Mountains is comprised of Tertiary nonmarine sedimentary rocks and a thick sequence of felsic Tertiary volcanic and volcaniclastic rocks. The Cargo Muchacho Mountains, likely an upthrown block related to faulting and uplift of the Chocolate Mountains, are comprised of Mesozoic granite with minor Tertiary basalt. The Salton Sea Geothermal Field (SSGF) is located on the southern shore of the Salton Sea. The SSGF hosts a hypersaline thermal-brine reservoir enriched in alkalic, base, and precious metals, including the critical mineral lithium. Modeling suggests that lithium in clay deposits within a closed-basin lacustrine mineral system lithium typically is locally sourced. In the SSGF, lithium sources were likely nearby felsic Tertiary volcanic rocks of the Chocolate Mountains.

pdescrip: New geologic mapping of the Blackbird Creek and Opal Lake 7.5' quadrangles, accompanied by sampling for geochemistry, Idaho cobalt belt
abstract: The mountainous Salmon area in the northern end of the Basin and Range portion of eastern Idaho is underlain by Proterozoic metasedimentary rocks of the Belt Supergroup, which were intruded by Proterozoic, Paleozoic, Cretaceous, and Tertiary granitic rocks. The Idaho cobalt belt district, southwest of the town of Salmon, contains numerous mines and prospects, including the Blackbird mine that sporadically produced Cu-Co ores over a 60-year span. Mineral deposits in the Idaho cobalt belt district are mostly strata-bound Fe-Co-Au-Bi-Y-REE deposits that have a complex history of multistage mineralization, mainly the result of magmatic-hydrothermal processes in a copper-gold (IOCG) mineral system. Deposit types include strata-bound sulfide and magnetite-rich lenses a well as discordant tourmaline-rich breccias. Copper-cobalt ores from Blackbird contain high REE and Y concentrations.

pdescrip: Westminster Terrane of the Mid-Atlantic Piedmont
abstract: The Westminster Terrane is a 3,456 square-kilometer, tectonostratigraphic unit in the Mid-Atlantic Piedmont comprised of a heterolithic suite of deep-water Neoproterozoic to early Cambrian greenschist-grade metamorphic volcanogenic and sedimentary rocks. The region has undergone several orogenic events that has resulted in telescoping of lithologies, creating complex lateral relationship of original facies. This makes traditional stratigraphic nomenclature difficult to apply and complicates geologic mapping of lithologic units. Mineralization in the Westminster Terrane is the result of a volcanogenic seafloor mineral system, with a broad range of potential copper-zinc sulfide mineralization carrying primarily copper, iron, zinc and magnesium, with minor barite, lead, antimony, cobalt, gold and silver. Recent quarrying in the Wakefield Marble has revealed evidence of mineralization in this area of sparse outcrop.

pdescrip: Mentor Complex Region
abstract: This project in northwestern Minnesota centers on the presumably Archean mafic-ultramafic Mentor Igneous Complex. The study area contains four major structural zones (Argyle, Fourtown, Marcoux, and unnamed) and three Archean geologic subprovinces (Wabigoon, Quetico, and Wawa-Abitibi) of the Superior Province of the Canadian Shield. These subprovinces are intruded by numerous syn- and post-orogenic intrusions of varying compositions, including the Mentor Igneous Complex. The Precambrian bedrock is obscured by thick horizons of saprolite, discontinuous Mesozoic strata, and glacial sediment that can be over 100m thick. Multiple mineral systems are likely present in the map area, including mafic-magmatic (Ni-Cu-Co-PGE sulfide, Fe-Ti-V oxide, chromite?); volcanogenic seafloor (Algoma iron, Cu-Zn sulfide, Zn-Cu sulfide, Polymetallic sulfide); IOA-IOCG (IOCG); Porphyry Sn (LCT Pegmatite); magmatic REE (carbonatite); and orogenic (graphite, gold). Potential commodities include C (graphite), Co, Cs, Cu, Fe, Li, Mg, Mn, Ni, PGM, REE, Sn, Ta, and Zn, along with industrial minerals olivine and plagioclase.

pdescrip: Weldon quadrangle, Atlantic Coastal Plain, North Carolina
abstract: The Atlantic Coastal plain is a relict Plio-Pleistocene landscape that consists of a series of progressively younger paleoshorelines created by glacio-fluvial transgressive-regressive changes in sea level. Marine terraces are dissected by rivers flowing into the ocean, creating incised valley with intervening ramps or interfluves that become progressively younger and step down in elevation towards the coast. The Weldon quadrangle, near the Coastal Plain-Piedmont boundary in northernmost North Carolina, contains the updip limit of the incised valley of the paleo-Roanoke River; scarps defining the valley walls truncate or join as many as three Pliocene to Pleistocene marine paleoshorelines. Heavy mineral placer deposits (ilmenite/rutile/leucoxene ± monazite/xenotime ± zircon) occur in depositional zones along marine paleoshorelines from Florida to Virginia. These deposits can be important sources of titanium, zircon, and rare earth elements (REEs).

pdescrip: Clayton Valley, Rhyolite Ridge, western Nevada
abstract: The Clayton Valley/Rhyolite Ridge area is in a zone of active dextral transtention in western Nevada. The area has a protracted deformation history with widespread Paleozoic to Mesozoic contraction, uplift, and subsequent extensional collapse and dismemberment. The metamorphic basement is late Proterozoic, Cambrian, and Ordovician sedimentary rocks intruded by Jurassic to Cretaceous felsic stocks and later subjected to bimodal volcanism. Clayton Valley itself is surrounded by fault-bounded mountain ranges on all sides. Clayton Valley contains North America''s only lithium brine operation, and recent exploration has resulted in discovery of a world-class resource of lithium/boron-bearing clays adjacent to the brine field. The deposits are related to a lacustrine evaporite mineral system, which operated within the closed drainage basin in the arid climate. It is probable that lithium in residual brines reacted with lake sediments, ash layers, and volcanic rocks to form lithium-rich clays.

pdescrip: Upper Coastal Plain, South Carolina
abstract: The Orangeburg Scarp is a Pliocene shoreline that is the boundary between Cretaceous, Paleocene, and Eocene terrigenous fluvial, deltaic, and estuarine sediments above the scarp and Pliocene and Pleistocene marine deposits below the scarp. Proposed mapping areas on both sides of the scarp are informed by a recent Earth MRI high-resolution geophysical survey that revealed areas of positive aeroradiometric anomalies that may be related to undiscovered heavy mineral placers deposits. Known commodities and deposit types are placer mineral systems concentrate insoluble resistate minerals (such as ilmenite, rutile, monazite, xenotime, and zircon) liberated from crystalline rocks and further transported and deposited by the movement of water. In this area REEs sourced from crystalline rocks above the Fall Line may be concentrated in heavy mineral placers in terrigenous sediments above the Orangeburg scarp and along river valleys cutting through the scarp.

pdescrip: Virginia Fall Zone
abstract: The Fall Zone in Virginia marks the geologic boundary between Devonian to late Paleozoic age igneous rocks and Neoproterozoic metavolcanic and metaigneous rocks in the eastern Piedmont province and the unconsolidated sediments in the Coastal Plain to the east. Unconsolidated Pliocene-age beach sands of the Coastal Plain contain placer deposits are past producers for heavy minerals. Heavy mineral placer deposits (ilmenite/rutile/leucoxene ± monazite/xenotime ± zircon) occur in depositional zones along marine paleoshorelines from Florida to Virginia. The Earth MRI Placer Ti-Zr-REE focus area in Virginia encompasses known deposits containing economic concentrations of titanium, zirconium, and REEs, and large positive aeroradiometric anomalies within this area are consistent with the presence of additional heavy minerals in the near-surface.

pdescrip: Cedar Canyon mining district, northeast Washington
abstract: In northeastern Washington Proterozoic rocks of the Belt Supergroup and Deer Trail and Windermere Groups are overlain by Paleozoic metasedimentary rocks. Jurassic and Late Cretaceous compression resulted in regional faulting and plutonism. After the Late Cretaceous, rapid extension brought midcrustal-level rocks to the surface and was accompanied by extensive Eocene-aged volcanism. Mineralization in the Cedar Canyon mining district is likely related to this Eocene tectonic episode. The Cedar Canyon mining district was a significant past producer of tungsten. Mineralization in the mining district is a result of a porphyry Cu-Mo-Au mineral system. Polymetallic sulfide veins carrying tungsten and molybdenum cut Eocene-age plutons, suggesting structural control and have a possible genetic relationship to host peraluminous plutons.

pdescrip: Upper Mississippi Valley mineral district, Wisconsin
abstract: Bedrock in the Upper Mississippi Valley mineral district include Cambrian to Ordovician siliciclastic and carbonate rocks that were mildly deformed at least twice during the Paleozoic. The earliest deformation was probably during the Early or Middle Ordovician, and the second more significant event was sometime after the Silurian and may have reactivated earlier structures. The classic MVT (Mississippi Valley type) Upper Mississippi Valley mineral district produced zinc, lead, and minor copper from the 1930s up until 1978. Mineralizing brines carrying thiosulfate and metals originated from the Illinois basin and migrated up dip into the mining district. Sphalerite, galena, pyrite, and other sulfide minerals precipitated by reaction with organic material in open-space fillings, in solution and collapse breccias, along pitches and flats, and in veins and joints, in host rocks around 270±4 Ma.

pdescrip: Geologic mapping in parts of the Big Delta, Eagle, and Mount Hayes quadrangles, Alaska
abstract: The Alaska Division of Geological and Geophysical Surveys is mapping a portion of the Yukon-Tanana Uplands Earth MRI focus area that includes several structurally bound assemblages of polydeformed Paleozoic metamorphic rocks. The western portion is dominated by the Lake George and Butte assemblages, metamorphosed igneous and basinal sedimentary rocks of the parautochthonous North American margin. The eastern area includes the Fortymile River and Nasina assemblages, parts of the allochthonous Yukon-Tanana terrane. The objective is to complete modern 1:100,000-scale geologic mapping, geochemistry, and geochronology in parts of the Big Delta, Eagle, and Mount Hayes quadrangles to further characterize the undifferentiated Cretaceous intrusive complexes in the region and capture the region's complete Mesozoic-Cenozoic magmatic and metamorphic history. The goal is increased understanding of the distribution of rock types and structures in the area with potential to host undiscovered critical minerals, evaluate the area for undiscovered alkaline or peralkaline intrusions among the area's numerous undifferentiated Cretaceous granitic bodies, and establish context for Sn, W, and REE mineralization. Potential critical minerals in the project area: Co, Cr, fluorspar, In, Mo, Nb, PGM, REE, Sb, Sn, Ta, Th, W, Y, and Zr.

pdescrip: Geologic mapping of four 7.5-minute quadrangles,Rampant Hills, La Plata, Monument Hills, and Thompson Park, Colorado
abstract: The Colorado Geological Survey plans to map four 7.5-minute quadrangles (Rampant Hills, La Plata, Monument Hills, and Thompson Park) in the historic La Plata mining district along the southwest edge of the San Juan Mountains and eastern portion of the Colorado Plateau in southwestern Colorado. The study area is southwest of the San Juan volcanic field and consists of marine and non-marine sedimentary rocks of Late Paleozoic to Late Mesozoic age. During the Late Cretaceous to Paleogene, these rocks were intruded by porphyritic alkaline (diorite and monzonite) sills and laccoliths, which caused structural doming of the region. The domal uplift is reportedly ~24 km in diameter. Erosion of this dome has exposed mineralized intrusive and sedimentary rocks within the project area. A younger stage of igneous activity included the intrusion of five irregularly shaped alkalic stocks and related syenite, diorite, and monzonite sills, which are associated with contact metamorphism, hydrothermal alteration, and stockwork veining. Barren and later ore-bearing faults also occur in the study area. The objectives of this project are to: 1) complete new mapping, at a scale of 1:24,000, or more detailed; 2) fill in data gaps among previous maps that partially cover the study area; and 3) provide higher resolution in areas where existing mapping is generalized or unclear. Concurrent geochemical and mineralogical studies are also being conducted. The goal is to better define the surface geology associated with mineral deposits in the La Plata polymetallic district. Past mineral production in the district included gold, silver, copper, lead, and zinc; critical minerals are associated with these precious and base metal deposits. Potential critical minerals in the project area: Pd, Pt, Te, V, and Zn.

pdescrip: Geologic mapping of the Sheep Creek-Mineral Hill mineral belt
abstract: The Sheep Creek-Mineral Hill mineral belt, which straddles the border of Idaho and Montana, hosts thin rare earth element (REE)-bearing carbonatite horizons and carbonate-hosted niobium-bearing rutile deposits of unknown age and origin. The Idaho Geological Survey proposes to conduct new geologic mapping in two quadrangles (Ulysses and Shoup) within the belt at a scale of 1:24,000. This mountainous area is underlain by granitic gneiss, amphibolite, gabbro, and paragneiss. Detailed geologic mapping and geochemical sampling are also planned for at least eight of the REE prospects in the Idaho portion of the mineral belt. To augment geologic mapping, rock samples will be collected for geochronology and geochemical characterization, along with stream-sediment samples, which may identify previously unknown mineralization. Deposits in the Sheep Creek-Mineral Hill mineral belt were explored in the 1950s for niobium and titanium, with some underground development in Montana. The area was also prospected for REE in the late 2000s, but no drilling was done. Stratigraphic correlations in the region are obscure, and source and ore controls are not evident from current data. The timing of REE mineralization in the Sheep Creek-Mineral Hill region is unknown, and geochronology may help determine its relationship to better-studied REE-bearing veins in the Lemhi Pass area. The objective is to provide detailed, publicly available geologic mapping and related geochemical data to better understand the geologic framework, distribution, age, and controls of the mineralization in the Sheep Creek-Mineral Hill mineral belt. The goal is to inform genetic models for carbonatite deposits in western North America. Potential critical minerals in the project area: Nb, Ti, and REE.

pdescrip: Geophysical, geological, geochemical, and geochronological analysis in Crow Wing and Aitkin Counties of north-central Minnesota
