abstract: The Minnesota Geological Survey, in partnership with the Minnesota Department of Natural Resources, the University of Minnesota Natural Resources Research Institute, and the USGS, is conducting geophysical, geological, geochemical, and geochronological analysis in Crow Wing and Aitkin Counties of north-central Minnesota, which contain the second largest Mn occurrence in the US. Manganiferous iron ore was produced from Paleoproterozoic iron formations from 1911 to 1984, mostly from shallow, supergene-enriched marine chemocline sediments known as brown ores. The largest tonnages, however, also included hematitic red-brown ore, which extended to depths over 1,000 feet, proximal to altered mafic intrusions. The magnetic anomaly associated with the system is larger than the extent of historic production; this implies the known Cuyuna Mn/Fe resource may be part of a much larger mineral system, permissive for additional Mn, as well as Co, PGE, and other metals. Due to thick sediment cover, only a few bedrock outcrops occur in the eastern portion of the area. The correlation of iron formation sequences in these sediment-covered, overturned, doubly plunging Penokean Orogen fold structures, and the nature of Mn hydrothermal and sedimentary facies controls, thus remain unclear. However, much exploratory drilling and mining on the several iron ranges within the area provide localized insight into the bedrock geology. To clarify mineral potential in this district of much legacy mining, the project is conducting a major compilation and synthesis of selected legacy data from mineral exploration records from fifteen industry collections including approximately 20,000 industry-donated drillhole records with assays. Selected drill cores will be logged and sampled for geochronological and lithogeochemical analyses. Ground geophysics will be conducted with passive seismic determination of depth to bedrock; nine donated industry airborne geophysical surveys, conducted from 1951 to 1974, will also be available for reference. The data compilation will result in a synthesis that will provide a new quantitative insight into the geology and mineral potential of the district and will then support a second phase of geological, geochemical, and geochronological analysis of existing rock cores, possibly with new drilling. Results of the study will also support design of airborne geophysics, which will be crucial in this area that is concealed by glacial sediments up to 100 m thick, especially for areas of greater structural complexity. Potential critical minerals in the project area: Co, Mn, PGE.

pdescrip: Geologic mapping in the Cherokee Pass, Higdon, and Marquand 7.5' quadrangles in Missouri
abstract: The Missouri Geological Survey (MGS) is conducting bedrock geologic mapping in the Cherokee Pass, Higdon, and Marquand 7.5' quadrangles to develop geologic framework models for critical mineral evaluation, including cobalt, nickel, manganese, rare earth elements (REE), titanium, tungsten, and zinc. The quadrangles are in the eastern St. Francois Mountains and contain a portion of the cobalt-rich Fredericktown-Mine La Motte subdistrict of the Southeast Missouri Lead District. The world-class Southeast Missouri Mississippi Valley-type (MVT) Lead District has associated zinc, copper, silver, cobalt, and nickel mineralization in active and past-producing mines, making Missouri one of only nine states with potentially economically viable cobalt deposits. Potential also exists for gallium, germanium and indium associated with MVT mineralization. These quadrangles contain the Higdon lead-zinc-cobalt-nickel deposit as well as multiple known occurrences of cobalt, nickel, manganese, zinc, and tungsten. Manganese and tungsten are also found in igneous rocks within the project area. Additionally, igneous-hosted deposits occurring outside of the project area are known to contain REE and cobalt mineralization. The area is characterized by Mesoproterozoic igneous rock "knobs" representing exposed paleotopographic highs, onlapped by valley fill of Cambrian-age sedimentary strata, which are overlain on the eastern margin by Lower Ordovician strata. Bedrock geologic mapping will better describe and define igneous rock units and sedimentary facies, and aid evaluation of geologic resource potential and mineralogical and geochemical unit compositions. This mapping project will support evaluation of MVT-hosted zinc-cobalt-nickel mineralization, potential for Mesoproterozoic mineralization, manganese mineralization in multiple lithologies, and relationships between Cambrian-age strata and Mesoproterozoic rocks. Field work, petrography, geochemical analyses, USGS geophysical and geochemical data, and data from multiple in-house and external sources will be incorporated into the project. New mapping will support development of a critical minerals framework in the Fredericktown-Mine La Motte subdistrict area, and aid improvement of basement framework, mineralization, and ore control and fluid conduit models. Potential critical minerals in the project area: Co, Mn, Ni, REE, Ti, W, and Zn.

pdescrip: Geologic mapping of three 7.5-minute quadrangles, Mapleton, Presque Isle, and the Number Nine Lake, Maine
abstract: The Maine Geological Survey is conducting detailed 1:24,000-scale geologic mapping and geochemical, geochronological, and paleontological characterization of sedimentary iron-manganese deposits and the surrounding rocks in Aroostook County to better understand the conditions of deposit formation. Iron-Mn mineralization in the county has been known for more than 150 years. However, the last bedrock mapping and exploration for Fe-Mn deposits was 70 years ago. This project will focus on three 7.5-minute quadrangles: the Mapleton, Presque Isle, and the Number Nine Lake quadrangles, all of which contain known deposits. Iron-Mn deposits in the district occur in banded iron formation-style rocks within the lower to middle Silurian New Sweden Formation in the northern sub-district, Maple Mountain Formation in the central sub-district, and Smyrna Mills Formation in the southern sub-district. Fine-grained Fe-Mn deposits are associated with (a) an oxide facies of red hematite, slate, and banded ironstone, and (b) a carbonate facies of green siliceous carbonate rocks. Mineralized zones are stratiform or lenticular, steeply dipping to near vertical due to tight folding, and may have been thickened somewhat along hinge zones. This work will produce detailed bedrock maps where only small-scale, or reconnaissance maps currently exist and will map distributions and characteristics of manganese-bearing deposits. Northern Maine has widespread Silurian sedimentary rocks that may contain similar layers of favorable geology for deposition of Fe-Mn deposits. The objective is to establish the Silurian stratigraphy in the region and the goal is to better understand the metallogenic conditions that produced the Fe-Mn deposits. Potential critical mineral in the project area: Mn.

pdescrip: Geologic mapping in Nestoria, Three Lakes, Ralph, and Ralph SW quadrangles in Michigan
abstract: The Michigan Geological Survey (MGS) is mapping in the Upper Peninsula of Michigan, one of the most historically important areas of U.S. mineral production for the past 175 years, with copper from the Keweenaw district and White Pine deposit, and iron from the Marquette, Menomonie, and Gogebic iron ranges. The region also contains the small, very high-grade, conduit-type “Eagle-Lundin” Ni-Cu-PGE-Co sulfide deposit, metamorphic graphite deposits with minor past production, and secondary manganese production from the Groveland iron mine north of Iron Mountain. Magmatic REE deposits may also be hosted in poorly understood Paleoproterozoic (and Archean?) granites. Finally, undiscovered LCT pegmatites may be present in Archean rocks that have historically received little attention. The Upper Peninsula has had minimal 1:24:000-scale geologic mapping and geophysical data compilation over the last forty years. State of Michigan and USGS geologists have selectively mapped areas, typically related to their mineral potential, but these areas are not necessarily contiguous, and a comprehensive compilation of such mapping has not been made. Geologic mapping in this region has been hampered by extensive glacial cover, wetlands, and difficult access; nearly all mapping in this region of complex Precambrian geology was conducted prior to the advent of plate tectonic concepts. The MGS will map in four quadrangles (Nestoria, Three Lakes, Ralph, and Ralph SW) at 1:24,000 scale and will field verify recently acquired high-resolution geophysical signatures. The MGS will also examine relevant available drill core to ensure a comprehensive map can be made. There is an extensive suite of archived exploratory drill cores with descriptive logs and limited geochemical data at the State of Michigan’s Upper Peninsula Core Repository and at private/industry core storage locations (for example, Cleveland-Cliffs warehouses) that will provide crucial information for these mapping projects. Samples from these collections and from outcrop will be used for detailed petrological and geochemical studies. The MGS will also reach out to the mineral industry in Michigan to determine if there are other available data resources that can be accessed to evaluate near surface geologic samples or core. This mapping program, coupled with new airborne geophysical and complete lidar coverages, aims to improve the geologic understanding of the area and to identify near surface and deeper geologic features and mineral systems favorable for critical minerals. Potential critical minerals in the project area: Co, Cr, Cs, graphite, Li, Mn, Nb, Ni, PGE, REE, Sn, Ta, Ti, and Zn.

pdescrip: Geologic mapping of the Illinois-Kentucky Fluorspar District (IKFD) and the Midwest Permian Ultramafic District (MPUD)
abstract: The Illinois State Geological Survey (ISGS) is continuing mapping the Illinois-Kentucky Fluorspar District (IKFD) and the Midwest Permian Ultramafic District (MPUD), which are locations of proven critical mineral resources and were a major source of fluorspar prior to the 1970s. Ultramafic lamprophyres, carbonatites, and mineralized lamprophyric breccias (for example, Hicks Dome) containing significant quantities of rare earth elements occur within the IKFD-MPUD. Diatremes and breccia pipes are well documented near and within Hicks Dome, where various rare earth element minerals have been identified. A USGS Earth MRI Phase 1 airborne geophysical survey revealed numerous magnetic anomalies that may represent unmapped dikes and possible igneous centers in addition to Hicks Dome. Field mapping and geochemical analysis of metasomatic bedrock features are being conducted proximal to identified magnetic anomalies. The ISGS is also conducting stream sediment sampling near magnetic anomalies, with geochemical analysis of heavy mineral separates. Geologic mapping will take place in known fluorspar subdistricts within the IKFD at the apex of Hicks Dome (Karber’s Ridge Quadrangle, Hardin Co., IL). The ISGS will conduct surficial mapping, sampling, and geochemical analysis of near-surface fluorspar occurrences, soils, and oxidized and regolith zones at Hicks Dome to shed light on how critical minerals are concentrated in these materials. Southern Illinois represents a remarkable location to map and study the relationship between magnetic anomalies and carbonatite-related rare earth element and fluorspar mineralization. Project results will shed light on genetic links between the IKFD and the MPUD. The goal is a 3D model for both districts that integrates subsurface well, structural, geophysical, geochemical, mineralogical, and historical mine footprint data; the 3D model is expected to aid in critical mineral assessment and future exploration efforts. Potential critical minerals in the project area: Be, barite, F, Nb, REE, Th, Ti, and Y.

pdescrip: Mapping the the South Central Kentucky and North Central Tennessee mineral districts (KTMD)
abstract: The Kentucky Geological Survey, in collaboration with the Tennessee Geological Survey, is developing a regional stratigraphic framework and mineral system conceptual models as well as an accompanying integrated database that covers a representative area of the South Central Kentucky and North Central Tennessee mineral districts (KTMD). These mineral districts are in the Cumberland Saddle region of the Cincinnati Arch that separates the Illinois and Appalachian Basins, and they encompass a focus area that is permissive for mineral commodities related to at least two mineral systems: marine chemocline, and basin brine path. The KTMD host deposits of sphalerite, galena, fluorite and barite mostly related to basin brine path Mississippi Valley-type (MVT) mineralization within units of the Lower Ordovician Upper Knox Group. Unlike most other MVT deposits in North America, the KTMD deposits contain substantial amounts of fluorite, like the nearby Illinois-Kentucky Fluorspar District (IKFD) to the west-northwest, and the Central Kentucky Mineral district to the north. The close association of fluorite deposits and igneous dikes in the IKFD has long been invoked to explain a magmatic origin of fluorine in fluorite. In the KTMD, sphalerite is the only historically ore-grade mineral and has been shown to contain elevated concentrations of critical minerals, including germanium, gallium and cobalt. Large, unevenly distributed sphalerite ore bodies are associated with the outer zones of solution-collapse breccia bodies. Sphalerite occurs in varying proportions with galena, fluorite, barite, marcasite, pyrite, calcite, dolomite, and quartz. In addition to solution-collapse breccias, barite occurs filling fractures and faults within Ordovician to Mississippian carbonates and in residual surface deposits formed by the weathering of barite-bearing vein and breccia deposits. The project is using the extensive collection of geologic data and cores at the Earth Analysis Research Library at the Kentucky Geological Survey. The project will provide compilation of mappable data, selection of samples, sampling and analysis of mineral cores for geochemistry and petrography, integration of the results into supporting databases, and interpretation and synthesis of data over the entire area. The objective is improved understanding of the distribution, geologic context, structural controls, and mineralogical associations of critical minerals in this focus area. The goal is to develop regional stratigraphic framework and mineral system conceptual models to support the critical mineral assessment in the KTMD. Potential critical minerals in project area: barite, Co, F, Ga, Ge, and Zn.

pdescrip: Geologic mapping of the Sheep Creek-Mineral Hill mineral belt
abstract: The Sheep Creek-Mineral Hill mineral belt extends in a northwest direction from Idaho into Montana and hosts rare earth element (REE)-bearing carbonatite horizons and carbonate-hosted niobium-bearing rutile deposits of unknown age and origin. The Montana Bureau of Mines and Geology is conducting geologic mapping within the Sheep Creek-Mineral Hill mineral belt at a scale of 1:24,000 in the Alta and Horse Creek Pass quadrangles. Detailed geologic mapping and geochemical sampling will be conducted around clusters of REE prospects in the Montana portion of the mineral belt. To augment geologic mapping, rock samples are being collected for geochronology and geochemical characterization, along with stream-sediment samples to help pinpoint undiscovered mineralization. In Montana, the area of interest is underlain by granitic gneiss, amphibolite, gabbro, and paragneiss. REE minerals are distributed through carbonate host rocks that occur in a band of pre-Mesoproterozoic gneisses and schists and localized within amphibolite layers. REE deposits were explored in the 1950s for niobium and titanium with some underground development in Montana, and were prospected for REEs in the late-2000s. The REE deposits have not been drilled. Monazite has low thorium, and niobium is present in columbite and niobium-rutile. Stratigraphic correlations in the region are obscure, and source and ore controls are not evident from current data. The timing of REE mineralization in the Sheep Creek-Mineral Hill region is unknown, and dating may help determine its relationship to better studied REE-bearing veins in the Lemhi Pass area, Idaho. The objective of the project is to provide detailed, publicly available geologic mapping and related geochemical data to better understand the geologic framework, distribution, age, and controls of the mineralization in the Sheep Creek-Mineral Hill REE belt. The goal is to inform genetic models for carbonatite deposits in western North America. Potential critical minerals in project area: Nb, REE, and Ti.

pdescrip: Geologic mapping in the Thacker Pass area of the McDermitt caldera in northern Nevada
abstract: The Nevada Bureau of Mines and Geology (NBMG) is conducting 1:24,000-scale geologic mapping in the Thacker Pass area of the McDermitt caldera in northern Nevada, which hosts some of the richest Li districts in the United States and North America. The area is currently under extensive exploration, mostly for Li-bearing clays in Miocene intracaldera basins in the southern part of the caldera in Nevada, and in its northern extension in Oregon. McDermitt caldera is part of the large igneous province that produced the Columbia River Basalt Group and other silicic calderas of the Yellowstone hotspot track. Little is known about the critical factors of caldera evolution and basin development generating Li-bearing clay resources. Unresolved issues include source rocks of Li, the potential role of hydrothermal activity during the initial caldera-forming eruption, and the effect of resurgence-related magmatism on the concentration of Li. The Thacker Pass area is in the southern part of the McDermitt caldera; the area hosts the most important regional Li resources and has been extensively drilled in the last years. The Thacker Pass section is about 100 m thick and is divided into five distinct units from youngest to oldest: unit 1, capping basalt and upper shale; unit 2, tephra-dominant with intercalated shale; unit 3, carbonaceous shale- dominant with thin tephra; unit 4, mixed tephra and shale; and unit 5, densely welded McDermitt Tuff. Ore-grade Li mineralization occurs in shale-dominant strata of unit 3 and in overlying unit 2. Lithium-bearing clays consist of mineralogically complex assemblages of illite (2.5-2.7 weight percent Li2O) and smectite (0.8-1.5 weight percent Li2O) in variable proportions. Detailed geologic mapping in conjunction with thin section petrography, whole-rock and mineral geochemistry, interpreted geophysical data, and high-precision geochronology will provide important lithological and structural constraints on the distribution and potential origin of Li deposits in the McDermitt caldera area. The principal objective of the proposed task is to document the geology of the Thacker Pass area in the southern part of the McDermitt caldera, including the clay-hosted Li deposits, and to provide a lithostratigraphic and structural context to the Li deposits. Potential critical mineral in the project area: Li.

pdescrip: Geologic mapping in the Wichita Mountains in Southern Oklahoma
abstract: The Oklahoma Geological Survey (OGS) is conducting geologic and geochemical mapping in the Wichita Mountains in Southern Oklahoma to determine the critical mineral potential of the area. The Wichita Mountains consist of knobs and hills comprising a sequence of Cambrian to Proterozoic igneous bodies and Cambrian to Ordovician sedimentary successions. These exposures provide rare views of the basement rocks of the southern midcontinent. Regional and local flow of hydrothermal fluids associated with magmatic events resulted in hydrothermal alteration, producing enrichment in critical and other elements in the area. Past production in the area includes copper, iron, vanadium, zeolite, and zirconium. The Wichita Mountains also contain uranium occurrences, resulting from hydrothermal activity associated with pegmatite and aplite dikes, genetically related to the peralkaline Quanah Granite. Mineral deposits containing aluminum, chromium, copper, iron, lead, titanium, vanadium, and zinc, as well as trace amounts of gold and silver, are reported in the literature. Potential for other critical minerals such as beryllium, cobalt, manganese, niobium, PGE, REE, and tellurium is unknown, but these have been detected in stream sediment geochemical analyses. Detailed mapping of various parts of eight 7.5’ quadrangles at 1:24,000 scale or greater is being conducted using a combination of compiled existing maps and new mapping. The quadrangles include Glen Mountains, Cooperton, Saddle Mountain, Meers, Snyder, Odetta, Quanah Mountain, and Mount Scott. Additional data will be compiled from the OGS core and sample repository. The goal is to investigate the critical mineral potential of the Witchita Mountains area. Potential critical minerals in the project area: Al, Be, Co, Cr, Mn, Nb, PGE, REE, Te, Ti, V, U, Zn, and Zr.

pdescrip: Geologic mapping in the eastern Piedmont of South Carolina
abstract: The South Carolina Department of Natural Resources-Geological Survey (SCGS) is conducting new detailed geologic mapping and geochemical sampling in a portion of the Haile-Ridgeway and Brewer focus area in the eastern Piedmont of South Carolina. The primary purpose is to produce new 1:24,000-scale geologic maps of nine 7.5-minute quadrangles in the Carolina terrane (Flint Hill, Great Falls, Heath Springs, Kershaw, Lancaster Southeast, Liberty Hill, Rabon Crossroads, Westville, and White Oak Creek) and to compile those maps at 1:100,000-scale with existing 1:24,000-scale STATEMAP and SCGS maps. Mapping will help delineate the distribution of base metal and associated critical mineral deposits in the eastern Carolina terrane. Geochemical sampling will target two main areas: 1) Carolina terrane rocks that consist of a heterogeneous assemblage of greenschist-facies, calc-alkaline mafic–felsic metavolcanic and epiclastic sedimentary rocks, and 2) late Paleozoic plutonic rocks (for example, Liberty Hill). Additionally, mineralized zones of quartz-sericite, propylitic, andalusite-quartz, and siliceous alteration will be evaluated for critical minerals. The project area includes the Haile gold mine, whose ore deposits are part of a high sulfidation porphyry Cu-Mo-Au mineral system that has been worked since the mid-1800’s for gold and silver but also has shown potential for topaz, telluride minerals, and REE. Base metal ore deposits are primarily hosted along the contact between lower metavolcanic and upper metasedimentary rocks, and are associated with quartz-sericite, propylitic, andalusite-quartz, and siliceous alteration of the metamorphosed felsic volcanic and sedimentary host rocks; topaz mineralization is commonly found in veinlets and silicified zones and may locally be related to intrusion of late Paleozoic plutons. Volcanic and volcanogenic host rocks have been metamorphosed and deformed by Paleozoic orogenesis and subsequent continental rifting. The goal of the project is to enhance our understanding of the geologic framework of the Haile-Ridgeway and Brewer focus area, aid in the development of lithostratigraphic and structural models, and investigate the potential for economic deposits of critical minerals and base metals. Potential critical minerals in the project area: As, Bi, F (topaz), REE, Sb, Te, and Zn.

pdescrip: Geologic mapping in three quadrangles (Cornudas, Hueco Station, Black Mountains) in Texas
abstract: Researchers from the Texas Bureau of Economic Geology are conducting 1:24,000-scale geologic mapping and geochemical sampling activities to support rare-earth element (REE) assessments in three quadrangles (Cornudas, Hueco Station, Black Mountains) in the Cornudas airborne geophysical survey area, filling in previously unmapped continuations of the Cornudas intrusions. This survey area is one of four being flown by the U. S. Geological Survey (USGS) in the Trans-Pecos focus area of far west Texas where Paleogene alkaline igneous units with REE resource potential are exposed. Paleogene igneous rock exposures have been mapped in quadrangles near the present study area (1) in the Cornudas Mountains in the northeastern part of the Sixteen Mountains quadrangle and the northwestern part of the adjacent Cerro Diablo quadrangle, (2) at Sixteen Mountains and Dog Mountain in the southeastern part of the Sixteen Mountains quadrangle, (3) at Cerro Diablo in the southeastern part of the Cerro Diablo quadrangle, and (4) in parts of the Sierra Tinaja Pinta in the southwestern part of the Cerro Diablo quadrangle. The new mapping extends previous STATEMAP-related and Earth MRI geologic mapping generally to the east of the geophysical survey areas and is specifically focused on exposures of shallow Paleogene igneous intrusions and their potential as critical mineral resources. The subsurface extent and expression of these intrusive rocks are not well understood. Airborne geophysical surveys being conducted as a major part of this Earth MRI effort are intended to reveal the lateral and vertical expression of physical properties that can serve as proxies for these igneous units and host strata. The three quadrangles being mapped in the present study cover nearly two-thirds of the Cornudas geophysical survey area in Texas, where several igneous intrusions are exposed in the Cornudas Mountains, Cerro Diablo, and the Sierra Tinaja Pinta. The Texas part of the broader Cornudas survey area is on the Diablo Plateau. All Diablo Plateau intrusive rocks are hypabyssal or subvolcanic units emplaced at medium to shallow depths in the crust. Igneous bodies include discordant sheets, sills, plugs, domes, and laccoliths intruded into Lower Permian limestone and Lower Cretaceous sandstone. These igneous rocks show limited compositional variation, ranging from nepheline syenite and phonolite to syenite and trachyte. The goal of the project is to construct improved two- and three-dimensional geologic models of the distribution, extent, and volume of these potential resources and their relationship to strata into which they intrude. Potential critical minerals in the project area: REE.

pdescrip: Geologic mapping in the Yukon-Tanana Uplands, Chena project, Alaska
abstract: The Alaska Division of Geological and Geophysical Surveys (DGGS) is mapping a ~14,500 km2 (~5,600 mi2) area of the Yukon-Tanana Uplands Earth MRI focus area. The Chena field-geologic mapping project will build on ongoing USGS and DGGS collaborative efforts to upgrade geologic data in the Yukon-Tanana Uplands. The primary objectives are to: complete modern 1:100,000-scale geologic mapping, geochemistry, and geochronology in parts of the Big Delta, Circle, Fairbanks, and Charley River 1:250,000 quadrangles. The mapping campaign will parse and further characterize the Cretaceous intrusive complexes in the region. It will establish context for Sn, W, and REE mineralization and evaluate the area for undiscovered tin granites and peralkaline intrusions among the area's numerous Cretaceous granitic bodies. Mapping, geochemistry, and geochronology will be used to capture the region's complete Mesozoic-Cenozoic magmatic history, which will increase understanding of the distribution of igneous rocks with potential to host undiscovered critical minerals. Potential critical minerals in the project area: As, Bi, Co, In, REE, Re, Se, Sn, Te, PGE, W, and graphite.

pdescrip: Geologic mapping in the Black Hawk district, New Mexico
abstract: The Black Hawk arsenide-5-element vein deposits are an unusual high-grade, hydrothermal vein deposit type that contains silver (Ag) and various critical minerals including nickel (Ni), cobalt (Co), bismuth (Bi), arsenic (As) and local uranium (U), zinc (Zn), copper (Cu), and antimony (Sb). The arsenide-5-element vein deposits in the Black Hawk district are the only such deposits known in New Mexico and have been known since they were first mined for Ag in the late 1880s. The Black Hawk district in the Burro Mountains, in southwestern New Mexico, occurs within a Proterozoic highland that has been affected by Laramide compression and magmatic intrusions followed by caldera magmatism and later during extension associated with the development of the Rio Grande rift. The Black Hawk deposits are ankerite-bearing, northeast-trending fissure veins hosted within Proterozoic metamorphic and granitic rocks. The vein deposits are thought to be Late Cretaceous in age, and possibly related to the porphyry copper deposits, based upon field relationships and a K-Ar age of 65.3±1.2 Ma on alteration assemblages at the Black Hawk mine. The purposes of this project are to (1) map the geology, structure, alteration, and mineralized areas, (2) determine the mineralogy, chemical composition, origin, and critical-mineral potential of the arsenide-5-element vein deposits in the Black Hawk district, and (3) compare the Black Hawk deposits to other arsenide-5-element vein deposits in the world. Highly altered areas could be indicative of higher grades of undiscovered mineral resources and used as vectors towards highly mineralized zones. Geochemical whole rock and mineral analyses of igneous rocks, mineralized veins, and alteration zones will aid in a better understanding of the genetic relationship between the igneous intrusions and the mineralization processes in the area. Potential critical minerals in the project area: As, Bi, Co, Sb, and Zn.

pdescrip: Geologic mapping in Arkansas Mississippi Valley-Type Mineral District
abstract: The Arkansas Geological Survey is conducting detailed geologic mapping in the Mississippi Valley-Type (MVT) Mineral District in north Arkansas, an area that contains the critical mineral zinc and associated minerals in the Basin brine path mineral system. The proposed two-dimensional geologic mapping is divided between two areas within the Northern Arkansas MVT Mineral District focus area that contain several small historical lead and zinc mining districts: the Northeast District, northeast of the Batesville Manganese District and the North Central District in north-central Arkansas, stretching from the Buffalo National River north to the Arkansas-Missouri state line. The project will locate and describe geologic units within these two MVT districts that contain critical minerals, collect samples of those units, especially in mineralized zones, for geochemical analyses, and integrate the data into a geospatial database for ease of use. Results will help pave the way for subsequent research to produce an updated model for MVT zinc and lead mineral resource assessments in the region and possibly nationwide. The known critical mineral in the project area is Zn; potential additional critical minerals in the project area include barite, cobalt, and nickel.

pdescrip: Geologic mapping in the Orient 15-minite quadrangle in northeast Washington
abstract: The Washington Geological Survey is conducting geologic mapping in the Orient 15-minute quadrangle in northeast Washington, which has a high density of metallic mineral occurrences, is the site of historical mining, and has not previously been mapped. The objective is to assemble a 1:48,000-scale geologic map of the Orient quadrangle with a unified geologic framework, detailed rock descriptions, and supporting geochronology/geochemistry analyses to better document and understand the role of Eocene extension and igneous activity on mineralization. This mapping activity, along with a study of pluton emplacement in the Cedar Canyon district, will allow the development of a more complete picture of the conditions responsible for producing high concentrations of critical mineral occurrences in these two areas along the regional-scale Kettle detachment fault system. The project will include detailed micro-geochemical and petrologic study of zircon and calc-silicate minerals near plutons to constrain geochemical conditions and emplacement depths related to mineralization. The close association between Eocene rocks and mineralization in this area suggests that much, if not most mineralization formed in response to regional-scale extension. Potential critical minerals in the project area: Bi, graphite, Sb, Sn, W, and Zn.

pdescrip: New geologic mapping of the Blackbird Mountain, Gant Ridge, and Gant Mountain 7.5' quadrangles, Idaho cobalt belt
abstract: The Idaho Cobalt Belt consists of strata-bound Fe-Co-Cu-Au-Bi-Y-REE deposits that have a complex history of multistage mineralization and late epigenetic vein emplacement. The proposed Cobalt Belt III project builds on current Earth MRI mapping and geochemical sampling in the Cobalt, Degan Mountain, and Taylor Mountain quadrangles (Cobalt Belt I) and Blackbird Mountain and Opal Lake quadrangles (Cobalt Belt II). The Idaho Geological Survey is conducting additional new geologic mapping in three adjoining quadrangles (Blackbird Mountain, Gant Ridge, and Gant Mountain) at a scale of 1:24,000. To augment the geologic mapping objectives, geochemical characterization will be performed on rock and stream sediment samples along the Idaho Cobalt Belt. Potential critical minerals in the project area: Bi, Co, REE, Y.

pdescrip: Geologic mapping of six 1:24k quadrangles (Delta, Hampstead, Hereford, Finksburg, Reisterstown, and Rockville) in Potomac terrane, Maryland
abstract: The Maryland Geological Survey is mapping six 1:24k quadrangles in the Earth MRI focus area known as the Potomac terrane, with georeferenced geochemical data from metamorphic mafic-ultramafic bodies and the surrounding geologic formations. The Potomac terrane is composed of metasedimentary rocks with included mafic-ultramafic blocks, forming a 100-km long, 20-km wide zone of polygenetic mélange trending NE through the central Maryland Piedmont from the Potomac River (SE) to Susquehanna River (NE). Mineralization within the Potomac terrane focus area is associated with these mafic-ultramafic bodies. In Maryland, individual bodies vary greatly in size and shape from outcrop-scale to 22 km2 and were mined historically, with chromite being the dominant mineral commodity produced. This task will focus on historically sampled mineralized areas as well as a new concerted effort sampling mafic-ultramafic bodies and their surrounding metasedimentary rocks. The six quadrangles to be mapped are distributed across the Potomac terrane, encompassing the most heavily mined and quarried areas. This updated, detailed geologic mapping will improve spatial accuracy of geologic contacts and resolve conflicts in formational nomenclature within Maryland. The spatial occurrence and chemical content of mafic-ultramafic rocks will be better constrained through georeferenced mapping and geochemical sampling. Ultimately this will provide a better geologic framework for understanding the tectonic origin of ultramafic rocks in the Maryland Piedmont. This may result in new insight to the occurrence and origin of mafic-ultramafic units within the Potomac terrane and elsewhere within the larger, Appalachian orogenic mafic-ultramafic belt that extends from Newfoundland, Canada to Alabama. Five of these designated quadrangles lie within the bounds of the USGS Appalachian Transect aeromagnetic survey, which will improve mapping of geologic contacts, particularly where they are not exposed at the surface. The integration of geochemical and aeromagnetic data with geospatial geologic mapping will facilitate recognition of chemical affinities, structural controls, and a better understanding of the origin of these bodies. Potential critical minerals in the project area: Co, Cr, Ni, PGM.

pdescrip: Geologic mapping of the Payne Creek and Bretz Mine 7.5′ quadrangles in the northern sector of the McDermitt caldera, Malheur County
abstract: The Oregon Department of Geology and Mineral Industries (DOGAMI) is conducting 1:24,000-scale geologic mapping of the Payne Creek and Bretz Mine 7.5′ quadrangles in the northern sector of the McDermitt caldera in Malheur County of southeastern Oregon. The McDermitt caldera contains known deposits or concentrations of several critical minerals, including lithium, gallium, and zirconium, as well as gold deposits. The only known lithium deposit in Oregon is associated with lake sediments within the McDermitt caldera. The McDermitt caldera is a single, ∼45 km (north-south) by ~30 km (east-west) early Miocene caldera straddling the state line border of southeastern Oregon and northwestern Nevada. It lies at the southern end of the Columbia River flood basalt province and is regarded as the continental point of emergence of the Yellowstone hotspot, and its younger trace extending northeast through Idaho to Yellowstone National Park in northwest Wyoming. Caldera structures focused numerous hydrothermal systems in the area leading to mercury, zirconium-rich uranium, gallium, and gold mineralization. The project will allow DOGAMI to provide more detailed mapping and stratigraphic assessment of pre- and post-caldera geologic units, better understand the geologic history of the McDermitt caldera, and allow for inventory and analysis of the geologic conditions of the mineral system controlling the distribution of critical minerals. Potential critical minerals in the project area: Ga, Li, and Zr.

pdescrip: Geologic mapping of the Ironton, Rhodes Mountain, and Rock Pile Mountain 7.5' quadrangles in the western and central St. Francois Mountains, Missouri
abstract: The Missouri Geological Survey (MGS) is conducting bedrock geologic mapping of the Ironton, Rhodes Mountain, and Rock Pile Mountain 7.5' quadrangles in the western and central St. Francois Mountains to develop geologic framework models for critical mineral evaluation. Mesoproterozoic igneous-hosted IOA/IOCG deposits in the region contain known cobalt, rare earth elements (REEs), bismuth, tin, titanium, and tungsten. Manganese is found with both IOA and MVT deposits. Cobalt, nickel, and zinc are found in MVT deposits, and may have associated gallium, germanium, and indium. The quadrangles are located within the Southeast Missouri Iron Metallogenic Province and contain a portion of the cobalt-rich Fredericktown-Mine La Motte subdistrict of the Southeast Missouri Lead District. A small-scale structure contour map of the top of the Mesoproterozoic basement will be modified with new data. Relationships between mineralization, Mesoproterozoic rocks, sedimentary facies, and structure are poorly understood in the St. Francois Mountains and relationships between multiple intrusive and extrusive units are not documented. Delineation of previously unknown igneous units is expected, as is greater definition of Cambrian facies and their relationships to mineralization. Bedrock mapping is key to understanding regional geologic frameworks and settings of IOA/IOCG and MVT deposits. Potential critical minerals in the project area: Bi, Co, Ga, Ge, In, Mn, Ni, REE, Sn, Ti, W, and Zn.

pdescrip: Geologic mapping of the Daggett 7.5- minute quadrangle and portions of the Manix, Troy Lake, Hidden Valley West, and Hector 7.5- minute quadrangles in southern California
abstract: The California Geological Survey (CGS) is conducting 1:24,000-scale mapping of the Daggett 7.5- minute quadrangle and portions of the Manix, Troy Lake, Hidden Valley West, and Hector 7.5- minute quadrangles in southern California. Geologic mapping will target rocks tentatively identified by previous authors as the Miocene Barstow Formation and equivalent age units at Daggett Ridge and the western Cady Mountains. The CGS is also conducting a geochemical reconnaissance of the Miocene Barstow Formation and equivalent age units. The study area encompasses a series of dry lakes in northwest-trending valleys within the Eastern California Shear Zone, extending from the Gravel Hills southeast to Bristol Lake. The Barstow Formation is interpreted to represent a fining-upward sequence of basin-fill sediments; lithium occurs in fine-grained sedimentary units within the Barstow Formation and equivalent age units. The formation of sedimentary-hosted lithium deposits is understudied and needs further investigation. Although these deposit types make up less than 3% of the global lithium resource, the sedimentary-hosted lithium deposit at Thacker Pass, Nevada is the fifth largest known lithium resource in the world, which demonstrates the potential importance of this deposit type. The mapping and geochemical reconnaissance will aim to better characterize the extent and geologic setting of sedimentary-hosted lithium deposits in the study area. This study will provide new integrated geologic and geochemical mapping of an area with critical mineral resource potential and will provide new interpretations of above-ground geologic and geochemical data as it relates to mineral resource potential for future subsurface correlation. These new data will improve our understanding of the spatial distribution of and geologic controls on lithium mineralization potential across the study area. Constraining the timing and environment of mineralization is critical for future development of deposit models that are central to the generation of successful exploration targets. Potential critical mineral in the project area: Li.

pdescrip: Geologic mapping and sampling in the Alabama graphite-vanadium belt in Micaville, Newell, and Wedowee 7.5-minute quadrangles in Randolph County, Alabama
abstract: The Geological Survey of Alabama is conducting detailed geologic mapping and sampling in the Micaville, Newell, and Wedowee 7.5-minute quadrangles in Randolph County, Alabama, within an area designated as the “Expanded Alabama graphite-V belt”. This belt is adjacent to the known resources of the historical Alabama graphite-V belt, which had significant graphite production from the late 1800s through the 1950s and is estimated to have the most extensive deposits of high-grade flake graphite in the conterminous United States. The Expanded Alabama graphite-V belt, as identified by the USGS, encompasses an area of approximately 1,817 mi2 (4,707 km2) in the northern Piedmont province of east-central Alabama and covers parts of eighty-eight quadrangles in eleven counties. The northern Piedmont hosts an abundance of nonfuel geologic resources but remains one of the most poorly understood parts of the southernmost Appalachian orogen due, in large part, to a paucity of detailed investigations and analyses of its major constituent geologic units. The current project supports Earth MRI efforts to qualify/quantify potentially previously undiscovered graphite and vanadium resources in the Expanded Alabama graphite-V belt. Project work will include lithologic and structural mapping of the Micaville, Newell, and Wedowee, Alabama quadrangles; collecting roughly 200 carbonaceous schist and phyllite samples for geochemical analysis; and compiling the new geologic maps and associated files into a digital geodatabase using ESRI ArcGIS software. Samples will also be collected from the previously mapped adjacent quadrangles (Lineville East, Mellow Valley, Milltown, New Site, Ofelia, Roanoke East, Roanoke West, Wadley North, and/or Wadley South quadrangles in parts of Chambers, Clay, Randolph, and Tallapoosa Counties, Alabama) for geochemical analysis to further characterize each unit comprising the Expanded Alabama graphite-V belt in the northern Alabama Piedmont. Potential critical minerals in the project area: graphite and V.

pdescrip: Geologic mapping and geochemical sampling in the Radersburg and Giant Hill 7.5’ quadrangles, Montana
abstract: The Montana Bureau of Mines and Geology (MBMG) is conducting 1:24k geologic mapping and geochemical sampling in the Radersburg and Giant Hill 7.5’ quadrangles, which are within the eastern intrusive contact zone between the Late Cretaceous Boulder Batholith and older, predominately sedimentary rocks that are capped by the Late Cretaceous Elkhorn Mountains Volcanics. The work builds on nearly a decade of field-based geologic mapping studies in the region of the Boulder Batholith, which hosts world-class metallic ore deposits. In addition to geologic mapping and geochemical sampling, the project also aims to collect samples for analysis that will help delineate the timing and uplift history of the ore deposits - including U/Pb age determinations, (U-Th)/He and fission track ages, and fluid inclusion studies. The east flank of the Elkhorn Mountains is part of the Elkhorn Mountains volcanic field, which hosts polymetallic epithermal veins, porphyry Cu-Mo prospects, skarns (Cu-Au), and Pb-Zn±Ag carbonate replacement deposits. A key goal of mapping will be to refine the volcanic stratigraphy of the Elkhorn Mountain Volcanics and associated volcaniclastic sedimentary rocks, which are important hosts for ore deposits and may contain lithocap-style (argillic and advanced argillic) alteration. Numerous small intrusions are present throughout the Radersburg and Giant Hill quadrangles; dating these intrusions and any associated mineralization will improve critical minerals prospectivity assessments of the Boulder Batholith region. The project aims to produce modern geologic mapping, critical minerals geochemical sampling and mineralogical analyses, and new descriptions and interpretations of ore systems with known historic production. Potential critical minerals in the project area: As, Bi, Ga, Ge, In, Mn, REE, Sb, Te, V, and W.

pdescrip: Lidar for FY19-EMRI-Mountain Pass carbonatite terrane, California and Nevada
abstract: This Earth MRI lidar project was acquired summer 2019 for the Primary Area of Interest (AOI) of approximately 2,729 square miles encompassing the Mountain Pass region including portions of San Bernardino County in California and Clark County in Nevada. This task order was for planning, acquisition, processing, and derivative products of QL2 lidar data collected at 0.71 meters ANPS, including overlap. Lidar data and derivative products were produced in compliance with this task order based on the National Geospatial Program Lidar Base Specification Version 1.3, which are incorporated by reference to this task order. This specification may be viewed at https://doi.org/10.3133/tm11B4. These lidar specifications are required baseline specifications.

pdescrip: Lidar for FY19-EMRI-Placer Ti-Zr-REE deposits in Virginia and North Carolina
abstract: Primary Area of Interest (AOI) approximately 1706 square miles of Eastern and Central Virginia. Earth MRI and NRCS-VA AOI. BAAs for Henrico and Williamsburg added as depicted in the Area of Interest (AOI) in Attachment A. The VA_Southampton_2019_D19 AOI has been expanded to the Albers National Indexing Scheme. This project will support NRCS; Henrico County, VA; the City of Williamsburg, VA; and the 3D Elevation Program (3DEP). The Defined Project Area (DPA) and associated AOIs. This task order is for Planning, Acquisition, processing, and derivative products of lidar data to be collected at an aggregate nominal pulse spacing (ANPS) of 0.71 meters, including overlap. Lidar data and derivative products produced in compliance with this task order are based on the National Geospatial Program Lidar Base Specification Version 1.3, which are incorporated by reference to this task order. These lidar specifications are required baseline specifications. For any item which is not specifically addressed, the referenced Version 1.3 specifications will be the required specification authority. This task order requests Fall 2019/Spring2020 leaf-off lidar surveys to be collected. For more information, contact the national 3D Elevation Program at 3DEP@usgs.gov.

pdescrip: Lidar for FY19-EMRI-Hicks Dome Igneous Complex and the Illinois Kentucky Fluorspar District, Illinois, Kentucky, and Indiana
abstract: Primary Area of Interest (AOI) in the state of Kentucky of approximately 3,429 square miles, including the full and partial counties of: Henderson, Daviess, Union, Webster, Hopkins, Crittenden, Livingston, Muhlenberg, Caldwell, Lyon, Christian, Todd, Marshall, Trigg, and Calloway. Some of this project overlaps onto the neighboring states of Tennessee and Indiana. The AOI for this task has been buffered by 100 meters prior. This task order is for Planning, Acquisition, processing, and derivative products of lidar data to be collected at an aggregate nominal pulse spacing (ANPS) of 0.71 meters, including overlap. Lidar data and derivative products produced in compliance with this task order are based on the National Geospatial Program Lidar Base Specification Version 1.3, which are incorporated by reference to this task order. These lidar specifications are required baseline specifications. For any item which is not specifically addressed, the referenced Version 1.3 specifications will be the required specification authority. This task order requests a fall/winter 2019 leaf-off lidar survey to be collected. For more information, contact the national 3D Elevation Program at 3DEP@usgs.gov.

pdescrip: Lidar for FY19-EMRI-Hicks Dome Igneous Complex and the Illinois Kentucky Fluorspar District, Illinois, Kentucky, and Indiana
abstract: Primary Area of Interest (AOI) in the state of Illinois of approximately 7,697 square miles, including the full and partial counties of: St. Clair, Monroe, Randolph, Union, Alexander, Pulaski, Massac, Johnson, Williamson, Franklin, Hamilton, White, Gallatin, Saline, Pope, Hardin, Wayne, Edwards, and Wabash. Some of this project overlaps onto the neighboring states of Kentucky, Missouri, and Indiana. The AOI for this task has been buffered by 100 meters. This task order is for planning, acquisition, processing, and derivative products of lidar data to be collected at an aggregate nominal pulse spacing (ANPS) of 0.5 meters (QL 2+), including overlap. Lidar data and derivative products produced in compliance with this task order are based on the National Geospatial Program Lidar Base Specification Version 1.3, which are incorporated by reference to this task order. For any item which is not specifically addressed, the referenced Version 1.3 specifications will be the required specification authority. This task order requests a fall/winter 2019 leaf-off lidar survey to be collected. For more information, contact the national 3D Elevation Program at 3DEP@usgs.gov.

pdescrip: Lidar for FY19-EMRI-Idaho Cobalt belt
abstract: Primary Area of Interest (AOI) of approximately 42,304 square miles in Idaho (including 31 full and partial counties) with some areas of overlap onto the neighboring states of Oregon, Montana, Wyoming, Nevada, and Utah. The AOI for this task has been buffered by 100 meters. This task order requests a fall 2019/spring 2020 leaf-off lidar survey to be collected. This project will support the Federal Emergency Management (FEMA) Risk Mapping, Assessment, and Planning (MAP) program; the National Resources Conservation Service (NRCS) high resolution elevation enterprise program; the Boise National Forest goal of acquiring high quality lidar throughout the forest; Bureau of Land Managements (BLM) management of Abandoned Mine Lands and delineation of the Jarbidge Wilderness Area; and the 3DEP mission.

pdescrip: The Geoscience Data Acquisition for Western Nevada (GeoDAWN) project is a joint effort between the USGS, Department of Energy Geothermal Technologies Office, the Bureau of Land Management, the USDA Natural Resources Conservation Service, and the Nevada B
abstract: The Geoscience Data Acquisition for Western Nevada (GeoDAWN) project is a joint effort between the USGS, Department of Energy Geothermal Technologies Office, the Bureau of Land Management, the USDA Natural Resources Conservation Service, and the Nevada Bureau of Mines and Geology. The goal of the effort is to collect airborne magnetic and radiometric data and lidar data over a large part of western Nevada centered on the Walker Lane geologic province. The Nevada Bureau of Mines and Geology will conduct geologic mapping in the Clayton Valley and Rhyolite Ridge, areas known to contain lithium resources. The area of focus is highly prospective for geothermal resources--Nevada being second only to California in geothermal energy production and capacity. In addition, the new data collection effort will cover the Humboldt mafic igneous complex, which has potential for hosting iron, cobalt, rare earth elements, platinum group metals, chromium, nickel, and copper.

pdescrip: This project by the Kentucky Geological Survey is part of a companion effort with the West Virginia Geological and Economic Survey and several central and eastern State Geological Surveys to conduct a geochemical reconnaissance survey of rare earth element- and lithium-enriched high-alumina underclay deposit of the central and eastern US.
abstract: This project by the Kentucky Geological Survey (KGS), is part of a companion effort with the West Virginia Geological and Economic Survey (WVGES), and is being done in cooperation with the Geological Surveys of Indiana, Illinois, Iowa, Maryland, and Pennsylvania. The goal of the larger effort is to conduct a geochemical reconnaissance survey of rare earth element (REE)- and lithium-enriched high-alumina underclay deposit of the central and eastern US to investigate possible regional and stratigraphic variations and enrichements. The KGS will focus on the potentially REE-enriched high-alumina Pennsylvanian underclay deposits in Kentucky, Indiana, Illinois, and Iowa. Previous work in the northern Appalachian Basin has demonstrated that Pennsylvanian paleosols, underclays, and flint clays have the potential to have high alumina contents and associated or inferred REE contents. The goal of this KGS-led project is to provide a regional sampling of representative Lower and Middle Pennsylvanian underclay and paleosol beds to allow a reconnaissance understanding of geochemical trends and provide the basis for possible more targeted studies in the future.

pdescrip: This project by the West Virginia Geological and Economic Survey is part of a companion effort with the Kentucky Geological Survey and several central and eastern State Geological Surveys to conduct a geochemical reconnaissance survey of rare earth element- and lithium-enriched high-alumina underclay deposit of the central and eastern US.
abstract: This project by the West Virginia Geological and Economic Survey (WVGES) is part of a companion effort with the Kentucky Geological Survey (KGS) and is being done in cooperation with the Geological Surveys of Indiana, Illinois, Iowa, Maryland, and Pennsylvania. The goal is to conduct a geochemical reconnaissance survey of rare earth element (REE)- and lithium-enriched high-alumina underclay deposit of the central and eastern US. Aluminum-rich, kaolinitic clays associated with coal beds in Pennsylvanian strata occur throughout the Appalachian Plateau Province, the Central Appalachian Basin, the Illinois Basin, and adjacent areas. These clays have been utilized for brick-making and pottery for more than 200 years. Recent studies of the critical mineral potential of Appalachian coals suggest low-level REE-enrichment (>300 ppm) in the underclays. Similar clays host high lithium concentrations (>2,000 ppm), and the presence of elevated aluminum makes elevated Ga and In possible. The project will conduct targeted sampling for geochemical analyses, which will be combined with careful characterization of the host stratigraphy, in order to better understand the following unknowns: locations and abundances of rare earth elements (REEs) in high-alumina underclay deposits in the central and eastern US; the mode(s) of enrichment; differential enrichment processes for observed concentrations of Li versus REEs; role of source rocks; regional differences in parent rock type; paleosol weathering patterns; effects of paleogeography on chemical weathering and leaching in Pennsylvanian subtropical to tropical climates; and the role of depositional and diagenetic factors. This geochemical reconnaissance effort will help to identify specific stratigraphic intervals of interest, to evaluate their regional variations, and to determine the presence or absence of elevated concentrations of associated critical mineral commodities. The WVGES is responsible for leading the multi-state effort and will coordinate sample collection and characterization, to develop a synthesis report that that will inform future research activities.

pdescrip: The Indiana Geological and Water Survey is leading this geochemical reconnaissance effort in conjunction with the Illinois State Geological Survey to provide information on rare earth element (REE) endowment of Devonian-age phosphatic rocks in the central and eastern US.
abstract: The Indiana Geological and Water Survey (IGWS) is leading a geochemical reconnaissance effort to provide information on rare earth element (REE) endowment of Devonian-age phosphatic rocks in the central and eastern US. The effort is being conducted in conjunction with the Illinois State Geological Survey (ISGS) to provide a community-based geochemical sampling program across the Appalachian Basin (Indiana, Ohio, Kentucky, Tennessee, Alabama, Georgia, Virginia, West Virginia, Pennsylvania, and New York) and the Illinois Basin (Illinois, Iowa, Missouri, Arkansas, Oklahoma, Kentucky, and Tennessee). This two-year effort will require collaboration with State Geological Surveys and members of the Devonian research community to strategically sample phosphatic strata from Devonian units. The IGWS will provide guidance and technical support throughout the sample collection process. Multi-element geochemical analysis will ultimately enable the assessment of spatial and stratigraphic architecture necessary for future prospectivity mapping and testing genetic ore deposit models. The IGWS is leading this multi-state effort in conjunction with the ISGS to develop a synthesis report that recommends will help identify areas that may be prospective for hosting critical mineral resources in these deposits and guide future research.

pdescrip: The Illinois State Geological Survey is working with the Indiana Geological and Water Survey to conduct a geochemical reconnaissance to determine the rare earth element (REE) endowment of Devonian-age phosphatic rocks in the central and eastern US.
abstract: The Illinois State Geological Survey (ISGS) is conducting a companion effort with the Indiana Geological and Water Survey (IGWS) for a geochemical reconnaissance effort to provide information on rare earth element (REE) endowment of Devonian-age phosphatic rocks in the central and eastern US. The goal of this ISGS community-based sampling project of Devonian sedimentary phosphate deposits in the central US for geochemical analysis to identify regions and stratigraphic intervals within the Devonian phosphatic sediments that are potentially enriched in heavy-rare earth element and other critical mineral commodities.  With the cooperation of Arkansas, Iowa, Kentucky, Missouri, Tennessee, and Oklahoma State Geological Surveys, the ISGS will execute sampling of phosphorite-rich horizons in outcrops and drill cores to be submitted to USGS for geochemical analysis.  The ISGS will construct a project database to be used by ISGS and IGWS for capturing information on the samples (e.g., location, rock type, stratigraphy, etc.) as well as the results of the geochemical analysis. This effort will provide the regional framework to understand the geographic and stratigraphic distribution of REEs in Devonian phosphatic sediments and help guide potential future research efforts.

pdescrip: This stream sediment reanalysis project is a joint effort between the Alaska Division of Geological & Geophysical Surveys and the U.S. Geological Survey to obtain modern elemental analyses for historical USGS stream sediment samples in the Yukon-Tanana Upland (YTU), Alaska an area prospective for many critical and non-critical mineral resources in multiple mineral-system types.
abstract: This project is a joint effort between the Alaska Division of Geological & Geophysical Surveys and the USGS to obtain modern elemental analyses for historical USGS stream sediment samples in the Yukon-Tanana Upland (YTU), Alaska. The YTU is prospective for many critical and non-critical mineral resources in multiple mineral-system types. The area is currently of intense interest to the mineral industry because of its gold, silver, copper, and molybdenum (rhenium) potential. The Mount Fairplay area has known occurrences of rare earth elements (REE) and thorium, and the presence of mafic-ultramafic complexes in the YTU suggests copper, nickel, cobalt, and platinum-group elements (PGE) may be present as well. Stream-sediment samples were chosen to best represent bedrock that is actively weathering and include all the mineral phases contributing to the elemental compositions of the region. This project will provide consistent coverage of stream sediment samples throughout the YTU, with a full suite of multi-element analyses, including major elements, trace elements, REE, and where enough pulp material is available, gold and PGE to highlight potential mineral deposit targets.

pdescrip: Geochemical Reconnaissance of Ordovician-aged Rare Earth Element-Enriched Sedimentary Phosphate in the Central United States
abstract: Recent USGS research has discovered that francolite (a carbonate-rich apatite mineral), comprising sedimentary phosphate-rich rock (phosphorite), can be highly enriched in rare earth elements (REEs), and notably the heavy rare earth elements (HREEs). A continent-scale assessment has identified several unmined phosphorites in the U.S. that are high grade and larger than the world's largest conventional HREE deposits (Emsbo et al., 2015, 2017). The objectives of this study are to collect new geochemical data on the REE concentrations of Ordovician phosphorites across the Midwest and to map REE concentrations by age of 2 phosphorite. This data will also help future evaluation of ore mineralizing system models by documenting geochemical gradients, phosphorite sedimentology, and characteristics of phosphorite-enclosing strata. Ultimately, this is a geochemical reconnaissance project to test if regional concentrations of REEs in Ordovician phosphorites are consistent with the values recorded by Emsbo et al. (2015) and to gather data on the distribution of the phosphatic host deposits. While the Ordovician stratigraphy of the Midwestern U.S. is relatively well studied, there is little data on regional REE distribution or concentrations of phosphorites. Known commodities and deposit types are phosphate-rich Ordovician sedimentary deposit that may contain rare earth element-rich phosphate s (REEs) and yttrium. This project is being conducted in partnership with the Indiana Geological and Water Survey.

pdescrip: Geochemical Reconnaissance of Ordovician-aged Rare Earth Element-Enriched Sedimentary Phosphate in the Central United States
abstract: Recent USGS research has discovered that francolite (a carbonate-rich apatite mineral), comprising sedimentary phosphate-rich rock (phosphorite), can be highly enriched in rare earth elements (REEs), and notably the heavy rare earth elements (HREEs). A continent-scale assessment has identified several unmined phosphorites in the U.S. that are high grade and larger than the world's largest conventional HREE deposits (Emsbo et al., 2015, 2017).
