pdescrip: Reconnaissance geochemistry on porphyry copper systems in the southwestern United States
abstract: The Arizona Geological Survey and the New Mexico Bureau of Geology and Mineral Resources are conducting a regional geochemical project that focuses on porphyry copper systems in the southwestern United States. The southwestern North American porphyry copper province, straddling the United States-Mexico borderlands of Arizona, New Mexico, westernmost Texas, Sonora, Chihuahua, and Sinaloa, comprises one of the world’s largest endowments of copper, principally due to the abundance and preservation of Late Cretaceous to early Paleogene (“Laramide”) intrusive rocks and related hydrothermal systems. Porphyry systems also contain potentially recoverable critical mineral co-/by-products such as platinum-group-elements, tellurium, indium, germanium, and gallium in anode slimes remaining after copper is refined. Additional potential critical minerals include tungsten, tin, rhenium, bismuth, scandium, nickel, cobalt, manganese, arsenic, aluminum (alunite), and zinc. Our understanding of the distribution of critical minerals in porphyry systems is lacking due to a paucity of detailed geochemistry both at the regional and system scale. Existing publicly available geochemical datasets are inconsistent in methodology, elements analyzed, detection limits, and spatial coverage. New systematic data acquisition that uses the same analytical methods and sampling strategy is required to produce a robust dataset to delineate favorable geologic terranes and porphyry systems with critical mineral potential. This project will provide: 1) a compilation of existing legacy geochemical data; 2) new sample collection from porphyry copper occurrences and other selected sites for system-scale detailed work; 3) in-depth sample characterization (for example, petrography, stained slabs, whole-rock geochemistry, mineral chemistry, isotope geochemistry, geo/thermochronology, geo/thermobarometry, fluid inclusion data); and 4) compilation of legacy and new data into a uniform geochemical database. The goals of this project are to: 1) provide a rigorous comprehensive compilation of geochemical analyses (both new and legacy data) of porphyry systems in the Laramide magmatic arc of Arizona and New Mexico with an emphasis on specific porphyry systems that have elevated concentrations of critical minerals; and 2) develop a better understanding of the distribution of critical minerals within the alteration haloes of porphyry systems, and 3) provide evidence for critical mineral potential for individual prospects/deposits and within specific alteration types and rock types. Potential critical minerals in the project area: Al, As, Bi, Co, Ga, Ge, In, Mn, Ni, PGE, Sc, Sn, Te, W, and Zn.

pdescrip: Reconnaissance geochemistry of the Western Phosphate Field, Idaho, Wyoming, Utah, and Montana
abstract: This four-state cooperative effort, administered by the Idaho Geological Survey and funded by the USGS Earth MRI program, will evaluate the enrichment of critical minerals in the Permian Phosphoria Formation. Exposures of this formation occur across 350,000 square kilometers in Idaho, Utah, Wyoming, and Montana, and it is one of the largest commercial resources of phosphate rock in the world. Mining of the Phosphoria Formation in this region, referred to as the Western Phosphate Field, has provided phosphorus for the fertilizer industry since the early 1900s. The Phosphoria Formation formed within a marine chemocline system, and includes a succession of black organic-rich mudstones, siltstones, phosphorites, carbonates, and cherts deposited 265 million years ago on the western margin of North America. The richest phosphate deposits are in the Meade Peak and Retort members, which both display considerable lithologic and stratigraphic variability. Previous studies have shown that elevated levels of rare earth elements and other critical minerals are concentrated within phosphorites and black shales in these two members. The project centers on collecting new geological and geochemical data primarily from the Meade Peak and Retort members. Data are being acquired along measured stratigraphic sections in the context of a basinwide framework and at locations considered strategic from a mineral resource or scientific standpoint (for example: suitable outcrops, mine exposures, and archived drill core). The objective is to construct geologic models that assess and delineate the critical mineral resource potential of the Western Phosphate Field in order to provide an enhanced understanding of marine chemocline mineral systems. Potential critical minerals in project area: Cr, F, REEs, and V.

pdescrip: Reconnaissance geochemistry of the Pennsylvanian black shales from the Cherokee-Forest City, Illinois, and Appalachian basins
abstract: Organic-rich Pennsylvanian black shales known to contain intervals enriched in rare earth elements (REE) and other critical minerals, including molybdenum, nickel, platinum group elements, uranium, and vanadium, occur across ~2 million km2 from the U.S. Midcontinent to the Appalachian Basin. Although these black shales are proven sources of critical minerals, they represent an underexplored and underexploited potential resource. The state geological surveys of Illinois, Indiana, Iowa, Kansas, Kentucky, Missouri, and Ohio are collaborating in a reconnaissance geochemical and petrophysical study of Pennsylvanian black shales from the Cherokee-Forest City, Illinois, and Appalachian basins to investigate their critical mineral potential. Critical mineral concentrations in these metalliferous black shales could surpass concentrations in more traditional deposit types. Preliminary data from the Illinois Basin suggest that black shales vary laterally in critical mineral concentrations; therefore, current limited datasets may poorly describe the true critical mineral potential within and across basins. Using major and trace element chemistry, regional stratigraphic mapping, and other geochemical and petrographic analyses, this research aims to investigate controls for critical mineral concentrations related to: (1) ocean chemistry and paleoenvironmental conditions; (2) facies controls; and (3) favorable stratigraphic and depositional conditions and diagenetic processes. Two specific testable hypotheses are: (1) critical mineral abundance varies spatially across the basins and vertically within individual black shale horizons; and (2) chemical composition and mineralogy vary with unit thicknesses and change with diagenetic process as it relates to critical mineral enrichment. The objective is to produce a comprehensive geochemical database from specific focus areas in Pennsylvanian black shales alongside documented thickness variations, which will allow improved resource maps and exploration models for critical minerals across targeted Midcontinent basins. Environmental scanning electron microscopy connected with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy will be used to analyze selected samples. These analyses will enable identification of mineral phases in intervals enriched in critical minerals and will support future investigations into genetic processes that lead to critical mineral enrichments. In addition, this project will lay the foundation for improving regional, inter-basin stratigraphic correlations. The goal is to improve our understanding of critical mineral resources in the extensive sequences of Pennsylvanian black shales in basins of the U.S. Midcontinent. Potential critical minerals in project area: Mo, Ni, PGE, REE, U, and V.

pdescrip: Reconnaissance geochemistry of the West Desert Skarn, Utah
abstract: The Utah Geological Survey is conducting a 400-sample regional geochemical assessment over the West Desert Zn-Cu-In skarn in the Fish Springs mining district in western Juab County, Utah. The Fish Springs district is in the Deep Creek-Tintic mineral belt in the Basin and Range Province in west-central Utah. The West Desert skarn is on the northern end of the Fish Springs Range and is centered on an Eocene monzonite stock that intruded folded and faulted Cambrian and Ordovician basement. It is the only known indium resource in the U.S. Historical mineralization is primarily Pb-Ag vein and replacement deposits in the Silurian Laketown Dolomite, which are anomalous in As, Cd, Mn, Mo, V, and Zn. Bleaching and recrystallization of the carbonate rocks along the northwestern range front in the Fish Springs district and a very strong magnetic high in the pediment led to drill testing and the discovery of a concealed, Eocene-age felsic intrusive complex dated at 38.5±1.0 Ma and the associated West Desert Zn-Cu-In skarn, previously known as the Crypto skarn. The main ore hosts are the Ordovician Ely Springs Dolomite and Pogonip Group, and deeper mineralization is in the Cambrian Orr Formation. Mineralization at West Desert consists of sphalerite with lesser chalcopyrite occurring commonly with massive magnetite in skarn and replacement bodies with widespread marbelization and hornfelsing of country rock around the mineralization. To understand the source and subsequent mobilization and concentration of indium, both the magmatic complex and Paleozoic basement are being comprehensively sampled to delineate the geochemical signature of barren, altered, and mineralized zones (both oxide and sulfide). This sampling will be done on drill core and underground/surface outcrop, providing a 3D geochemical model of enrichment and depletion zones that will also be able to consider structural controls (faults, bedding, etc.). Additionally, because sampling will include mineralized zones, results will also allow assessment of the potential enrichment of other critical minerals, such as germanium and gallium, which are also commonly associated with zinc mineralization. Comprehensive geochronology will be conducted to determine the length of the magmatic event. Further isotopic and/or mineral chemistry will be carried out as needed to fully characterize the source, transport, and concentration of indium. The goals are to 1) identify the source of indium enrichment, 2) delineate deportment of the indium in both the oxide and sulfide zones of the deposit, and 3) evaluate the geochemical dispersion of both indium and the overall deposit signature. Potential critical minerals in project area: Ga, Ge, In, and Zn.

pdescrip: Reconnaissance geochemistry in the Medicine Bows Mountains, Wyoming
abstract: The Wyoming State Geological Survey (WSGS) is conducting an analytically intensive geochemistry and geochronology reconnaissance project in the Medicine Bows Mountains, Wyoming. The Medicine Bows have high potential for critical mineral deposits vital to U.S. growth and stability. The area''s complex geology, including the juxtaposition of the Archean Wyoming province with the accreted Colorado province terranes along the Cheyenne belt and associated shear zones, multiple episodes of mafic and felsic intrusions, thick packages of metasediments and metavolcanics, possible later metamorphic events, and Laramide uplift, suggest that diverse mineral systems exist within the Medicine Bows. Historical mining in the area has confirmed the existence of economically viable gold, silver, copper, PGE, and minor uranium and rare earth elements (REE) deposits. Historical mining efforts also noted the existence of metals now considered critical, the presence of which has been confirmed by subsequent scientific studies. However, geochemical data for the region are not available in a comprehensive public database. Preexisting data are inconsistent in methodology, elements analyzed, spatial coverage, and public availability. This project will develop and conduct an exhaustive sampling program to target areas of known and unknown mineralization in shear-hosted veins; layered mafic-ultramafic intrusions and associated felsic intrusions along the Cheyenne belt; felsic intrusions, pegmatites, and vein systems in the accreted Proterozoic terranes; Precambrian sulfide-rich metasediments and metavolcanics; radioactive paleoplacers; and REE- and uranium-rich pegmatites. The study area encompasses the entire Wyoming portion of the Medicine Bow Mountains--a Precambrian-cored Laramide uplift. This project will directly complement the airborne magnetic and radiometric survey scheduled for summer 2023 in the Medicine Bows and eastern Sierra Madre Mountains. The goal is an integrated understanding of the geochemical, structural, petrological, and deformational processes that make up regional mineral systems; this will have the potential to aid mineral exploration efforts not only in the Medicine Bows, but also in areas with a similar geologic history, such as the Sierra Madre range to the west and elsewhere along the trend of the Cheyenne belt. Potential critical minerals in the project area: Co, Cr, Ni, PGE, REE, and Zn.

pdescrip: Reconnaissance geochemistry in the Colorado Plateau, New Mexico, Colorado, Utah, and Arizona
abstract: The Colorado Plateau is part of the four corners region in New Mexico, Colorado, Utah, and Arizona, western United States and includes 36 Earth MRI focus areas covering 14 mineral systems and 38 deposit types, representing a significant geologic and mineral resource province, hosting numerous critical minerals. The New Mexico Bureau of Geology and Mineral Resources, in collaboration with the Utah Geological Survey and the Colorado Geological Survey, will have a select group of existing National Uranium Resource Evaluation (NURE) sediment pulps reanalyzed using modern geochemical methods. These sediment samples represent the best opportunity for comprehensive regional geochemistry in this area. In addition, stream sediments in select focus areas containing critical minerals will be resampled to add to our knowledge in specific areas. These data will be important for regional mineral resource assessments and general geologic studies. Potential critical minerals in the project area: barite, Co, fluorspar, Ga, Ge, Hf, Mn, Nb, Ni, PGE, REE, Sc, Ti, V, Zn, Zr.

pdescrip: Reconnaissance geochemistry in the Devonian metlliferous black shales of the Illinois Basin in Illinois, Iowa, Indiana, and Kentucky
abstract: The Illinois State Geological Survey, in collaboration with the Iowa, Indiana, and Kentucky state geological surveys, is collecting new geochemical data on critical mineral concentrations of Devonian black shales (Marine chemocline mineral system) across the Illinois Basin and mapping those concentrations by age of the sedimentary rocks. These data will contribute to future evaluation of black shale mineralization models by documenting geochemical gradients, sedimentology, and characteristics of black shale-enclosing strata. The study area covers outcrop and subsurface occurrences in southeastern Iowa, central to southern Illinois, western Indiana, and western Kentucky. Ultimately, this is a geochemical reconnaissance project to document regional critical mineral concentrations in Devonian black shales and to test if those concentrations vary spatially and stratigraphically within individual units in relation to changes in composition, texture, and mineralogy with the potential to differentiate primary depositional features from later diagenetic history. Potential critical minerals in the project area include Cr, Ni, PGE, Re, and V; additional potential minerals include Cu, Au, Mo, and U.

pdescrip: Reconnaissance geochemistry in the granitoids in Virginia North Carolina and South Carolina
abstract: The North Carolina Geological Survey, in collaboration with the South Carolina Geological Survey and the Virginia Department of Energy Geology and Mineral Resources, is conducting a regional study to characterize the potential for regolith related REE deposits in the southeastern US. Enrichment of REE by ion adsorption processes in clay regolith derived from anorogenic (A-type) and metaluminous calc-alkaline (I-type) granitic rocks can result in economic concentrations of REE. This regolith deposit model requires the presence of large granitoid plutons that have undergone long periods of intense chemical weathering in warm humid conditions with minimal erosion and have evidence of REE mobility. Geologic and climatic conditions in the Southeastern US, including the Carolinas and Virginia, fit this model. These states are known to have REE-enriched A-type granites with a long chemical weathering history, which are comparable to conditions for known REE-enriched systems in South China. The main objectives of this study are: (1) identification of target granitoid plutons in each of the three states, using available geochemical, geophysical, and remote sensing data, along with geomorphology, geologic mapping, and estimates of regolith thickness: (2) collection of up to 600 samples of soil, saprolite, and inferred parent rock materials for new geochemical and mineralogical analyses; and (3) compilation of a comprehensive geochemical database that will characterize REE distribution, based on factors such as parent rock, regolith thickness, and mineralogy. This database will help determine geologic factors contributing to REE mobility and help identify which regions warrant further investigation into their potential for REE resources in the Carolinas and Virginia. Potential critical minerals in the project area: REE and scandium.

pdescrip: Geologic mapping and reconnaissance geochemistry of Upper Mississippi Valley (UMV) Zinc and Lead District in Illinois, Iowa, and Wisconsin
abstract: The Illinois State Geological Survey (ISGS), in collaboration with the Wisconsin Geological and Natural History Survey and the Iowa Geological Survey, is undertaking three major tasks to geologically map and characterize the Earth MRI focus area of the Upper Mississippi Valley (UMV) Zinc and Lead District in Illinois, Iowa, and Wisconsin. These tasks are: 1) Conducting mapping of the UMV Zn-Pb District, including information such as historical mines, boreholes, mineralization, structural geology, and newly collected samples with geochemical analysis; 2) Producing new geochemical data, as well as petrographic, mineralogic and thermometric results, driven by the newly created map, to fill data gaps and to refine a sampling strategy for resource assessments of critical minerals in the district; and 3) Constructing a geodatabase with all data and a supplemental report that includes a high-level geologic interpretation to provide context to the data. The goal is to create a significantly improved understanding of the UMV Zn-Pb District and provide crucial information for future critical mineral exploration. Potential critical minerals in the project area: Ba, Ga, Ge, In, and Zn.

pdescrip: Reconnaissance geochemical investigation of Devonian shales across Pennsylvania, West Virginia, and Maryland
abstract: The Pennsylvania Geological Survey, in collaboration with the West Virginia Geological and Economic Survey and the Maryland Geological Survey, is conducting a reconnaissance geochemical investigation of Devonian shales across Pennsylvania, West Virginia, and Maryland to assess their economic potential for vanadium, molybdenum, cobalt, nickel, rhenium, and platinum group metals. The Devonian shales are part of the “Marine chemocline mineral system – Black shale deposit type” of interest to Earth MRI, and examination of the critical mineral enrichment potential of these shales is vital to the nation. Geochemical analysis of the Devonian shales will help determine baseline values and identify elevated zones of critical minerals across cores, in available samples, and in newly collected samples. The concentrations of critical minerals can vary greatly over centimeters; XRD analyses of shale samples will provide semi-quantitative mineralogy data, as the concentrations of some critical elements can be mineralogically controlled. This project aims to determine the location of enrichment zones in the Devonian shale stratigraphy. This information can then be used to determine if enrichment zones are widespread or vary in thickness in a region and to help build 3D models of critical-mineral enrichment. Potential critical minerals in the project area: Co, Mo, Ni, PGM, Re, and V.

pdescrip: 3D Mapping and Geochemical Investigation of the Illinois-Kentucky Fluorspar District and the Midwest Permian Ultramafic District
abstract: The Illinois-Kentucky Fluorspar District (IKFD) and the Midwest Permian Ultramafic District (MPUD) are locations of proven critical mineral resources. Recent Earth MRI-supported geologic mapping and geophysical of the Hicks Dome area provide vital prerequisites, yet these efforts alone are insufficient for researchers to fully understand the complexity of the area and assess it for critical mineral resources. This project, which is being done in concert with the Kentucky Geological Survey, will develop a 3-dimensional model that includes both districts in the area of review that integrates subsurface well, structural, geophysical, geochemical, mineralogical, and historical mine footprint data is necessary for critical mineral resource assessments as well as understanding the regional geology. Known commodities and deposit types are rare earth elements including HREE, cobalt, barite, fluorspar, beryllium, uranium, strontium, gallium, germanium, indium and titanium. Additional prospectivity for non-critical thorium, lead, zinc, silver, cadmium, and copper. This project is being conducted in partnership with the Kentucky Geological Survey.

pdescrip: Western Kentucky Fluorspar District 3-Dimensional Geological Model
abstract: The Illinois-Kentucky Fluorspar District (IKFD) and the Midwest Permian Ultramafic District (MPUD) are locations of proven critical mineral resources. Recent Earth MRI-supported geologic mapping and geophysical of the Hicks Dome area provide vital prerequisites, yet these efforts alone are insufficient for researchers to fully understand the complexity of the area and assess it for critical mineral resources. This project, which is being done in concert with the Kentucky Geological Survey, will develop a 3-dimensional model that includes both districts in the area of review that integrates subsurface well, structural, geophysical, geochemical, mineralogical, and historical mine footprint data is necessary for critical mineral resource assessments as well as understanding the regional geology. Known commodities and deposit types are rare earth elements including HREE, cobalt, barite, fluorspar, beryllium, uranium, strontium, gallium, germanium, indium and titanium. Additional prospectivity for non-critical thorium, lead, zinc, silver, cadmium, and copper. This project is being conducted in partnership with the Illinois State Geological Survey .

pdescrip: Mine wastes characterization in the Steeple Rock district, New Mexico
abstract: There are tens of thousands of inactive or abandoned mine features in 274 mining districts in New Mexico (NM), including coal, uranium, metals, and industrial minerals districts. Many of these mines have related mine waste materials, which could have potential for containing critical minerals, because the actual mineral production was generally for precious and base metals and not critical minerals. Therefore, any critical minerals that occur naturally in the mineral deposits would presumably be present in the mine wastes. The New Mexico Bureau of Geology and Mineral Resources is conducting a project to characterize and estimate the critical mineral endowment of mine wastes in three mining districts in NM: (1) Copper Flat at Hillsboro, (2) Black Hawk in Burro Mountains, and (3) Carlisle-Center mines in Steeple Rock district. The Copper Flat mine is an example of a porphyry copper deposit, among other related deposit types. Known critical minerals in the district include Te, As, Bi, Mg, Mn, and Zn. The Black Hawk district (also known as Bullard's Peak) is an example of the arsenide mineral system and contains five-element veins among other deposit types. Known critical minerals in the district include Co, Ni, As, REE, Bi, W, Te, fluorite, and Zn. The Carlisle-Center mines are examples of a low sulfidation, volcanic-epithermal system and contain Au-Ag veins among other deposit types. Known critical minerals in the district include As, Bi, Te, fluorite, and Zn. Detailed maps will be produced, showing the geology and extent of mine wastes in the project areas within the three mining districts sampled and characterized. Approximately 100 composite mine waste samples (from mine dumps, low grade stockpiles, tailings from all three sites) will be collected for geochemical analysis in order to calculate the total critical mineral potential in these wastes. Samples will be selected for additional mineralogical, microbial, and leaching studies based upon the chemical analyses. Approximately 15 water samples for pH, major cation and anion and trace elements will be obtained from all three sites to determine if there are any critical minerals potential in the mine waters. Potential critical minerals in the project area: As, Bi, Fluorite, Mg, Mn, REE, Te, W, and Zn.

pdescrip: Mine waste from phosphate mining in central and north Florida
abstract: The Florida Geological Survey (FGS) is conducting a study on mine waste associated with phosphate mining in central and north Florida to determine if the mine waste contains critical mineral resources. Phosphate deposits have been shown to host rare earth elements (REEs) and other valuable trace elements in the waste byproduct in concentrations comparable to the host rock. The most significant fraction of the mine waste is in the form of phosphogypsum stored in large stacks known as gypsumstacks or â€œgypstacksâ€, some exceeding 400 feet above the land surface. Other forms of mine waste include tailings, waste rock piles, often used as backfill, and clay settling ponds that contain phosphate slimes and froth tailings. The study is largely focused in the central Florida area where phosphate mining has been active in the Peace River Basin since the late 1800â€™s. The Bone Valley Member of the Peace River Formation, which commonly contains phosphate sand and gravel (>30% by volume), is the unit targeted by phosphate mining. Most of the Bone Valley Member has been mined out and/or reclaimed. The second, smaller area is the north Florida phosphate district in Hamilton County. The surficial geology there consists of formations within the Miocene Hawthorn Group, which contains the phosphate-grain-rich Statenville Formation, overlain in places by undifferentiated Neogene/Quaternary sediments, all stratigraphically above Oligocene carbonates. Collection of mine waste materials samples will include phosphogypsum, sediment tailings, reclamation fill, and other mine waste produced from phosphate plants at selected sites. Geochemical analyses will be conducted on the collected samples as well as powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis to provide mineralogical characterization of the unique types of mineralization occurring in some of the solid waste streams. Additionally, water samples will be collected and analyzed from accessible ponds that are located at multiple sites. These ponds are the remnants of phosphate mining and processing, and the analysis will help establish a water quality baseline in them and may demonstrate the fluid mobility of various components within mine waste across multiple locations. The FGS will use the best publicly available LiDAR data from the Florida Geographic Information Office to produce a high-resolution digital elevation model (DEM) of the proposed study areas, to assist in the identification of geomorphologic and mining activity features within the study area. Potential critical minerals in the project area: REEs.

pdescrip: Mine waste at California Gulch (Leadville Mining District), Bonita Peak Mining District, and the Central City/Idaho Springs sites, Colorado
abstract: The Colorado Geological Survey (CGS), in collaboration with the Colorado School of Mines (CSM) and the Colorado Division of Reclamation, Mining and Safety (DRMS) is conducting a reconnaissance investigation of critical minerals in mine-related waste materials in Colorado, which include waste pile material (tailings and/or waste rock), metallurgical waste, and perpetual mine-related water (e.g., ponds, discharges) from mineral deposits that have the potential to contain critical minerals. Analytical data for many of the critical minerals in these mine-related wastes does not currently exist. The CGS has identified ten perpetual inactive mine discharge sites and six areas with waste piles associated with the critical mineral deposits for this study. This investigation includes two years of monitoring at the ten perpetual mine discharges to determine the concentration of critical minerals in mine effluent. All these discharges are associated with mineral deposits that host critical minerals. The discharges are monitored regularly and often contain the critical minerals zinc, magnesium, and manganese as well as other metals such as cadmium, lead, and copper, but have not previously been monitored for other critical minerals that are associated with these deposit types. The project will collect seasonal (five times per year) water samples and field parameters from the ten perpetual mine discharges over two years. Samples will also be collected of filter cake and/or sludge samples which may contain elevated concentrations of critical minerals from water treatment plants associated with these discharges. Mine waste pile sampling targets those that are accessible, relatively large, and that contain critical minerals are numerous in Colorado; six areas were chosen for this study for sampling and documenting the waste piles. The project will collect composite and other mine waste samples and field data from the six areas. Critical minerals in the waste piles in the six target areas have not been adequately characterized. All these areas are associated with USGS critical mineral focus areas; three Superfund sites were included in this investigation: California Gulch (Leadville Mining District), Bonita Peak Mining District, and the Central City/Idaho Springs sites. Potential critical minerals in the project area: Mg, Mn, REEs, and Zn.

pdescrip: Mine waste characterization of rare earth element-enriched mine waste at the Browns Bottom Quarry in Dubuque, Iowa
abstract: The Iowa Geological Survey (IGS) proposes to characterize the mine waste at the active Browns Bottom Quarry (BBQ) site in Dubuque, Iowa, USA. For more than four decades carbonate bedrock has been mined for crushed stone aggregate at this location, as well as the adjacent Weber Quarry. Similar to most mining operations of this type, the overburden must be stripped off and stockpiled. At this locality, the overburden consists of thin unconsolidated glacial materials overlying Ordovician-aged phosphatic bedrock of the Elgin Member of the lower Maquoketa Formation. In the Midwest, the Maquoketa has been the target of an ongoing geochemical reconnaissance project as part of Phase 3 of the U.S. Geological Survey’s (USGS) Earth Mapping Resources Initiative (Earth MRI) Program. The primary objective of the Phase 3 project is to evaluate the critical mineral enrichment of phosphatic rocks (phosphorites) in the lower Maquoketa Formation. Results from geochemical analyses of samples collected at the BBQ site in 2021 demonstrate that the phosphorites are enriched in heavy rare earth elements (HREEs), with an average concentration of 415 ppm ?REE. When considering the average HREE concentrations of the phosphorites from these initial data and the volume of material that has been stripped, it is estimated that over 1,000 tons of HREEs exist in the waste rock that has been stockpiled at the active BBQ site. The IGS will collect representative samples, from drill holes and test pits, of the stockpiled material for further geochemical analysis so that the REE-enrichment at this locality can be further constrained.

pdescrip: Mine waste characterization of Upper Mississippi Valley Lead-Zinc District from Illinois and Wisconsin
abstract: The Upper Mississippi Valley (UMV) Lead-Zinc District is one of the largest base metal districts in North America and the type locality for Mississippi Valley-Type (MVT) deposits. It covers more than 7,800 km2 in northwest Illinois, northeast Iowa, and southwest Wisconsin. The UMV Lead-Zinc District in this area has produced over 1.5 million short tons of zinc and 500,000 short tons of lead. The ores contain 4% to 8% of Zn, and 0.5% to 1% of Pb. A large volume of mine wastes has rejected from extraction of Pb and Zn ores forming mine, mill tailings, and rock piles across the UMV Lead-Zinc District. Remaining lead and zinc in some of these mine tailings piles gave high concentrations of lead (446 to 1,943 ppm) and zinc (3,502 to 20,088 ppm) but there is no reliable data available on the distribution or the concentrations of critical minerals such as Zn, Ba, U, Cd, Ag, Ga, In, and Ge-bearing minerals within MVT sphalerite ore, and associated barite ore. For this 2-year Earth Mapping Resource Initiative (EMRI) project, the ISGS and WGNHS proposes to characterize UMV mine waste sites that may potentially contain critical mineral resources. The ISGS has prioritized two mine waste sites in Illinois for their volume, geology, and potentially resources: (1) the Bautsch-Gray Mine Superfund Site, and (2) the Little Giant Mine’s mine tailing pile. Mine wastes materials will be collected following the newly created USGS Sampling Protocol for mine waste characterization efforts to standardize the sampling carried out by the state geological surveys. The samples will be analyzed for geochemistry and mineralogy to identify mine waste sites for their extraction of critical minerals in the future.

pdescrip: Mine waste characterization of Southeastern Missouri Iron Metallogenic- Pea Ridge Site
abstract: The Missouri Geological Survey (MGS) plans to conduct Mine Waste Characterization of the Pea Ridge tailings impoundment in the world class Southeast Missouri Iron Metallogenic Province. Field work and sampling is planned for a two-year time frame and will be supported by sampling protocols and training provided by USGS. Samples, both mine waste and ore rock, will be sent to USGS for detailed geochemical analysis. Work will be completed in FY25.

pdescrip: Mine waste characterization of mine waste piles in Montana
abstract: MBMG have identified eight candidate sites for mine waste sampling.  These sites include the following: 1.) Beal Mountain Mine, Silver Bow Co, 2.) South Fork Parker Creek Mine, Silver Bow Co., 3.) Calvert Mine and Mill, Beaverhead Co., 4.) Elkhorn Mine, Beaverhead Co., 5.) Tucker Creek Mine, Silver Bow Co., 6.) Flat Creek/Iron Mountain Mine, Mineral Co., 7.) Charter Oak Mine, Powell Co., and 8.) Shapleigh Mine/Little Emma Mine, Granite Co. Four of the eight mine sites will be visited by MBMG staff to be mapped out in terms of mine waste (i.e., tailings) features. The mine waste will be comprehensively sampled and assayed to estimate the grade and tonnages of any critical minerals (e.g., rare earth elements, germanium, zinc, etc.) present. The mineralogies of the mine waste at these sites will also be examined in order to provide a realistic assessment of the economic values and/or environmental liabilities these collections of material represent. The results of these investigations will be publicly available through a simplified web browser-based map system.

pdescrip: Mine waste characterization of the Tungsten Queen Mine Tailings in North Carolina
abstract: The goal of this study is to characterize tailings from the Tungsten Queen mine, located in the Hamme Tungsten Mining District. The Hamme Tungsten Mining District, an S-R-V tungsten deposit type in the Porphyry Cu-Mo- Au mineral system (Hofstra & Kreiner, 2020), has been delineated as focus area SE032. The Tungsten Queen mine produced over 1 million STU WO3 while in operation from 1943 to 1971 (Foose et al., 1980) and is the only tungsten mine east of the Rocky Mountains (Chaumba et al., 2015). The Tungsten Queen mine contains an estimated two million tons of tailings (Myertons, 1975; Reid et al., 2017), which have been identified as a probable resource for tungsten (Myertons, 1975) and may also contain the critical minerals bismuth, manganese, and scandium. Potential non-critical mineral commodities include copper, fluorspar, silver, and high-grade quartz. In addition to the site’s critical mineral potential, this assessment could also help inform environmental protection efforts for two local reservoirs. The proposed study aims to sample three tailings ponds associated with the Tungsten Queen mine. In addition to the surface composite samples, this study will collect and sample three sonic drill cores.

pdescrip: Mine waste characterization of mine waste sites in Washington
abstract: The Washington Geological Survey will characterize the geochemistry and physical characteristics of 10 known mine waste sites. Characterizing the mine waste across a wide range of geochemical parameters will also help establish if particular sites are best consid¬ered environmental liabilities or economical assets (or both). All of the sites are on either state or federal public land and are in favorable areas on our critical mineral favorability model. The sites were identified using a subset of mine-waste features from the USMIN database (mine dumps, tailings, tailing ponds, open pit mines, and strip mines) and excluding those that are related to coal; those that are in wilderness areas, national parks, and Tribal lands; and those that are neither within a USGS critical mineral focus area nor within 5 km of a critical mineral occurrence. This resulted in 72 possible features from the USMIN database. Using land-ownership data, we then excluded sites on private land due to our inability to gain access permission within the time frame of this proposal process. Sites will be sampled according to USGS protocols and sent to USGS for geochemical analysis.

pdescrip: Mine waste characterization of New York mine waste localities
abstract: The NYSGS in collaboration with NYSDEC propose to complete sample collection for solid rock (tailings) mine waste characterization. In alignment with USGS sampling protocols, this project will collect approximately 150 samples from potential mine waste localities. Mine sites whereupon large tailings piles were established iron mines (particularly in the eastern and northwest Adirondack Mountains, but also in the Hudson Highlands), iron-titanium mines (in the south-central Adirondacks), and lead-zinc operations (in the northwest Adirondacks). These mine wastepiles might contain minerals of economic importance in the tailings. There are sporadic mine waste locations in the lower Hudson Valley in Putnam, Orange, and Westchester Counties that may have very old and possibly accessible mine tailings and are included as potential sampling locations. In the Adirondack region, iron mines can be divided into four categories with varying numbers of mines in each. These are (1) magnetite-rare earth element deposits with low titanium, (2) magnetite deposits with low titanium, (3) magnetite-boron deposits with low titanium, and (4) magnetite-ilmenite deposits (Lupulescu et al., 2015).

pdescrip: Hyperspectral mapping of the Southwestern US
abstract: Visible to shortwave infrared (VSWIR) imaging spectrometer (also known as hyperspectral) data will be acquired over ~500,000 km2 of the Southwestern United States under an interagency agreement (IAA) with NASA, using their AVIRIS (Airborne Visible InfraRed Imaging Spectrometer) sensors. VSWIR data, derived mineral maps and other interpretive products will provide actionable information on the location and formation of critical minerals related to both hydrothermal alteration and rock weathering processes. This will also include potential lithium deposits contained in numerous evaporite deposits in closed basins exposed throughout the Basin and Range. Additional data will be acquired using one of several thermal infrared (TIR) sensors (e.g., NASAâ€™s HyTES and/or MASTER instruments) which will support discrimination, identification, and mapping of primary rock-forming minerals. The latter data will also aid in geologic mapping and mine waste sampling efforts being undertaken by those states. The primary (or highest priority) collection area was designed in order to complete existing large area data collections across the states of California and Nevada, including adjacent portions of Southern Arizona. These areas contain numerous mines and other geologic and land-use features which will benefit from studies utilizing hyperspectral data covering those areas. For example, natural hazard related studies such as volcanoes, landslides, wildfires, floods, earthquakes, snowpack monitoring, dust emissions and wildfire-induced debris flow mapping will also benefit from the acquisition of this data.

pdescrip: Hyperspectral mapping of the Florida Phosoho-Gypsum deposits
abstract: High spatial resolution (< 5 meters/pixel) visible to shortwave infrared (VSWIR) and thermal infrared (TIR) imaging spectrometer (also known as hyperspectral) data will be acquired over two areas actively mined for phosphate-bearing rocks and gypsum. The first of these two areas is ~3,400 square kilometers and east of Tampa; and the other is ~886 square kilometers and north of Lake City in the Florida panhandle. VSWIR data, derived mineral maps and other interpretive products will provide actionable information on the location and formation of critical and industrial minerals which can be used to produce fertilizer, aid in construction (e.g., gypsum is used for drywall) and hurricane resiliency. Critical minerals include those containing rare earth elements which are sometimes found in trace amounts in phosphorite deposits. Two instruments will be flown by commercial vendors, including the VSIWR AISA-FENIX sensor developed by Specim and the TIR MAKO sensor developed by Aerospace Corp. These data will also aid in geologic mapping and mine waste sampling efforts being undertaken by the state Florida as part of its Earth MRI mine waste project.

pdescrip: Hyperspectral mapping of the Florida Paleo-Shoreline Placer deposits
abstract: High spatial resolution (< 5 meters/pixel) visible to shortwave infrared (VSWIR) and thermal infrared (TIR) imaging spectrometer (also known as hyperspectral) data will be acquired over an area of the state actively mined for heavy mineral sands. This area is ~890 square kilometers and west of Jacksonville, Florida. VSWIR data, derived mineral maps and other interpretive products will provide actionable information on the location and formation of critical and industrial minerals which can be used for both aggregates in construction, shoreline protection and hurricane resiliency. Critical minerals mined in these heavy mineral sands include monazite, which is a rare earth elements mineral. Two instruments will be flown by commercial vendors, including the VSIWR AISA-FENIX sensor developed by Specim and the TIR MAKO sensor developed by Aerospace Corp. These data will also aid in geologic mapping and mine waste sampling efforts being undertaken by the state Florida as part of its Earth MRI mine waste project.

pdescrip: Hyperspectral targets in Puerto Rico for geologic mapping, building materials and landslide hazards
abstract: High spatial resolution (< 5 meters/pixel) visible to shortwave infrared (VSWIR) and thermal infrared (TIR) imaging spectrometer (also known as hyperspectral) data will be acquired over three areas in Puerto Rico to assist with future geologic mapping, landslide hazard assessments and inventory of active cement plants and limestone quarries in the southwest portions of the island. The three areas of interest include box outline of data acquired near Mayaguez (coverage = ~74 square miles or ~193 square kilometers), Ponce (coverage = ~151 square miles or ~392 square kilometers) and the west-central town of Jayuya (coverage = ~157 square miles or ~407 square kilometers). VSWIR data, derived mineral maps and other interpretive products will provide actionable information on industrial minerals which can be used to aid in construction and hurricane resiliency. Also, past landslide scarps can be mapped, and their vegetation recovery can be best assessed using hyperspectral imagery. Two instruments will be flown by commercial vendors, including the VSIWR AISA-FENIX sensor developed by Specim and the TIR MAKO sensor developed by Aerospace Corp. These data will also aid in geologic mapping in higher terrain areas which are difficult to reach or inaccessible due to lack of roads and landslide activity. Additional (MAKO only) data was acquired in a fourth area of interest that provides a connecting data bridge of continuous data between the Ponce and Mayaguez boxes. The latter box covers an area ~440 square miles (~1,140 square kilometers), including water and overlapping areas of the adjacent Ponce and Mayaguez boxes.
